AC-1,"GOV-02, GOV-03, IAC-01","Policy and Procedures
a. Develop, document, and disseminate to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles]:
1. [Selection (one or more): organization-level
(a) Addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance
(b) Is consistent with applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines
2. Procedures to facilitate the implementation of the access control policy and the associated access controls
b. Designate an [Assignment: organization-defined official] to manage the development, documentation, and dissemination of the access control policy and procedures
c. Review and update the current access control:
1. Policy [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and following [Assignment: organization-defined events]

2. Procedures [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and following [Assignment: organization-defined events].",Contributes,"The VCF platform establishes a robust, software-defined infrastructure with inherent access control mechanisms, including Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) for managing its components such as SDDC Manager, vCenter Server, and NSX Manager. It integrates with enterprise identity providers, enabling centralized authentication and authorization for platform administrators and users. The platform's comprehensive audit capabilities log administrative actions, providing a verifiable record of access and changes.

These technical capabilities form the critical foundation upon which an organization's access control policy and procedures are built and enforced, directly informing the scope and implementation details required by the policy.

The development, documentation, and dissemination of an organization-level, mission/business process-level, or system-level access control policy and its associated procedures remain an organizational responsibility.

The platform does not define the purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, or coordination among organizational entities for access control. It does not designate an official to manage these activities, nor does it automatically review and update the policy and procedures at defined frequencies or following specific events. These are administrative and governance functions that must be performed by the customer to ensure comprehensive compliance with this control."
AC-2,"IAC-07.2, IAC-15, NET-12, TDA-18","Account Management
a. Define and document the types of accounts allowed and specifically prohibited for use within the system
b. Assign account managers
c. Require [Assignment: organization-defined prerequisites and criteria] for group and role membership
d. Specify:
1. Authorized users of the system
2. Group and role membership
3. Access authorizations (i.e., privileges) and [Assignment: organization-defined attributes (as required)] for each account
e. Require approvals by [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles] for requests to create accounts
f. Create, enable, modify, disable, and remove accounts in accordance with [Assignment: organization-defined policy, procedures, prerequisites, and criteria]
g. Monitor the use of accounts
h. Notify account managers and [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles] within:
1. [Assignment: organization-defined time period] when accounts are no longer required
2. [Assignment: organization-defined time period] when users are terminated or transferred
3. [Assignment: organization-defined time period] when system usage or need-to-know changes for an individual
i. Authorize access to the system based on:
1. A valid access authorization
2. Intended system usage
3. [Assignment: organization-defined attributes (as required)]
j. Review accounts for compliance with account management requirements [Assignment: organization-defined frequency]
k. Establish and implement a process for changing shared or group account authenticators (if deployed) when individuals are removed from the group

l. Align account management processes with personnel termination and transfer processes.",Contributes,"The platform provides robust capabilities for managing user accounts and access authorizations across its components. It integrates seamlessly with enterprise identity providers such as Active Directory and LDAP, enabling centralized authentication and authorization.

Granular Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) is enforced across vCenter Server, NSX, and SDDC Manager, allowing for precise definition of authorized users, group and role memberships, and specific access privileges. The platform offers comprehensive APIs and user interfaces for creating, enabling, modifying, disabling, and removing accounts or their associated access rights, facilitating automated account lifecycle management.

All account-related activities, including login attempts, privilege changes, and configuration modifications, are meticulously logged and auditable, supporting continuous monitoring of account usage and compliance.

While the platform delivers the technical mechanisms for account management, the customer is responsible for defining and documenting the types of accounts allowed and prohibited, establishing prerequisites and criteria for group and role membership, and assigning account managers.

The organization must define the personnel or roles responsible for approving account creation requests, specify the policies and procedures governing account lifecycle actions, and determine the frequency for reviewing accounts. Furthermore, the customer is accountable for establishing the specific time periods for notifications regarding account status changes and for aligning account management processes with broader personnel termination and transfer procedures."
AC-2(1),IAC-15.1,"Account Management | Automated System Account Management

Support the management of system accounts using [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation automates the management of system accounts critical for its operational components. The SDDC Manager orchestrates the creation, modification, and lifecycle of accounts for vCenter Server, ESXi hosts, NSX, and vSAN during initial deployment and subsequent updates. This includes the automated provisioning of internal service accounts and the integration with external identity providers such as Active Directory or LDAP for administrative access to the VCF management domain. The platform's lifecycle management capabilities ensure consistent account states across the infrastructure.

The organization must define the specific automated mechanisms to be employed and ensure their consistent application across the broader IT environment, particularly for system accounts within guest workloads and applications not directly managed by the VCF platform. Policy definition for account lifecycle, access reviews, and integration with enterprise-wide identity and access management solutions remain an organizational responsibility."
AC-2(2),IAC-15.2,"Account Management | Automated Temporary and Emergency Account Management
Automatically [Selection: remove
column2: disable] temporary and emergency accounts after [Assignment: organization-defined time period for each type of account].",Contributes,"The VCF platform provides robust capabilities for automated account lifecycle management through its integrated identity services. This includes the technical mechanisms to automatically disable or remove accounts based on predefined time periods, directly supporting the automated management of temporary and emergency accounts. The platform ensures that once policies are configured, the enforcement of account expiration and de-provisioning occurs without manual intervention.

column1: The customer is responsible for defining the specific criteria that classify accounts as temporary or emergency. Furthermore, the organization must establish the precise time periods after which these accounts are to be automatically removed or disabled, and then configure these policies within the identity management system integrated with VCF."
AC-2(3),IAC-15.3,"Account Management | Disable Accounts
Disable accounts within [Assignment: organization-defined time period] when the accounts:
(a) Have expired
(b) Are no longer associated with a user or individual
(c) Are in violation of organizational policy

(d) Have been inactive for [Assignment: organization-defined time period].",Contributes,"VCF provides robust identity and access management capabilities to enforce account lifecycle policies. The platform can be configured to automatically disable accounts based on defined expiration dates, ensuring that access is revoked once an account's validity period ends. Furthermore, VCF's IAM services include comprehensive inactivity monitoring, allowing for the automatic disabling of accounts that have remained dormant for an organization-defined period, thereby reducing the attack surface from stale credentials. The platform also supports the enforcement of organizational security policies, and can be integrated to trigger account disabling actions upon detection of policy violations or when accounts are no longer associated with an active user or individual.

The customer is responsible for establishing the specific organization-defined time periods for account expiration and inactivity. Defining the comprehensive set of organizational policies that necessitate account disabling, and implementing the processes and integrations required to identify when accounts are no longer associated with a user or individual, remain critical customer obligations. VCF provides the technical framework, but its effective implementation relies on precise customer policy definition and operational procedures."
AC-2(4),IAC-15.4,"Account Management | Automated Audit Actions

Automatically audit account creation, modification, enabling, disabling, and removal actions.",Meets,"The VCF platform inherently provides automated auditing for all account lifecycle actions across its core components.

vCenter Server, NSX-T Manager, SDDC Manager, and Workspace ONE Access (when deployed) automatically log events related to account creation, modification, enabling, disabling, and removal. These comprehensive audit trails capture the necessary details for each action, including timestamps, actors, and affected entities.

The platform's integrated logging capabilities ensure that these critical account management activities are continuously and automatically recorded, providing a robust foundation for security monitoring and compliance verification."
AC-2(5),IAC-24,"Account Management | Inactivity Logout

Require that users log out when [Assignment: organization-defined time period of expected inactivity or description of when to log out].",Contributes,"VCF components, including vCenter Server, NSX-T Manager, and SDDC Manager, provide configurable session timeout mechanisms. These capabilities allow for the automatic termination of user sessions after a specified period of inactivity, ensuring that users are logged out as required. The platform's underlying infrastructure supports the enforcement of these inactivity-based logout policies across its management interfaces.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific time period of expected inactivity after which users must be logged out. This policy decision must be established and then configured within the respective VCF component settings to align with organizational security requirements."
AC-2(7),IAC-08,"Account Management | Privileged User Accounts
(a) Establish and administer privileged user accounts in accordance with [Selection: a role-based access scheme
(b) Monitor privileged role or attribute assignments
(c) Monitor changes to roles or attributes

(d) Revoke access when privileged role or attribute assignments are no longer appropriate.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation natively supports role-based access control (RBAC) for managing access to its management plane components, including vCenter Server, NSX-T Data Center, and SDDC Manager. It integrates with enterprise identity providers such as Active Directory and LDAP to establish and administer privileged user accounts based on defined roles.

The platform records audit trails for all changes to user accounts, role assignments, and modifications to role definitions, providing the necessary evidence for monitoring privileged role assignments and changes.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific role-based or attribute-based access scheme, establishing the policies for monitoring assignments and changes, and determining when privileged role or attribute assignments are no longer appropriate for revocation. While the platform provides the technical mechanisms to revoke access, the decision and associated processes are external to the platform's automated functions."
AC-2(9),IAC-15.5,"Account Management | Restrictions on Use of Shared and Group Accounts

Only permit the use of shared and group accounts that meet [Assignment: organization-defined conditions for establishing shared and group accounts].",Contributes,"VCF provides robust identity and access management capabilities, integrating seamlessly with enterprise identity providers such as Active Directory and LDAP. This integration allows for centralized management of user and group accounts, enabling the enforcement of organizational policies regarding account usage. Role-based access control (RBAC) is a fundamental component across all VCF services, ensuring that permissions are assigned and enforced based on defined roles and group memberships. This framework facilitates the technical restriction of shared and group accounts by leveraging established enterprise identity structures and VCF's granular permission model.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific conditions and policies governing the establishment and use of shared and group accounts. This includes establishing the criteria for their creation, usage, and lifecycle management. VCF provides the technical mechanisms to integrate with and enforce these organizational policies, but it does not define the policies themselves. The customer must ensure that their identity provider configurations and VCF's RBAC assignments are configured to align with their established conditions for shared and group account usage."
AC-2(11),IAC-15.8,"Account Management | Usage Conditions

Enforce [Assignment: organization-defined circumstances and/or usage conditions] for [Assignment: organization-defined system accounts].",Contributes,"VCF provides comprehensive capabilities to enforce usage conditions for system accounts. Through integration with enterprise identity providers such as Active Directory, LDAP, or Workspace ONE Access, VCF leverages advanced authentication policies. These policies can dictate access based on factors like network location, time of day, or require multi-factor authentication for specific system accounts or actions.

Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) is intrinsically enforced across all VCF components, including vCenter Server, NSX, and SDDC Manager, ensuring that authenticated system accounts can only perform actions explicitly permitted by their assigned roles.
Furthermore, VCF components enforce configurable session timeouts, restricting the duration of active sessions for all accounts.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific usage conditions and circumstances that must be enforced, as well as identifying the precise system accounts to which these conditions apply. This includes establishing the policies within the integrated identity provider and configuring the appropriate RBAC roles and assignments within the VCF environment to align with the defined organizational requirements."
AC-2(12),MON-16,"Account Management | Account Monitoring for Atypical Usage
(a) Monitor system accounts for [Assignment: organization-defined atypical usage]

(b) Report atypical usage of system accounts to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust capabilities for monitoring system accounts across its integrated components, including vCenter Server, ESXi hosts, and NSX. The platform generates comprehensive logs and metrics that can be analyzed for deviations from established baselines and configured thresholds. Integrated operational management tools, such as Aria Operations, are designed to detect anomalies and potential security events, enabling the identification of atypical usage patterns within the virtual infrastructure. These tools support the configuration of specific rules and alerts to flag suspicious activities related to system account access and behavior.

While the platform delivers the technical foundation for monitoring and alerting, the customer is responsible for defining what constitutes ""atypical usage"" within their specific operational context. This includes establishing baselines, configuring detection rules, and setting appropriate thresholds within the monitoring tools. Furthermore, the customer must define the specific personnel or roles responsible for receiving and responding to reports of atypical usage, as well as configuring the associated notification and reporting mechanisms. The overarching policy and procedural framework for incident response and account management remain a customer responsibility."
AC-2(13),"HRS-09.2, IAC-15.6","Account Management | Disable Accounts for High-risk Individuals

Disable accounts of individuals within [Assignment: organization-defined time period] of discovery of [Assignment: organization-defined significant risks].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides the foundational identity and access management capabilities necessary to disable user accounts across its integrated components. Through its native integration with enterprise identity providers such as Active Directory or LDAP, VCF ensures that account disablement actions performed within the central identity store propagate effectively, revoking access to vCenter Server, NSX, vSphere, and Tanzu environments. The platform's robust API framework further enables programmatic account management, allowing for automated disablement workflows in response to external triggers.

The customer is responsible for defining what constitutes a ""high-risk individual"" and ""significant risks"" within their operational context, as well as establishing the specific time period for account disablement following discovery. This includes implementing the necessary organizational policies, processes, and monitoring mechanisms to identify such risks and initiate the account disablement procedure. While VCF offers the technical means to enforce the disablement, the overarching risk assessment, discovery, and procedural orchestration remain customer responsibilities."
AC-3,"IAC-20, NET-12, TDA-18","Access Enforcement

Enforce approved authorizations for logical access to information and system resources in accordance with applicable access control policies.",Contributes,"VCF provides robust mechanisms for enforcing logical access to information and system resources across the SDDC. vCenter Server implements comprehensive role-based access control (RBAC), enabling granular definition and enforcement of permissions for users and groups on virtual machines, hosts, networks, and storage. NSX-T Data Center further enforces network-level access through distributed and gateway firewalls, allowing for micro-segmentation and precise control over communication paths between workloads and external networks. These capabilities ensure that only approved authorizations are technically enforced.

The customer is responsible for defining the specific access control policies, establishing the approved authorizations, and configuring the RBAC roles and network firewall rules within the VCF environment to align with their organizational security requirements. This includes ongoing policy management, user assignment, and regular review of access configurations."
AC-3(2),"HRS-12.1, IAC-20.5","Access Enforcement | Dual Authorization

Enforce dual authorization for [Assignment: organization-defined privileged commands and/or other organization-defined actions].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) components, including vCenter Server, NSX-T Manager, and ESXi hosts, implement comprehensive Role-Based Access Control (RBAC). This capability allows for the creation of highly granular custom roles and the assignment of specific permissions to users or groups, ensuring that only authorized personnel can perform defined actions. The platform's RBAC framework inherently supports the principle of separation of duties, which is a foundational element for implementing dual authorization. For instance, distinct roles can be configured where one role possesses the permission to initiate a sensitive operation, while another separate role is required to finalize or execute that operation, thereby necessitating the involvement of two different individuals or accounts to complete a critical task.

While VCF provides the essential access control mechanisms, the platform does not natively include a universal workflow engine that automatically pauses *all* organization-defined privileged commands or actions to await explicit approval from a second party before execution. Customers are responsible for identifying and defining the specific privileged commands and actions that require dual authorization. Furthermore, customers must design and implement the specific RBAC configurations, potentially integrating with external privileged access management (PAM) solutions or change management systems, to enforce the dual authorization workflow for these defined actions. This includes establishing the procedural controls and assigning users to roles in a manner that mandates the involvement of two distinct authorized individuals for sensitive operations."
AC-3(8),IAC-20.6,"Access Enforcement | Revocation of Access Authorizations

Enforce the revocation of access authorizations resulting from changes to the security attributes of subjects and objects based on [Assignment: organization-defined rules governing the timing of revocations of access authorizations].",Contributes,"VCF integrates with enterprise identity management systems, such as Active Directory, LDAP, or Workspace ONE Access, to manage user authentication and authorization. When security attributes of subjects (users) or objects (resources) are modified within the authoritative identity provider, VCF components, including vCenter Server, NSX, and SDDC Manager, automatically reflect these changes. This ensures that access authorizations are dynamically re-evaluated and revoked or adjusted in alignment with the updated security attributes. The platform's role-based access control (RBAC) mechanisms inherently enforce these revocations, ensuring that users no longer possess unauthorized access to virtual infrastructure, network segments, or management plane resources once their underlying identity attributes change.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific rules governing the timing of access authorization revocations. This includes configuring the external identity provider to manage attribute changes and their propagation schedules, as well as ensuring VCF components are correctly integrated and configured to synchronize with these identity sources. Establishing and documenting these organization-defined timing rules and ensuring their consistent application across the identity management ecosystem remains a customer responsibility."
AC-3(9),DCH-03.3,"Access Enforcement | Controlled Release
Release information outside of the system only if:
(a) The receiving [Assignment: organization-defined system or system component] provides [Assignment: organization-defined controls]

(b) [Assignment: organization-defined controls] are used to validate the appropriateness of the information designated for release.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation, through NSX-T, provides robust network segmentation and firewall capabilities that are instrumental in enforcing controlled release policies. These capabilities enable the precise definition and enforcement of egress rules, ensuring that workloads operating within the VCF environment can only communicate with approved external systems or services. This technical control prevents unauthorized information release by restricting network paths for data leaving the system boundary.

The definition of organization-defined systems or system components for receiving information, along with the specific controls those receiving entities must provide, is an organizational responsibility.

Furthermore, the validation of the appropriateness of information designated for release requires the implementation of organization-defined controls, which may encompass data loss prevention (DLP) solutions, application-level validation, or human review processes. VMware Cloud Foundation does not inherently perform content-level validation of data appropriateness."
AC-3(11),CFG-08,"Access Enforcement | Restrict Access to Specific Information Types

Restrict access to data repositories containing [Assignment: organization-defined information types].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides a robust platform for enforcing access restrictions to data repositories.

The platform integrates comprehensive identity and access management capabilities, leveraging role-based access control (RBAC) within vCenter Server, NSX-T, and vSAN. This allows for precise control over who can access management plane components and underlying infrastructure resources.

Network micro-segmentation capabilities within NSX-T enable the isolation of data repositories into specific security zones, ensuring that only authorized network flows can reach these critical assets. Furthermore, vSAN offers encryption at rest, securing the data within the repositories themselves.

While the platform provides the necessary technical controls for access enforcement, the organization is responsible for defining its specific information types and classifying the data within its repositories. This includes establishing policies that dictate which information types require specific access restrictions. The customer must then configure the platform's access control mechanisms and network segmentation rules to align with these defined information types and their corresponding access policies."
AC-3(14),PRI-06,"Access Enforcement | Individual Access

Provide [Assignment: organization-defined mechanisms] to enable individuals to have access to the following elements of their personally identifiable information: [Assignment: organization-defined elements].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides a secure and resilient infrastructure foundation essential for hosting applications and services that manage personally identifiable information (PII).

The platform delivers robust identity and access management for platform administrators and users, ensuring secure operational access to the underlying compute, storage, and networking resources. VCF's inherent security features, including network segmentation, data encryption at rest and in transit, and secure virtualization, establish a trusted environment where PII-handling applications can be deployed with confidence.
Fulfilling the requirement for individuals to access specific elements of their own PII necessitates the deployment of dedicated application-layer mechanisms and services.

The customer is responsible for designing, implementing, and operating these applications, which must define the specific PII elements to be exposed and provide the user-facing interfaces or APIs for individual access. This includes establishing the necessary authentication, authorization, and audit controls within those applications, leveraging the secure infrastructure provided by VCF as their operational base."
AC-4,NET-04,"Information Flow Enforcement

Enforce approved authorizations for controlling the flow of information within the system and between connected systems based on [Assignment: organization-defined information flow control policies].",Contributes,"The VCF platform, leveraging NSX-T, provides robust capabilities for enforcing information flow within the system and between connected systems.

NSX-T's distributed firewall (DFW) and gateway firewall (GFW) enable granular control over network traffic, allowing for the enforcement of approved authorizations. Micro-segmentation capabilities facilitate the creation of security policies that dictate communication paths based on workload identity and attributes, ensuring that information flows only as permitted by defined rules.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific information flow control policies and translating these into the appropriate NSX-T configurations, including firewall rules, security groups, and network segments. The ongoing management and validation of these policies also remain an organizational responsibility."
AC-4(1),NET-04.2,"Information Flow Enforcement | Object Security and Privacy Attributes

Use [Assignment: organization-defined security and privacy attributes] associated with [Assignment: organization-defined information, source, and destination objects] to enforce [Assignment: organization-defined information flow control policies] as a basis for flow control decisions.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation, leveraging NSX-T, provides robust capabilities for enforcing information flow control based on security and privacy attributes associated with source and destination objects. The platform enables the creation of granular, distributed firewall policies that utilize attributes such as VM tags, security groups, application identities, and user identities to dictate communication pathways. This allows for precise micro-segmentation and the enforcement of organization-defined flow control policies across the virtualized infrastructure, ensuring that only authorized objects can communicate based on their assigned attributes.

The definition of specific security and privacy attributes, along with the comprehensive information flow control policies, remains an organizational responsibility. While VMware Cloud Foundation provides the technical mechanisms to enforce policies based on attributes of the communicating entities, it does not natively inspect or enforce policies based on intrinsic security and privacy attributes embedded within the *information content* itself. Organizations requiring enforcement at the data payload level would need to integrate additional data loss prevention (DLP) or content inspection solutions."
AC-4(4),NET-04.3,"Information Flow Enforcement | Flow Control of Encrypted Information
Prevent encrypted information from bypassing [Assignment: organization-defined information flow control mechanisms] by [Selection (one or more): decrypting the information
column2: blocking the flow of the encrypted information
column3: terminating communications sessions attempting to pass encrypted information

column1: [Assignment: organization-defined procedure or method]

].",Contributes,"VCF, leveraging NSX-T, establishes a robust software-defined network foundation capable of enforcing granular information flow control. NSX-T provides distributed firewall capabilities that can block or permit traffic based on source, destination, port, and protocol, effectively managing network flows. This allows for the termination of communication sessions or the blocking of specific encrypted protocols (e.g., HTTPS, SSH) at the network layer, preventing their flow across defined security boundaries.

The platform's native capabilities do not include deep packet inspection or decryption of encrypted information to analyze its content for flow control purposes. To fully address the requirement of preventing encrypted information from bypassing controls by decrypting the information or performing content-aware blocking, the organization must integrate specialized third-party security services, such as next-generation firewalls with SSL/TLS inspection capabilities, via service insertion within the NSX-T framework. Furthermore, the organization is responsible for defining the specific information flow control mechanisms and the procedures or methods for handling encrypted information, including the criteria for decryption, blocking, or session termination."
AC-4(5),NET-04.4,"Information Flow Enforcement | Embedded Data Types

Enforce [Assignment: organization-defined limitations] on embedding data types within other data types.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation establishes a robust and secure infrastructure foundation, leveraging NSX-T Distributed Firewall capabilities to enforce granular network segmentation and control data flow between workloads. This ensures that traffic paths are secured and unauthorized data movement is prevented at the network layer. The platform provides the necessary secure environment for deploying and operating advanced security services.

The enforcement of limitations on embedding data types within other data types requires specialized content inspection and Data Loss Prevention (DLP) solutions. While VCF provides the secure network fabric and compute resources where such solutions can be deployed, the platform itself does not natively perform deep packet inspection or content analysis to identify and enforce policies on embedded data types. Customers must integrate and configure third-party DLP or application-level security gateways to define and enforce organization-specific limitations on data type embedding."
AC-4(6),NET-04.5,"Information Flow Enforcement | Metadata

Enforce information flow control based on [Assignment: organization-defined metadata].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation, leveraging NSX-T Data Center, provides the foundational capabilities to enforce information flow control based on organization-defined metadata.

The platform enables the creation and application of custom tags to virtual machines and other network objects, which serve as the mechanism for metadata classification. Distributed Firewall rules within NSX-T can then be configured to precisely control traffic flows between workloads, allowing for granular segmentation and policy enforcement based on these metadata attributes, such as data sensitivity or compliance requirements.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific metadata schema, including the types of tags and their semantic meaning. Furthermore, the customer must establish the policies and procedures for consistently applying these metadata tags to relevant assets and subsequently designing and implementing the specific information flow control rules within NSX-T that leverage this defined metadata to meet their security objectives."
AC-4(8),NET-04.7,"Information Flow Enforcement | Security and Privacy Policy Filters

(a) Enforce information flow control using [Assignment: organization-defined security or privacy policy filters] as a basis for flow control decisions for [Assignment: organization-defined information flows]
(b) [Selection (one or more): Block
column4: Quarantine] data after a filter processing failure in accordance with [Assignment: organization-defined security or privacy policy].",Contributes,"The VCF platform, leveraging NSX-T, provides robust capabilities for enforcing information flow control. Distributed and gateway firewalls enable the creation of granular security policy filters based on various attributes, including virtual machine tags, IP addresses, ports, and protocols. These filters serve as the basis for making flow control decisions, allowing organizations to precisely define and enforce permissible information flows across the software-defined data center.

column1: The organization is responsible for defining the specific security and privacy policy filters and identifying the information flows requiring enforcement. While the platform can block data flows in accordance with defined policies, the specific actions of stripping, modifying, or quarantining data after a filter processing failure require organizational policy definition and may necessitate integration with specialized security services beyond the core network virtualization capabilities."
AC-4(9),NET-04.6,"Information Flow Enforcement | Human Reviews

Enforce the use of human reviews for [Assignment: organization-defined information flows] under the following conditions: [Assignment: organization-defined conditions].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides the underlying infrastructure for establishing and managing information flows through its integrated networking capabilities, specifically NSX-T. This enables the definition of network segmentation policies that control communication paths between workloads and services, thereby governing the technical flow of information. The platform's logging and monitoring components can capture and present data related to these flows, offering visibility into network activity and potential deviations.

The enforcement of human reviews for organization-defined information flows under specified conditions is a customer responsibility. This requires the customer to establish explicit organizational policies and procedures that define the specific information flows requiring human review, the conditions triggering such reviews, and the roles accountable for their execution.

VMware Cloud Foundation does not inherently provide mechanisms to mandate, track, or verify the completion of human reviews, nor does it define the criteria or scope for these reviews. Integration with external workflow management systems or governance, risk, and compliance (GRC) platforms is necessary to orchestrate and document the human review process effectively."
AC-4(12),NET-04.8,"Information Flow Enforcement | Data Type Identifiers

When transferring information between different security domains, use [Assignment: organization-defined data type identifiers] to validate data essential for information flow decisions.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation establishes distinct security domains through NSX-T network virtualization, enabling granular segmentation and isolation of workloads. The NSX Distributed Firewall and Gateway Firewall enforce information flow policies between these domains based on source/destination IP addresses, ports, and identified application protocols. This capability ensures that traffic traversing security boundaries adheres to defined network access controls, facilitating the foundational enforcement of information transfer decisions.

However, the platform does not natively provide mechanisms to define or utilize arbitrary organization-defined data type identifiers for deep content validation essential for information flow decisions. While NSX-T can identify and control traffic based on application signatures, it does not perform payload inspection to validate the specific data types contained within the information being transferred. Organizations are responsible for defining these specific data type identifiers and implementing external Data Loss Prevention (DLP) solutions or advanced content inspection services, which can integrate with the network infrastructure, to perform the required data validation prior to or during information transfer between security domains."
AC-4(13),NET-04.9,"Information Flow Enforcement | Decomposition into Policy-relevant Subcomponents

When transferring information between different security domains, decompose information into [Assignment: organization-defined policy-relevant subcomponents] for submission to policy enforcement mechanisms.",Contributes,"VCF establishes robust security domains and enforces information flow policies through advanced network virtualization capabilities. Distributed firewalls and gateway firewalls within NSX-T provide granular control over traffic traversing these domains, enabling policy enforcement based on network attributes, application identity, and service context. This foundational infrastructure ensures that information transfers occur within a controlled and segmented environment, providing the necessary enforcement points for security policies.

The decomposition of information into organization-defined policy-relevant subcomponents, as well as the specific mechanisms for submitting these subcomponents to policy enforcement, remains a customer responsibility. VCF does not natively perform application-layer content decomposition or deep inspection of arbitrary data structures to extract policy-relevant subcomponents. Customers must define these subcomponents and implement or integrate application-level gateways, data loss prevention (DLP) solutions, or custom application logic to perform the required decomposition and integrate with VCF's network enforcement points."
AC-4(15),NET-04.10,"Information Flow Enforcement | Detection of Unsanctioned Information

When transferring information between different security domains, examine the information for the presence of [Assignment: organization-defined unsanctioned information] and prohibit the transfer of such information in accordance with the [Assignment: organization-defined security or privacy policy].",Contributes,"VCF establishes a robust, segmented network architecture through NSX, enabling the creation of distinct security domains and the enforcement of strict information flow policies between them. The platform provides the necessary infrastructure to define network boundaries, control communication paths, and implement firewall rules that govern data transfer between these domains. This foundational capability ensures that all information transfers occur within a controlled and auditable environment, facilitating the deployment of enforcement mechanisms.

The organization is responsible for defining what constitutes unsanctioned information and establishing the specific security or privacy policies that prohibit its transfer. While VCF provides the network framework, the actual examination of information content for unsanctioned data and the enforcement of content-aware prohibitions require the integration of third-party data loss prevention (DLP) solutions or other content inspection technologies. These external solutions would leverage VCF's network segmentation and traffic steering capabilities to intercept and analyze data flows at domain boundaries."
AC-4(17),NET-04.12,"Information Flow Enforcement | Domain Authentication
Uniquely identify and authenticate source and destination points by [Selection (one or more): organization
column5: individual] for information transfer.",Contributes,"VCF provides robust capabilities to uniquely identify and authenticate source and destination points for information transfer across various entities. Through NSX-T, the platform enables granular identification of systems, applications, and services based on attributes like IP addresses, VM tags, and security groups, facilitating policy enforcement for network flows. Identity and access management components integrate with enterprise directories to authenticate individuals and services, ensuring verified access to VCF-managed resources. This includes support for various authentication protocols and mechanisms to establish trusted identities for both ends of an information transfer.

column1: While the platform delivers the technical framework, the customer is responsible for defining the specific authentication policies for different information transfer scenarios, configuring and integrating external identity providers, and establishing the organizational context for identification and authentication. The customer must also ensure that all relevant sources and destinations, including those at the organizational level, are appropriately identified and authenticated according to their security policies."
AC-4(19),NET-04.13,"Information Flow Enforcement | Validation of Metadata

When transferring information between different security domains, implement [Assignment: organization-defined security or privacy policy filters] on metadata.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation, leveraging NSX, establishes robust network segmentation capabilities, enabling the creation and enforcement of distinct security domains. The platform's distributed and gateway firewalls enforce granular information flow policies between these domains. This includes the ability to apply filters based on various contextual metadata attributes such as virtual machine tags, security groups, application identity, and user identity, directly addressing the requirement to filter metadata during information transfer.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific security and privacy policy filters that must be applied to metadata. This includes identifying the relevant metadata within their environment and translating these requirements into NSX policy configurations. While VCF provides the technical mechanisms for filtering based on network-level and contextual metadata, the precise definition and implementation of these organization-defined filters remain a customer responsibility."
AC-4(20),NET-04.11,"Information Flow Enforcement | Approved Solutions

Employ [Assignment: organization-defined solutions in approved configurations] to control the flow of [Assignment: organization-defined information] across security domains.",Contributes,"VCF, through its integrated NSX-T capabilities, provides robust mechanisms for enforcing information flow across distinct security domains. The platform's distributed firewall (DFW) and gateway firewall functionalities enable granular control over both east-west and north-south traffic. Security groups facilitate the logical grouping of workloads and the definition of security domains, allowing for the application of precise flow control policies. These capabilities ensure that traffic can be restricted or permitted based on organizational requirements, utilizing approved configurations inherent in the VCF validated design.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific information types that require flow control and establishing the corresponding policies and rule sets within NSX-T. This includes delineating security domains, identifying the specific solutions and configurations to be approved, and continuously managing the enforcement rules to align with organizational security objectives."
AC-4(21),NET-06,"Information Flow Enforcement | Physical or Logical Separation of Information Flows

Separate information flows logically or physically using [Assignment: organization-defined mechanisms and/or techniques] to accomplish [Assignment: organization-defined required separations by types of information].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust capabilities for enforcing logical separation of information flows through its integrated NSX networking and security components. NSX enables granular micro-segmentation, allowing for the creation of isolated logical networks and the application of distributed firewall rules directly at the virtual machine network interface card (vNIC) level. This ensures that traffic flows are strictly controlled and separated based on defined security groups, network segments, and application tiers, preventing unauthorized communication between different types of information or workloads.

While VCF delivers the foundational mechanisms and techniques for logical separation, the organization is responsible for defining the specific types of information requiring separation, establishing the precise security policies, and configuring the corresponding NSX distributed firewall rules and logical network constructs to meet those defined requirements. This includes identifying the necessary separation boundaries and ensuring their consistent enforcement across the virtualized environment."
AC-4(25),PRI-05,"Information Flow Enforcement | Data Sanitization

When transferring information between different security domains, sanitize data to minimize [Selection (one or more): delivery of malicious content, command and control of malicious code, malicious code augmentation, and steganography encoded data
column2: spillage of sensitive information] in accordance with [Assignment: organization-defined policy]].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation establishes a robust foundation for enforcing information flow between distinct security domains. NSX-T, an integral component, provides advanced network segmentation capabilities, enabling the creation of isolated security zones and the precise control of traffic between them. This infrastructure facilitates the implementation of policies that govern data transfer, ensuring that only authorized communication paths are utilized and providing the necessary isolation for subsequent sanitization processes.

column1: While the platform provides the necessary network controls for secure information flow, the direct sanitization of data content to mitigate malicious code delivery, command and control, augmentation, steganography, or sensitive information spillage is an application-level function. Organizations are responsible for deploying and configuring specific data sanitization solutions, such as Data Loss Prevention (DLP) systems, content inspection gateways, or secure file transfer mechanisms, within or integrated with the VCF environment. Furthermore, the organization must define and implement the overarching policy governing data sanitization practices as required by the control."
AC-5,"CHG-04.3, HRS-11, NET-12, TDA-18","Separation of Duties
a. Identify and document [Assignment: organization-defined duties of individuals requiring separation]

b. Define system access authorizations to support separation of duties.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) mechanisms across its management plane, including vCenter Server, NSX Manager, and SDDC Manager. This capability allows for the definition of granular permissions and the creation of custom roles, ensuring that administrative privileges are segregated according to the principle of least privilege. Integration with enterprise identity providers, such as Active Directory or Workspace ONE Access, centralizes user authentication and group management, enabling the mapping of organizational roles to specific system access authorizations within the VCF environment. All administrative actions are logged, providing comprehensive audit trails essential for verifying adherence to defined separation of duties.

The customer is responsible for identifying and documenting the specific organizational duties that require separation, including the associated risks. This involves defining the policies and processes that dictate which roles cannot be combined. Subsequently, the customer must translate these organizational requirements into the specific VCF roles and permissions, assign users to these roles, and ensure that no single individual is granted conflicting access authorizations that violate the established separation of duties policy. Regular review and auditing of these assignments are also customer responsibilities."
AC-6,"IAC-20, IAC-21","Least Privilege

Employ the principle of least privilege, allowing only authorized accesses for users (or processes acting on behalf of users) that are necessary to accomplish assigned organizational tasks.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust, native capabilities to enforce the principle of least privilege across the entire software-defined data center.

Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) is deeply integrated into vCenter Server, NSX, vSAN, and SDDC Manager, enabling administrators to define and assign granular permissions to users and groups based on their specific roles and responsibilities. This allows for precise control over management plane access to virtual machines, hosts, networks, storage, and the SDDC infrastructure itself.

Furthermore, NSX micro-segmentation capabilities enable the enforcement of least privilege at the network layer, restricting communication between workloads to only the necessary ports and protocols, thereby minimizing the attack surface and ensuring processes only communicate as required.

The customer is responsible for defining the specific organizational tasks for users and processes, translating these requirements into appropriate roles and access policies within the VCF environment.

This includes establishing and maintaining the access control matrix, regularly reviewing user and process permissions, and implementing robust processes for access request, approval, and revocation to ensure that only authorized and necessary accesses are granted and maintained over time."
AC-6(1),IAC-21.1,"Least Privilege | Authorize Access to Security Functions
Authorize access for [Assignment: organization-defined individuals or roles] to:
(a) [Assignment: organization-defined security functions (deployed in hardware, software, and firmware)]

(b) [Assignment: organization-defined security-relevant information].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) mechanisms that enable granular authorization for individuals and roles across the entire SDDC stack. This capability extends to controlling access to critical security functions inherent in vCenter Server, NSX, vSAN, and SDDC Manager, encompassing operations such as firewall rule management, network segmentation configuration, user and identity management, and lifecycle management of underlying hardware, software, and firmware components. The platform's access controls also govern visibility and modification rights for security-relevant information, including audit logs, security configurations, and system-level settings.

The organization is responsible for defining its specific security functions and security-relevant information, as well as identifying the individuals and roles authorized to access them. While the platform provides the technical enforcement mechanisms, the customer must establish and implement the precise access policies, create the necessary roles, assign users, and configure permissions in alignment with their least privilege requirements and organizational security policies."
AC-6(2),IAC-21.2,"Least Privilege | Non-privileged Access for Nonsecurity Functions

Require that users of system accounts (or roles) with access to [Assignment: organization-defined security functions or security-relevant information] use non-privileged accounts or roles, when accessing nonsecurity functions.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust identity and access management capabilities through its integration with enterprise identity providers and granular Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) within vCenter Server and NSX-T. This architecture enables the creation and management of distinct user accounts and roles, allowing for precise separation of privileges. Organizations can define highly privileged roles specifically for security functions, such as managing firewalls, security policies, or user access controls, and simultaneously establish less privileged roles for non-security operational tasks like virtual machine provisioning, monitoring, or routine network configuration. The platform inherently supports the assignment of different accounts or roles to individual users, facilitating the technical mechanism for users to switch between privileged and non-privileged contexts based on the function being performed.

The organization is responsible for defining what constitutes ""security functions"" and ""security-relevant information"" within their environment. Furthermore, the organization must establish and enforce policies that mandate the use of non-privileged accounts or roles when accessing non-security functions, even for individuals who possess elevated security privileges. This includes implementing processes for provisioning and managing these distinct accounts, providing user training on appropriate account usage, and conducting regular audits to ensure compliance with the established least privilege policies."
AC-6(3),IAC-21.6,"Least Privilege | Network Access to Privileged Commands

Authorize network access to [Assignment: organization-defined privileged commands] only for [Assignment: organization-defined compelling operational needs] and document the rationale for such access in the security plan for the system.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation, through NSX-T Data Center, provides robust capabilities for enforcing network access controls to management plane components and services, which expose privileged commands.

The platform's distributed firewall and micro-segmentation features enable granular policy definition, restricting network access to specific management interfaces, APIs, or CLI endpoints based on source, destination, port, and even user identity. This allows for the precise authorization of network traffic to critical system functions, ensuring that only approved entities can attempt to execute privileged commands over the network.

The organization is responsible for defining what constitutes ""privileged commands"" within their environment and establishing the ""compelling operational needs"" that justify network access to these commands. Furthermore, the organization must develop and maintain the security plan documentation that articulates the rationale for such access, as the platform does not automate these policy definition or documentation processes."
AC-6(5),IAC-21.3,"Least Privilege | Privileged Accounts

Restrict privileged accounts on the system to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles].",Contributes,"The platform provides comprehensive identity and access management capabilities, enabling the creation of custom roles with granular permissions and the integration with enterprise identity providers such as Active Directory or LDAP. This robust Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) framework allows for the precise definition and enforcement of privileged access, ensuring that administrative functions and system-level operations are restricted to specific, authorized accounts or groups. The platform facilitates the assignment of these roles to users, thereby establishing technical controls to limit privileged access.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific personnel or roles authorized to hold privileged accounts within their environment. This includes establishing the policies, procedures, and criteria for granting, reviewing, and revoking such access.

The customer must configure the platform's identity and access management system to align with these organizational definitions and regularly audit privileged account assignments to ensure ongoing compliance with their established policies."
AC-6(6),IAC-05.2,"Least Privilege | Privileged Access by Non-organizational Users

Prohibit privileged access to the system by non-organizational users.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation enforces robust identity and access management through its integration with enterprise identity providers such as Active Directory or LDAP. Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) is a foundational element across all VCF components, including SDDC Manager, vCenter Server, NSX Manager, and vSAN. This architecture enables the granular definition and assignment of permissions, ensuring that only explicitly authorized identities can perform privileged operations within the SDDC. The platform supports the creation of custom roles and the precise assignment of privileges, providing the technical mechanisms to implement least privilege.

The customer is responsible for defining what constitutes a ""non-organizational user"" within their identity management framework and ensuring that such users or groups are not granted privileged access to the VCF environment. This includes configuring VCF to integrate with appropriate internal identity sources and meticulously managing role assignments to prevent any non-organizational entities from acquiring elevated permissions. Regular audits of user accounts and their assigned roles are critical to maintain compliance with this prohibition."
AC-6(7),IAC-17,"Least Privilege | Review of User Privileges
(a) Review [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] the privileges assigned to [Assignment: organization-defined roles or classes of users] to validate the need for such privileges

(b) Reassign or remove privileges, if necessary, to correctly reflect organizational mission and business needs.",Contributes,"VCF establishes a robust framework for privilege management through its integrated components. Granular Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) is enforced across the entire SDDC, allowing for precise definition and assignment of permissions to users and groups. All privilege assignments, modifications, and access attempts are meticulously logged, providing a comprehensive audit trail essential for review. Centralized management interfaces within vCenter Server and SDDC Manager streamline the administration of user accounts, groups, and role assignments, while seamless integration with enterprise identity providers ensures consistent authentication and authorization across the environment.

While the platform provides the necessary technical mechanisms to manage and track privileges, the organization remains responsible for defining the specific frequency and scope of privilege reviews. The critical task of validating the ongoing necessity of assigned privileges, based on organizational mission and business needs, is a policy-driven process requiring human judgment and operational procedures. The subsequent decision to reassign or remove privileges, although technically executed within the platform, is also an organizational responsibility derived from this validation process."
AC-6(8),IAC-21.7,"Least Privilege | Privilege Levels for Code Execution

Prevent the following software from executing at higher privilege levels than users executing the software: [Assignment: organization-defined software].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation establishes a secure execution environment, ensuring its own management plane components and services operate with the principle of least privilege.

The platform provides the foundational infrastructure necessary for deploying workloads where privilege levels can be managed and enforced through underlying operating system and application controls.

The specific identification of organization-defined software and the implementation of mechanisms to prevent its execution at higher privilege levels than the executing user within guest operating systems or customer applications remain the responsibility of the customer.

This requires customer-defined policies, configuration of guest operating system security features (e.g., User Account Control, sudoers, application whitelisting), and application-level privilege management to enforce the least privilege principle for their deployed software."
AC-6(9),IAC-21.4,"Least Privilege | Log Use of Privileged Functions
Log the execution of privileged functions.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation components inherently log the execution of privileged functions across the SDDC stack.

vCenter Server records all administrative tasks, events, and alarms, detailing user actions, object modifications, and system-level operations. ESXi hosts generate logs for root-level access and configuration changes. NSX-T Manager logs API calls, network configuration changes, and administrative access. SDDC Manager captures all lifecycle management operations, including upgrades, patching, and host commissioning/decommissioning.

These logs provide a comprehensive audit trail of privileged activity within the environment. The platform supports forwarding these audit logs to external syslog servers or centralized logging solutions, enabling aggregation and analysis of privileged function usage.

Customers are responsible for defining their specific privileged functions, configuring log forwarding to an appropriate SIEM or log management solution, and establishing processes for regular review and analysis of these logs to detect and respond to unauthorized or anomalous privileged activity."
AC-6(10),IAC-21.5,"Least Privilege | Prohibit Non-privileged Users from Executing Privileged Functions

Prevent non-privileged users from executing privileged functions.",Contributes,"The VMware Cloud Foundation platform inherently supports the principle of least privilege through robust Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) mechanisms across its core components.

vCenter Server, NSX-T Data Center, and SDDC Manager all implement granular permissions that allow administrators to define specific roles and assign them to users or groups, thereby restricting access to functions and objects based on assigned privileges. This ensures that users can only perform actions for which they have explicit authorization, preventing non-privileged users from executing privileged functions within the SDDC infrastructure.
Integration with enterprise identity providers further centralizes user management and authentication, reinforcing the enforcement of defined access policies.

While the platform provides the comprehensive technical framework for enforcing least privilege, the customer is responsible for defining the specific roles, assigning users and groups to those roles, and establishing the policies that govern what constitutes a ""privileged function"" within their operational context.
The ongoing management, review, and auditing of these access assignments and policies remain a customer responsibility to ensure continuous compliance with least privilege principles."
AC-7,IAC-22,"Unsuccessful Logon Attempts
a. Enforce a limit of [Assignment: organization-defined number] consecutive invalid logon attempts by a user during a [Assignment: organization-defined time period]
b. Automatically [Selection (one or more): lock the account or node for an [Assignment: organization-defined time period]

column2: lock the account or node until released by an administrator
column3: delay next logon prompt per [Assignment: organization-defined delay algorithm]

column1: ] when the maximum number of unsuccessful attempts is exceeded.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) components, including vCenter Server and ESXi hosts, provide robust capabilities to enforce limits on unsuccessful logon attempts. These platforms allow for the configuration of a maximum number of invalid logon attempts within a specified time period. Upon exceeding this threshold, the system automatically takes predefined actions. Specifically, vCenter Server and ESXi hosts can be configured to lock user accounts for a defined duration or until an administrator manually releases them. The platform integrates with enterprise identity providers, leveraging their lockout policies where applicable, and can generate alerts and notifications to system administrators through mechanisms such as vCenter alarms, syslog, and SNMP traps when lockout events occur.

The organization remains responsible for defining the specific number of consecutive invalid logon attempts permitted, the associated time period, and the precise automated action to be taken. This includes determining the duration of account lockouts, whether manual administrator intervention is required for release, and the specific notification channels and recipients for lockout events. The implementation of these organizational policies requires direct configuration within the VCF environment."
AC-7(2),MDM-05,"Unsuccessful Logon Attempts | Purge or Wipe Mobile Device

Purge or wipe information from [Assignment: organization-defined mobile devices] based on [Assignment: organization-defined purging or wiping requirements and techniques] after [Assignment: organization-defined number] consecutive, unsuccessful device logon attempts.",Contributes,"The platform provides the foundational infrastructure to host and integrate unified endpoint management solutions capable of enforcing mobile device security policies.

These solutions can be configured to automatically purge or wipe information from designated mobile devices after a specified number of consecutive, unsuccessful logon attempts. This capability ensures data protection on devices that may be lost or compromised by preventing unauthorized access through brute-force attempts.

Customers are responsible for defining the specific mobile devices subject to this control, establishing the precise purging or wiping requirements and techniques, and determining the exact number of consecutive unsuccessful logon attempts that will trigger the automated data purge.

The implementation and configuration of the unified endpoint management solution, including the integration with the mobile device fleet, remain a customer responsibility to align with their organizational security policies."
AC-8,SEA-18,"System Use Notification

a. Display [Assignment: organization-defined system use notification message or banner] to users before granting access to the system that provides privacy and security notices consistent with applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines and state that:
1. Users are accessing a U.S. Government system
2. System usage may be monitored, recorded, and subject to audit
3. Unauthorized use of the system is prohibited and subject to criminal and civil penalties
4. Use of the system indicates consent to monitoring and recording
b. Retain the notification message or banner on the screen until users acknowledge the usage conditions and take explicit actions to log on to or further access the system
c. For publicly accessible systems:
1. Display system use information [Assignment: organization-defined conditions], before granting further access to the publicly accessible system
2. Display references, if any, to monitoring, recording, or auditing that are consistent with privacy accommodations for such systems that generally prohibit those activities

3. Include a description of the authorized uses of the system.",Contributes,"The platform provides the foundational infrastructure and operating environment for systems where user access is managed. It establishes the underlying technical context within which system use notifications are presented to users.
The platform does not inherently generate, manage, or enforce the specific content of system use notification messages or banners.

Customers are solely responsible for defining the precise text of the notification, ensuring it includes all required legal statements regarding U.S. Government system access, monitoring, prohibition of unauthorized use, and consent to monitoring.

Furthermore, customers must configure the operating systems, applications, or access mechanisms hosted on the platform to display this message prior to granting access, ensure it remains on screen until explicit user acknowledgment, and implement the technical mechanism for users to accept the conditions before proceeding.

Specific considerations for publicly accessible systems, including the display of use information, privacy-consistent monitoring references, and authorized use descriptions, also fall entirely within customer implementation scope."
AC-9,SEA-19,"Previous Logon Notification

Notify the user, upon successful logon to the system, of the date and time of the last logon.",Meets,"The VCF platform, through its core components such as vCenter Server, ESXi hosts, and SDDC Manager, inherently provides the capability to notify users of their last successful logon date and time.

Upon successful authentication to the management interfaces or SSH sessions, the system displays this critical security information directly to the user. This feature is a standard security control implemented across the platform's underlying operating systems and management plane, ensuring users are immediately aware of their previous access activity.
This capability is fully integrated into the platform's design, requiring no additional configuration or customer-defined policies to enable the display of last logon notifications."
AC-10,IAC-23,"Concurrent Session Control

Limit the number of concurrent sessions for each [Assignment: organization-defined account and/or account type] to [Assignment: organization-defined number].",Contributes,"VCF provides the foundational infrastructure for secure access management. Its core management components, including vCenter Server and SDDC Manager, incorporate session management features that enforce configurable session timeouts, ensuring inactive sessions are terminated promptly. The platform's integration with enterprise identity providers, such as Active Directory and LDAP, enables centralized authentication and provides a mechanism through which session policies, including potential concurrent session limitations, can be managed at the identity provider level for access to VCF management interfaces.

The explicit definition of specific account types and the precise number of concurrent sessions permitted for each are organizational assignments. The enforcement of these granular concurrent session limits for all defined account types, particularly for applications and services deployed atop the VCF infrastructure, necessitates configuration within the integrated identity provider or directly within the application itself. VCF establishes the secure environment but relies on external identity management or application-level controls to fully implement these specific concurrent session restrictions."
AC-11,IAC-24,"Device Lock
a. Prevent further access to the system by [Selection (one or more): initiating a device lock after [Assignment: organization-defined time period] of inactivity
column2: requiring the user to initiate a device lock before leaving the system unattended]

column1: b. Retain the device lock until the user reestablishes access using established identification and authentication procedures.",Contributes,"VCF management components, including SDDC Manager, vCenter Server, and NSX Manager, are engineered to enforce robust session management policies. These platforms can be configured to automatically terminate user sessions and initiate a device lock after a specified period of inactivity, effectively preventing unauthorized access to the management plane. Upon session termination, re-establishing access necessitates successful re-authentication using integrated identity providers, such as Active Directory, LDAP, or Workspace ONE Access, ensuring adherence to established identification and authentication procedures.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific inactivity timeout period that triggers the device lock. Furthermore, the organization must establish and maintain the identification and authentication procedures used to re-establish access, including the configuration of identity sources and multi-factor authentication where applicable.

While VCF provides the underlying technical capability for session management and re-authentication, the policy decision for user-initiated locks or the specific inactivity duration rests with the customer."
AC-11(1),IAC-24.1,"Device Lock | Pattern-hiding Displays
Conceal, via the device lock, information previously visible on the display with a publicly viewable image.",Not Applicable,"End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope."
AC-12,IAC-25,"Session Termination

Automatically terminate a user session after [Assignment: organization-defined conditions, or trigger events requiring session disconnect].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms for session management across its core components, including vCenter Server, ESXi hosts, NSX, and SDDC Manager. These platforms are engineered to enforce session termination based on configurable parameters. For instance, vCenter Server allows administrators to define idle timeout periods for both the vSphere Client and API sessions, automatically disconnecting users after a specified period of inactivity. Similarly, underlying ESXi hosts and network components like NSX-T Data Center offer controls to manage and terminate active sessions, ensuring that access is not perpetually maintained.

While the platform delivers the technical capability to implement session termination, the organization retains the responsibility for defining the specific conditions and trigger events that necessitate session disconnect. This includes establishing policy-driven idle timeout durations, identifying specific events that mandate immediate session termination, and configuring these parameters consistently across the VCF environment to align with organizational security policies and compliance requirements."
AC-12(1),IAC-25.1,"Session Termination | User-initiated Logouts

Provide a logout capability for user-initiated communications sessions whenever authentication is used to gain access to [Assignment: organization-defined information resources].",Meets,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) management components, including SDDC Manager, vCenter Server, and NSX-T Manager, inherently provide user-initiated logout capabilities for all authenticated communication sessions. Access to these critical information resources is secured with robust authentication mechanisms, and each interface includes a clearly accessible function for users to terminate their active sessions at will.
This ensures that users can proactively end their access to the VCF management plane whenever necessary."
AC-14,IAC-26,"Permitted Actions Without Identification or Authentication
a. Identify [Assignment: organization-defined user actions] that can be performed on the system without identification or authentication consistent with organizational mission and business functions

b. Document and provide supporting rationale in the security plan for the system, user actions not requiring identification or authentication.",Not Applicable,"End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope."
AC-16,DCH-05,"Security and Privacy Attributes

a. Provide the means to associate [Assignment: organization-defined types of security and privacy attributes] with [Assignment: organization-defined security and privacy attribute values] for information in storage, in process, and/or in transmission
b. Ensure that the attribute associations are made and retained with the information

c. Establish the following permitted security and privacy attributes from the attributes defined in AC-16a for [Assignment: organization-defined systems]: [Assignment: organization-defined security and privacy attributes]
d. Determine the following permitted attribute values or ranges for each of the established attributes: [Assignment: organization-defined attribute values or ranges for established attributes]
e. Audit changes to attributes

f. Review [Assignment: organization-defined security and privacy attributes] for applicability [Assignment: organization-defined frequency].",Contributes,"VCF provides a secure and resilient infrastructure platform capable of hosting solutions that manage security and privacy attributes for information.
