The platform's comprehensive logging and monitoring capabilities, delivered through vRealize Log Insight and vRealize Operations, enable the collection and analysis of audit trails generated by applications and services responsible for attribute management. This facilitates the auditing of changes to security and privacy attributes.

Furthermore, VCF's inherent security features, including network segmentation via NSX, data-at-rest encryption with vSAN, and VM encryption, establish a robust environment for the secure processing, storage, and transmission of information, thereby supporting the integrity and retention of attribute associations.

The customer is responsible for defining the specific types of security and privacy attributes, their permissible values or ranges, and the systems to which they apply. The customer must also deploy and configure the necessary applications or data governance tools on the VCF platform to perform the actual association, retention, and review of these attributes with information. Establishing the frequency for reviewing attribute applicability and ensuring that attribute associations are consistently made and retained with information are also customer-managed processes."
AC-16(1),DCH-05.1,"Security and Privacy Attributes | Dynamic Attribute Association

Dynamically associate security and privacy attributes with [Assignment: organization-defined subjects and objects] in accordance with the following security and privacy policies as information is created and combined: [Assignment: organization-defined security and privacy policies].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides the foundational capabilities to dynamically associate security and privacy attributes with subjects and objects across the virtualized infrastructure. vSphere tags enable the classification of virtual machines, applications, and data stores, which can then be leveraged by other platform components. NSX-T Data Center security tags facilitate dynamic grouping of workloads and the enforcement of micro-segmentation policies based on these attributes. Furthermore, vSAN storage policies allow for attribute-based data handling, ensuring specific security and privacy requirements, such as encryption or data locality, are applied to virtual disk objects upon creation or modification. Automation frameworks, including vRealize Automation and PowerCLI, enable the programmatic application and modification of these attributes as information is created, combined, or moved within the environment.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific security and privacy attributes relevant to its data and systems, identifying the subjects and objects requiring such classification, and establishing the comprehensive security and privacy policies that govern these dynamic associations. While the platform offers the robust technical mechanisms for attribute enforcement and dynamic application, the content and scope of these policies remain an organizational mandate."
AC-16(2),DCH-05.2,"Security and Privacy Attributes | Attribute Value Changes by Authorized Individuals

Provide authorized individuals (or processes acting on behalf of individuals) the capability to define or change the value of associated security and privacy attributes.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation integrates with enterprise identity providers, enabling the assignment of roles and permissions to authorized individuals and processes. The platform provides granular access controls within vCenter Server, NSX, and vSAN, allowing administrators to define and modify user and group privileges. These capabilities extend to programmatic interfaces, ensuring that automated processes can also define or alter security and privacy attributes as delegated by authorized personnel.

The customer is responsible for establishing the specific policies that define ""authorized individuals"" and for determining which attributes are classified as ""security and privacy attributes"" within their broader organizational context. Furthermore, the customer must implement the necessary administrative processes and human accountability to ensure that changes to these attributes are performed in accordance with established security policies and operational procedures."
AC-16(3),DCH-05.3,"Security and Privacy Attributes | Maintenance of Attribute Associations by System

Maintain the association and integrity of [Assignment: organization-defined security and privacy attributes] to [Assignment: organization-defined subjects and objects].",Contributes,"VCF components, such as vSphere and NSX-T Data Center, provide robust mechanisms for defining and associating security and privacy attributes with various subjects and objects within the virtualized infrastructure. vSphere tags enable the classification of virtual machines, hosts, datastores, and networks with custom attributes, allowing for granular categorization based on security posture, data sensitivity, or operational role. NSX-T Data Center further extends this capability with security tags, which dynamically group workloads and facilitate the enforcement of micro-segmentation policies based on defined attributes. The platform inherently maintains the integrity of these attribute associations through its object model and access control mechanisms, ensuring that tags are consistently applied and their relationships to infrastructure components are preserved and auditable.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific security and privacy attributes relevant to its environment, identifying the subjects and objects requiring these associations, and establishing the policies and procedures for their consistent application and maintenance within the VCF environment. This includes developing a comprehensive tagging strategy and ensuring its operationalization."
AC-16(4),DCH-05.4,"Security and Privacy Attributes | Association of Attributes by Authorized Individuals

Provide the capability to associate [Assignment: organization-defined security and privacy attributes] with [Assignment: organization-defined subjects and objects] by authorized individuals (or processes acting on behalf of individuals).",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides the inherent capability to associate organization-defined security and privacy attributes with various subjects and objects across the virtualized infrastructure. Through vSphere Tags and Categories, administrators can define custom attributes (e.g., data classification, compliance scope) and apply them to virtual machines, hosts, datastores, and network objects. NSX-T Data Center extends this capability with security tags, enabling the dynamic grouping of network objects based on defined attributes for policy enforcement. All these operations are governed by robust Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) mechanisms, ensuring that only authorized individuals or automated processes acting on their behalf can perform these associations. The comprehensive API surface across VCF components further facilitates programmatic attribute assignment and management.

The customer is responsible for defining the specific security and privacy attributes relevant to their organization and determining which subjects and objects require these associations. Establishing and maintaining the authorization framework within VCF's RBAC to control who can define and apply these attributes, along with developing the overarching policies and procedures for attribute management, remains a customer obligation."
AC-16(5),DCH-05.5,"Security and Privacy Attributes | Attribute Displays on Objects to Be Output

Display security and privacy attributes in human-readable form on each object that the system transmits to output devices to identify [Assignment: organization-defined special dissemination, handling, or distribution instructions] using [Assignment: organization-defined human-readable, standard naming conventions].",Not Applicable,"End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope."
AC-16(6),DCH-05.6,"Security and Privacy Attributes | Maintenance of Attribute Association

Require personnel to associate and maintain the association of [Assignment: organization-defined security and privacy attributes] with [Assignment: organization-defined subjects and objects] in accordance with [Assignment: organization-defined security and privacy policies].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides the foundational capabilities to associate and maintain security and privacy attributes with infrastructure components. Furthermore, vSAN storage policies allow for the definition of storage attributes, such as encryption or RAID levels, which are associated with virtual machine objects to ensure data protection based on defined characteristics. Identity and access management within VCF, through vCenter SSO and integration with enterprise identity providers, facilitates the association of roles and permissions (attributes) with user accounts (subjects), controlling access to VCF resources.

The customer is responsible for defining the specific security and privacy attributes pertinent to their organization, identifying the subjects and objects to which these attributes apply, and establishing the comprehensive security and privacy policies that govern the association and maintenance of these attributes. AC-16(7),DCH-05.7,"Security and Privacy Attributes | Consistent Attribute Interpretation

Provide a consistent interpretation of security and privacy attributes transmitted between distributed system components.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) inherently provides mechanisms for consistent interpretation of security and privacy attributes across its distributed system components.

NSX-T Data Center ensures that security tags, groups, and policies are uniformly understood and enforced across the network fabric, guaranteeing consistent application of micro-segmentation and firewall rules. Similarly, vCenter Server manages role-based access control (RBAC) permissions and user attributes, ensuring these are consistently interpreted across all managed ESXi hosts and virtual machines, often integrating with external identity providers for a unified view. vSAN storage policies (SPBM) define and consistently apply attributes like availability and performance across the datastore.
These capabilities ensure that attributes critical to the platform's security and privacy posture are consistently interpreted and acted upon by VCF's internal components.

While VCF provides the foundational infrastructure and tools for consistent attribute interpretation, the customer is responsible for defining the specific security and privacy attributes relevant to their custom applications and data, and for designing those applications to leverage VCF's capabilities (e.g., NSX-T tags, vCenter RBAC) or implement their own consistent interpretation mechanisms. The customer must establish policies and processes for attribute definition, usage, and enforcement within their application layer to fully meet the control's intent."
AC-16(8),DCH-05.8,"Security and Privacy Attributes | Association Techniques and Technologies

Implement [Assignment: organization-defined techniques and technologies] in associating security and privacy attributes to information.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust capabilities for associating security and privacy attributes with infrastructure components that host information.

vSphere Tags enable the application of custom metadata to virtual machines, hosts, datastores, and other vSphere objects, allowing for programmatic identification and management based on defined security classifications or privacy requirements. NSX Security Tags facilitate dynamic grouping of virtual machines and network objects, enabling the enforcement of granular micro-segmentation policies that directly associate security attributes with network endpoints. Furthermore, vSAN Storage Policies allow for the definition of data protection and availability attributes, including encryption at rest, which is a critical security attribute applied to information storage.

While the platform offers the technical mechanisms for attribute association, the organization is responsible for defining the specific security and privacy attributes relevant to their information, establishing the techniques and technologies for their application, and implementing the overarching policies and processes for associating these attributes. AC-16(9),DCH-05.9,"Security and Privacy Attributes | Attribute Reassignment — Regrading Mechanisms

Change security and privacy attributes associated with information only via regrading mechanisms validated using [Assignment: organization-defined techniques or procedures].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides the foundational infrastructure and management plane to define, assign, and modify security and privacy attributes associated with virtualized workloads and network segments. Through NSX, security tags and groups can be dynamically assigned or reassigned to virtual machines and network interfaces, driving granular micro-segmentation policies. vSphere allows for the application of custom attributes to virtual machines and other inventory objects, which can be leveraged by external tools or automation workflows to influence security posture. AC-16(10),DCH-05.10,"Security and Privacy Attributes | Attribute Configuration by Authorized Individuals

The direct implementation of mechanisms that enable authorized users to determine if access authorizations match information access and use restrictions, or to employ automated or manual processes to assist users in making information sharing decisions, falls outside the scope of the VMware Cloud Foundation platform. The organization is responsible for defining its specific information sharing restrictions, classifying data, and establishing the policies that govern permissible search and retrieval activities. column2: Implement mechanisms] to ensure [Assignment: organization-defined access control decisions] are applied to each access request prior to access enforcement.",Contributes,"The VCF platform inherently provides robust mechanisms to ensure access control decisions are applied prior to access enforcement. column1: Specifically, NSX-T Data Center implements distributed firewalling and micro-segmentation, enforcing network access policies based on defined security groups and rules in real-time, ensuring that all network communication requests are evaluated against established access control decisions before any traffic is permitted. Similarly, vCenter Server enforces permissions for management plane operations, validating user and group authorizations against assigned roles before allowing actions on virtual infrastructure components.

While VCF provides the technical framework for enforcing these decisions, the organization is responsible for defining its specific access control policies, establishing the procedures for their creation and maintenance, and configuring the appropriate roles, permissions, and network security rules within the VCF environment to reflect these organizational decisions."
AC-25,IAC-27,"Reference Monitor

Implement a reference monitor for [Assignment: organization-defined access control policies] that is tamperproof, always invoked, and small enough to be subject to analysis and testing, the completeness of which can be assured.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides a robust, multi-layered architecture that inherently functions as a reference monitor for virtualized resources and network traffic. The ESXi hypervisor, as the foundational layer, mediates all access to underlying hardware, ensuring isolation and controlled resource allocation. Its design incorporates secure boot, signed modules, and a minimal attack surface, contributing to its tamper-resistance and ensuring it is always invoked as the core operating system. Furthermore, vSphere Trust Authority enhances the integrity and trustworthiness of the compute infrastructure by establishing a hardware root of trust. The vCenter Server enforces granular role-based access control for all management plane operations, ensuring only authorized personnel can interact with the virtual environment. NSX-T Data Center extends this by implementing a distributed firewall directly within the ESXi kernel, providing always-invoked, tamper-resistant micro-segmentation and network access control for workloads. These core components are designed for rigorous analysis and testing, with their completeness assured through VMware's development and validation processes.

While the platform provides the foundational mechanisms for a reference monitor, the organization is responsible for defining its specific access control policies. This includes establishing detailed role-based access control policies within vCenter Server and configuring precise network segmentation and firewall rules within NSX-T Data Center to align with their security requirements. The customer must also ensure these policies are comprehensively documented, regularly reviewed, and effectively tested to validate their completeness and efficacy."

Implementing dual authorization for changes requires the customer to define specific organizational policies and operational procedures that mandate a two-person rule or an explicit approval workflow. This includes configuring RBAC roles to enforce separation of duties, where one individual initiates a change and a second, authorized individual approves its implementation. While VCF provides the technical foundation for access control and auditing, the establishment and enforcement of the dual authorization *workflow* itself, including the integration with external change management processes or the definition of specific multi-step internal processes, remains a customer responsibility."
CM-5(5),CHG-04.4,"Access Restrictions for Change | Privilege Limitation for Production and Operation
(a) Limit privileges to change system components and system-related information within a production or operational environment

(b) Review and reevaluate privileges [Assignment: organization-defined frequency].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) implements robust Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) across its entire software-defined data center stack, encompassing vSphere, vSAN, NSX-T, and SDDC Manager. This capability enables precise control over the assignment of privileges, ensuring that access to initiate changes to system components and system-related information within production and operational environments is strictly limited.

Granular roles and permissions can be defined and assigned to users and groups, enforcing the principle of least privilege for all operational activities. Furthermore, the SDDC Manager automates critical lifecycle management operations, such as patching and upgrades, which inherently restricts direct human intervention and associated privileges for core infrastructure changes, channeling them through controlled, automated workflows.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific roles and permissions that align with its least privilege policies and for establishing a formal process for the periodic review and reevaluation of these assigned privileges. This includes defining the frequency of such reviews, documenting the outcomes, and enforcing any necessary adjustments to privilege assignments."
CM-5(6),CHG-04.5,"Access Restrictions for Change | Limit Library Privileges

Limit privileges to change software resident within software libraries.",Contributes,"The VCF platform implements robust role-based access control (RBAC) within SDDC Manager and vCenter Server to strictly limit privileges for modifying VCF's own software components, update bundles, and configuration files. This ensures that changes to the underlying cloud infrastructure software are controlled and auditable, effectively managing access to VCF's internal software libraries.

While VCF provides the secure and resilient infrastructure for hosting customer-defined software libraries, such as source code repositories, artifact management systems, or container registries, the implementation and enforcement of specific access restrictions and privilege limitations *within* these customer-deployed software libraries remain the responsibility of the customer. This requires configuring the access control mechanisms inherent to the chosen software library solution."
CM-6,"CFG-02, CFG-02.7","Configuration Settings

a. Establish and document configuration settings for components employed within the system that reflect the most restrictive mode consistent with operational requirements using [Assignment: organization-defined common secure configurations]
b. Implement the configuration settings

c. Identify, document, and approve any deviations from established configuration settings for [Assignment: organization-defined system components] based on [Assignment: organization-defined operational requirements]

d. Monitor and control changes to the configuration settings in accordance with organizational policies and procedures.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation establishes a secure baseline by deploying a standardized, validated architecture with secure default configurations for its core components, including vSphere, vSAN, NSX, and vCenter. The SDDC Manager automates the initial implementation of these settings and manages the lifecycle, ensuring configuration consistency across the environment. VCF components provide robust logging capabilities that capture configuration changes, enabling monitoring and control. Tools such as vSphere Host Profiles facilitate the enforcement of desired state configurations for ESXi hosts and can detect configuration drift.

However, the organization is responsible for defining its specific ""common secure configurations"" and ""operational requirements"" that inform the VCF deployment and ongoing management. Establishing and documenting these organization-defined settings, as well as identifying, documenting, and approving any deviations, falls to the customer. Furthermore, the organization must implement and enforce its own policies and procedures for monitoring and controlling changes to configuration settings, integrating VCF's technical capabilities into a comprehensive configuration management program."
CM-6(1),CFG-02.2,"Configuration Settings | Automated Management, Application, and Verification

Manage, apply, and verify configuration settings for [Assignment: organization-defined system components] using [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive capabilities for automated management, application, and verification of configuration settings across the entire SDDC stack. SDDC Manager automates the deployment, patching, and desired state configuration of ESXi hosts, vCenter Server, NSX Manager, and vSAN. vCenter Server leverages Host Profiles to enforce consistent configurations across ESXi hosts and manages virtual machine settings. NSX Manager automates the application of network and security policies, including firewall rules and segmentation. Aria Automation extends these capabilities by enabling the definition and automated deployment of application and workload configurations, often integrating with external configuration management tools. Aria Operations continuously monitors for configuration drift and compliance against established baselines, providing automated verification that settings are correctly applied and maintained.

While VCF delivers the robust technical mechanisms for automation, the organization remains responsible for defining the specific system components in scope for automated management and for establishing the precise configuration baselines and desired states that must be enforced. The customer must also define the overarching configuration management policies, change control processes, and human accountability for managing and reviewing these automated processes."
CM-6(2),CFG-02.8,"Configuration Settings | Respond to Unauthorized Changes

Take the following actions in response to unauthorized changes to [Assignment: organization-defined configuration settings]: [Assignment: organization-defined actions].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust capabilities for detecting and responding to unauthorized changes in configuration settings across its components. vCenter Server leverages Host Profiles to define and enforce desired configurations for ESXi hosts, automatically detecting and reporting any drift from the established baseline. SDDC Manager maintains the desired state for the VCF management domain components, identifying and alerting on unauthorized modifications. NSX-T Manager similarly ensures the integrity of network configurations. Furthermore, integrated logging and monitoring solutions like vRealize Log Insight and vRealize Operations Manager can ingest events from all VCF components, enabling the creation of custom alerts and dashboards to identify suspicious or unauthorized configuration changes in real-time. These alerts can be configured to trigger automated workflows via vRealize Orchestrator, facilitating a programmatic response.

The customer is responsible for defining the specific configuration settings that are critical to monitor and protect. They must also establish the organization-defined actions to be taken in response to detected unauthorized changes, including defining remediation procedures, escalation paths, and incident response workflows. While VCF provides the technical mechanisms for detection and potential automation, the overarching policy, process, and human accountability for defining, implementing, and overseeing these responses remain with the customer."
CM-7,CFG-03,"Least Functionality
a. Configure the system to provide only [Assignment: organization-defined mission essential capabilities]

b. Prohibit or restrict the use of the following functions, ports, protocols, software, and/or services: [Assignment: organization-defined prohibited or restricted functions, system ports, protocols, software, and/or services].",Contributes,"The VCF platform is architected with least functionality principles inherent to its design, deploying only the essential services required for its management plane and the delivery of virtualized infrastructure. Core components such as ESXi, vCenter Server, and NSX-T are hardened by default, with unnecessary services disabled to minimize the attack surface.

NSX-T provides robust network segmentation and distributed firewall capabilities, enabling granular control over ports, protocols, and services at the virtual machine and network segment level, which is critical for restricting unauthorized communications and functions.

While the platform provides the technical mechanisms to enforce least functionality, the organization is responsible for defining its specific mission-essential capabilities for tenant workloads and establishing comprehensive policies for prohibited or restricted functions, ports, protocols, software, and services. The customer must then leverage VCF's native security features, including NSX-T firewall rules, host profiles, and application-level configurations, to implement and enforce these organizational policies across their deployed applications and infrastructure."
CM-7(1),CFG-03.1,"Least Functionality | Periodic Review
(a) Review the system [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] to identify unnecessary and/or nonsecure functions, ports, protocols, software, and services

(b) Disable or remove [Assignment: organization-defined functions, ports, protocols, software, and services within the system deemed to be unnecessary and/or nonsecure].",Contributes,"The VCF platform is engineered with a foundational principle of least functionality, ensuring that core SDDC components operate with only essential services enabled by default. NSX-T Data Center provides robust network segmentation capabilities, allowing for precise control over ports and protocols, thereby enforcing least privilege at the network layer. This enables administrators to define and restrict network communication to only those services explicitly required for workload operation. Furthermore, vCenter Server provides the management plane to configure and manage virtual machines and ESXi hosts, offering controls to disable or remove unnecessary virtual hardware or services at the platform level.

While the platform provides the technical mechanisms to enforce least functionality, the organization is responsible for defining the frequency of system reviews and actively identifying unnecessary or nonsecure functions, ports, protocols, software, and services within their specific application workloads and guest operating systems. This includes establishing the processes for periodic assessment, making determinations on what constitutes 'unnecessary' for their unique operational context, and subsequently utilizing the platform's capabilities to disable or remove identified components. The overarching policy and procedural framework for these reviews and subsequent remediation actions remain an organizational accountability."
CM-7(2),"CFG-03.2, SEA-06","Least Functionality | Prevent Program Execution
Prevent program execution in accordance with [Selection (one or more):
[Assignment: organization-defined policies, rules of behavior, and/or access agreements regarding software program usage and restrictions]

column2: rules authorizing the terms and conditions of software program usage].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation establishes a robust and secure virtualized infrastructure, providing a trusted execution environment for workloads. The platform ensures the integrity of the hypervisor and virtual machines through features such as Secure Boot for ESXi hosts and virtual machines, host hardening, and secure configuration baselines. This foundational security is critical for ensuring that any guest operating system-level controls for program execution prevention operate within a protected and uncompromised environment.

column1: The direct enforcement of policies to prevent unauthorized program execution, based on organization-defined rules of behavior, software usage restrictions, or access agreements, is a responsibility of the customer. This typically requires implementing operating system-level controls, application whitelisting or blacklisting solutions, or endpoint detection and response (EDR) platforms within the guest virtual machines. The customer must define and implement these specific policies and the mechanisms to enforce them at the workload level."
CM-7(4),CFG-03.3,"Least Functionality | Unauthorized Software
(a) Identify [Assignment: organization-defined software programs not authorized to execute on the system]
(b) Employ an allow-all, deny-by-exception policy to prohibit the execution of unauthorized software programs on the system

(c) Review and update the list of unauthorized software programs [Assignment: organization-defined frequency].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides a robust and secure infrastructure foundation, leveraging vSphere for host integrity and vSAN for secure storage. NSX-T Data Center offers advanced micro-segmentation capabilities, enabling granular network access controls that can restrict unauthorized software from communicating with other systems or external networks. This network isolation helps contain potential threats from unauthorized applications by limiting their ability to exfiltrate data or spread laterally.

The direct enforcement of an allow-all, deny-by-exception policy to prohibit the *execution* of unauthorized software programs within guest operating systems is not a native capability of the core VMware Cloud Foundation platform. Organizations must define the specific unauthorized software programs and implement dedicated endpoint security solutions, such as application whitelisting or endpoint detection and response (EDR) tools, within their virtual machines to achieve this execution prevention. Furthermore, the organization is responsible for establishing and adhering to a defined frequency for reviewing and updating the list of unauthorized software programs."
CM-7(5),CFG-03.3,"Least Functionality | Authorized Software
(a) Identify [Assignment: organization-defined software programs authorized to execute on the system]
(b) Employ a deny-all, permit-by-exception policy to allow the execution of authorized software programs on the system

(c) Review and update the list of authorized software programs [Assignment: organization-defined frequency].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) ensures the integrity and authorized execution of its core infrastructure components, including ESXi, vCenter Server, NSX, and vSAN. The platform's lifecycle management capabilities maintain a known good state for all VCF-managed software, effectively implementing a deny-all, permit-by-exception policy for its own operational software. Secure Boot and host integrity mechanisms further restrict unauthorized software from executing at the hypervisor level.

The organization remains responsible for defining the specific software programs authorized to execute within guest operating systems and applications deployed on the VCF infrastructure. Implementing a deny-all, permit-by-exception policy for these workloads requires the deployment and configuration of host-based security solutions or application control mechanisms within the guest virtual machines. The organization must also establish and adhere to a defined frequency for reviewing and updating the list of authorized software programs for their workloads."
CM-7(6),CFG-02.5,"Least Functionality | Confined Environments with Limited Privileges

Require that the following user-installed software execute in a confined physical or virtual machine environment with limited privileges: [Assignment: organization-defined user-installed software].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides the foundational infrastructure to host user-installed software within inherently confined virtual machine environments. The vSphere hypervisor ensures robust isolation between virtual machines, preventing unauthorized access or interference between workloads and from the guest OS to the underlying hardware.

NSX-T Data Center further enhances this confinement through micro-segmentation, enabling granular network policies that restrict communication paths for specific virtual machines or applications to only essential services, thereby limiting potential attack surfaces and privilege escalation vectors.
The organization is responsible for defining the specific user-installed software that must operate under these controls.

Additionally, the implementation of ""limited privileges"" within the guest operating system for that software, including user account permissions, application whitelisting, and other OS-level security configurations, remains a customer responsibility. This also encompasses establishing the policies and procedures governing the installation and execution of such software."
CM-7(7),CFG-02.5,"Least Functionality | Code Execution in Protected Environments

Allow execution of binary or machine-executable code only in confined physical or virtual machine environments and with the explicit approval of [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles] when such code is:
(a) Obtained from sources with limited or no warranty

(b) Without the provision of source code.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) establishes a highly secure and isolated virtualized infrastructure, inherently providing confined virtual machine environments. The platform leverages vSphere for robust VM isolation, secure boot, and host hardening, ensuring the integrity of the execution environment. NSX-T Data Center further enhances this confinement through micro-segmentation capabilities, allowing for granular network isolation of workloads and preventing unauthorized lateral movement, even for code running within guest operating systems. Workload domains within VCF provide logical separation, creating distinct, secure boundaries for different types of applications or code execution.

While VCF delivers the secure execution environments, the organization is responsible for defining the specific personnel or roles authorized to approve the execution of binary or machine-executable code. This includes establishing the policies and procedures for identifying code obtained from sources with limited or no warranty or without source code, and implementing the explicit approval workflow required before such code is deployed or executed within the VCF environment. The platform provides the secure foundation, but the governance and operational processes for code approval and source validation reside with the customer."
CM-7(8),END-06.7,"Least Functionality | Binary or Machine Executable Code
(a) Prohibit the use of binary or machine-executable code from sources with limited or no warranty or without the provision of source code

(b) Allow exceptions only for compelling mission or operational requirements and with the approval of the authorizing official.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation establishes a highly secure and validated infrastructure platform, ensuring the integrity of its own components through secure boot, code signing, and validated update processes.

It provides a robust and isolated environment for deploying and running customer workloads, including virtual machines and containers, which can host various binary and machine-executable code. The platform's capabilities for secure configuration, network segmentation, and access control contribute to containing the operational impact of any code executed within the environment.

However, the core responsibility for prohibiting the use of binary or machine-executable code from sources with limited or no warranty, or without the provision of source code, rests with the customer. This requires organizational policies, supply chain risk management processes, and technical controls implemented at the application or operating system layer within the customer's workload. Furthermore, the management and approval of exceptions for compelling mission or operational requirements, including obtaining authorization from an official, are entirely customer-defined governance and procedural functions that fall outside the direct technical scope of the VMware Cloud Foundation platform."
CM-7(9),CFG-02.5,"Least Functionality | Prohibiting The Use of Unauthorized Hardware
(a) Identify [Assignment: organization-defined hardware components authorized for system use]
(b) Prohibit the use or connection of unauthorized hardware components

(c) Review and update the list of authorized hardware components [Assignment: organization-defined frequency].",Contributes,"The VCF platform inherently operates on a defined set of authorized hardware components, strictly adhering to the VMware Hardware Compatibility List (HCL) for its underlying physical infrastructure, including ESXi hosts and network devices. This ensures that the foundational components of the Software-Defined Data Center (SDDC) are supported and validated. Within the virtualized environment, VCF provides robust capabilities to manage and configure virtual hardware for virtual machines, allowing for the enforcement of authorized virtual device configurations and preventing the introduction of unauthorized virtual components.

The organization retains the responsibility for defining and maintaining a comprehensive list of all authorized hardware components across the entire enterprise, extending beyond the VCF-managed infrastructure. This includes establishing and enforcing policies and technical controls to prohibit the physical connection or use of unauthorized hardware components at endpoints, network access points, and other non-VCF managed systems. Furthermore, the organization must define the frequency for reviewing and updating its overall list of authorized hardware components and the associated prohibition mechanisms."
CM-8,"AST-02, AST-02.3","System Component Inventory
a. Develop and document an inventory of system components that:
1. Accurately reflects the system
2. Includes all components within the system
3. Does not include duplicate accounting of components or components assigned to any other system
4. Is at the level of granularity deemed necessary for tracking and reporting
5. Includes the following information to achieve system component accountability: [Assignment: organization-defined information deemed necessary to achieve effective system component accountability]

b. Review and update the system component inventory [Assignment: organization-defined frequency].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) inherently provides robust capabilities for system component inventory management. The platform, through its integrated management stack including SDDC Manager, vCenter Server, NSX-T Manager, and vSAN, automatically discovers and maintains a comprehensive inventory of its underlying infrastructure components. This includes compute hosts, virtual machines, network segments, storage devices, and management appliances.

The inventory accurately reflects the deployed environment, encompassing all managed components within the VCF domain without duplicate accounting. VCF continuously tracks and updates component details, providing extensive information such as hardware specifications, software versions, network configurations, and interdependencies, which are essential for system component accountability.
While VCF provides the technical foundation for an accurate and continuously updated inventory of its infrastructure, the customer retains critical responsibilities.

The organization must define the specific level of granularity required for tracking and reporting, as well as the precise information deemed necessary to achieve effective system component accountability. Furthermore, the customer is responsible for establishing and documenting the formal processes for reviewing and updating the system component inventory at an organization-defined frequency. This also includes ensuring that the VCF-managed inventory integrates appropriately with broader enterprise asset management systems to prevent duplicate accounting of components across different systems and to encompass all system components, including those at the application layer running within virtual machines, which are outside VCF's direct infrastructure management scope."
CM-8(1),AST-02.1,"System Component Inventory | Updates During Installation and Removal

Update the inventory of system components as part of component installations, removals, and system updates.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) inherently maintains a comprehensive and current inventory of its managed system components through the SDDC Manager. As a central lifecycle management engine, SDDC Manager automatically updates its internal inventory whenever new hosts are added, workload domains are provisioned, or existing components are decommissioned. This includes detailed records of compute, storage, networking, and management components, ensuring that the platform's operational state is accurately reflected in its inventory. Furthermore, during system updates, patching, or upgrades orchestrated by SDDC Manager, the inventory is dynamically refreshed to reflect the current versions and configurations of all managed infrastructure elements.

While VCF provides robust automated inventory updates for its infrastructure components, the customer is responsible for establishing the overarching organizational policies and procedures that govern the complete system component inventory. This includes defining the scope of components to be inventoried beyond the VCF infrastructure itself, such as guest operating systems, applications, and other non-VCF managed assets. The customer must also implement processes for integrating VCF's detailed component inventory data into their enterprise-wide Configuration Management Database (CMDB) or asset management system, ensuring a unified and authoritative source of truth for all system components."
CM-8(2),AST-02.9,"System Component Inventory | Automated Maintenance

Maintain the currency, completeness, accuracy, and availability of the inventory of system components using [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) inherently maintains a comprehensive, current, accurate, and available inventory of its deployed system components through the SDDC Manager. This includes ESXi hosts, vCenter Servers, NSX-T Managers, vSAN datastores, and workload domains. The SDDC Manager continuously updates this inventory as components are added, removed, or upgraded, ensuring its currency and completeness. The inventory is readily accessible via the SDDC Manager user interface and APIs, providing high availability and accuracy reflecting the deployed state. Additional components and their configurations are inventoried by vCenter Server, and operational details can be further aggregated and maintained by vRealize Operations.

The organization is responsible for defining the full scope of ""system components"" that require inventory management, which may extend beyond the VCF infrastructure itself to include guest operating systems, applications, or other non-VCF managed assets. The customer must also establish and implement policies and procedures for the use and verification of these automated inventory mechanisms to ensure they meet organizational requirements for all defined system components."
CM-8(3),"AST-02.2, CFG-05.1, END-03.1","System Component Inventory | Automated Unauthorized Component Detection
(a) Detect the presence of unauthorized hardware, software, and firmware components within the system using [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms]
[Assignment: organization-defined frequency]
(b) Take the following actions when unauthorized components are detected: [Selection (one or more): disable network access by such components

].",Contributes,"The platform provides comprehensive automated mechanisms for discovering and maintaining an inventory of all hardware, software, and firmware components within the system. This capability includes continuous monitoring to identify the presence of new or unauthorized components by comparing discovered assets against established baselines and authorized inventories. It can detect deviations from approved configurations, thereby identifying unauthorized software or firmware.

Upon the detection of unauthorized components or significant configuration drift, the platform is equipped to initiate automated response actions. These actions include the ability to isolate affected components from the network to prevent further compromise and to automatically generate notifications to designated security operations personnel.
To fully satisfy this control, the organization must define what constitutes an unauthorized component by establishing and maintaining an authoritative inventory and baseline.

The organization is responsible for specifying the automated mechanisms to be employed for detection, defining the frequency of these detection activities, and selecting the precise actions to be taken when unauthorized components are identified.
Furthermore, the organization must identify the specific personnel or roles responsible for receiving notifications and for managing the overall response process."

Include in the system component inventory information, a means for identifying by [Selection (one or more): name

This inventory provides detailed technical specifications and operational status for all deployed SDDC components, forming the basis for a robust asset management program. The platform's internal role-based access control mechanisms define permissions for users to operate and manage these components within the VCF environment.

column1: The platform does not natively provide a means to directly associate specific individuals, positions, or organizational roles with the overarching administrative responsibility and accountability for these components. Establishing and maintaining the definitive linkage between technical inventory items and the responsible human entities requires customer-defined processes and integration with external configuration management databases or asset management systems."
CM-8(6),AST-02.4,"System Component Inventory | Assessed Configurations and Approved Deviations

Include assessed component configurations and any approved deviations to current deployed configurations in the system component inventory.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation maintains a comprehensive inventory of all SDDC components, including ESXi hosts, vCenter Server, NSX-T, and vSAN. The platform enforces a desired state configuration across these components, continuously monitoring for compliance and detecting any configuration drift from the established baseline. This provides the technical foundation for identifying and assessing component configurations.

The platform identifies current configurations and detects deviations from the desired state. However, the formal process for approving these deviations and ensuring their inclusion in a comprehensive system component inventory is a customer responsibility.

Customers must establish policies and procedures for configuration deviation management, including the approval workflow, and integrate VCF's configuration data into their broader asset management and configuration management database (CMDB) systems to fully satisfy the control's requirement for tracking approved deviations."
CM-8(7),AST-02.9,"System Component Inventory | Centralized Repository

Provide a centralized repository for the inventory of system components.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation inherently maintains a centralized repository for its own system components through the SDDC Manager. This management plane provides a comprehensive, authoritative inventory of all deployed VCF infrastructure, encompassing compute, storage, networking, and management domains, including ESXi hosts, vCenter Servers, NSX-T Managers, and vSAN clusters. This capability ensures that the platform's foundational elements are consistently tracked and managed from a single source.

While VCF provides this robust inventory for its own infrastructure, the customer is responsible for defining the full scope of ""system components"" within their broader enterprise environment. This includes establishing processes for integrating VCF's component inventory with an overarching enterprise Configuration Management Database (CMDB) and ensuring the completeness, accuracy, and ongoing maintenance of all system components, particularly those extending beyond the direct VCF deployment."
CM-8(8),AST-02.10,"System Component Inventory | Automated Location Tracking

Support the tracking of system components by geographic location using [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive capabilities for automated inventory and tracking of system components within the Software-Defined Datacenter (SDDC). The platform's management plane, including vCenter Server and Aria Operations, maintains a real-time inventory of virtual machines, hosts, datastores, and network components, tracking their logical placement within clusters, resource pools, and datacenters. This includes the ability to track components across geographically dispersed vCenter instances linked in Enhanced Linked Mode, providing visibility into their operational location. The platform automatically updates component status and location changes as virtual machines migrate or infrastructure scales.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific automated mechanisms and configuring the VCF environment to align with its definition of geographic location tracking. This includes accurately mapping the logical VCF inventory to physical geographic locations (e.g., associating vCenter datacenters with specific physical sites or regions) and establishing the policies and procedures for maintaining this information."
CM-8(9),AST-02.11,"System Component Inventory | Assignment of Components to Systems
(a) Assign system components to a system

(b) Receive an acknowledgement from [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles] of this assignment.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation maintains a comprehensive inventory of its managed components, including virtual machines, hosts, networks, and storage. The platform inherently assigns these components to logical systems such as vSphere clusters, resource pools, and network segments, establishing clear relationships within the infrastructure. This capability provides the necessary data and structure to identify which components belong to which systems.

Establishing the formal process for assigning system components to specific systems and ensuring that organization-defined personnel or roles formally acknowledge these assignments remains a customer responsibility. This requires defining the specific personnel or roles, implementing a workflow for acknowledgement, and maintaining records of such acknowledgements outside of the platform's direct operational scope."
CM-9,"CHG-05, CFG-01","Configuration Management Plan

c. Defines the configuration items for the system and places the configuration items under configuration management
d. Is reviewed and approved by [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles]

e. Protects the configuration management plan from unauthorized disclosure and modification.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides a highly standardized and automated platform that inherently manages the configuration of its own components, including ESXi hosts, vCenter Server, NSX, vSAN, and SDDC Manager. The platform's lifecycle management capabilities ensure that the underlying infrastructure maintains a consistent and desired state, significantly reducing the scope and complexity of configuration management for the customer's foundational infrastructure. This includes automated deployment, patching, and upgrades, which are critical aspects of configuration management processes.

The organization remains responsible for developing, documenting, and implementing a comprehensive configuration management plan that addresses specific roles, responsibilities, and configuration management processes and procedures pertinent to their overall system and applications.
