] at [Assignment: organization-defined level of detail].",Contributes,"The VCF platform provides comprehensive design and implementation information for its own security controls, encompassing detailed architectural specifications, security configuration guides, and API documentation. This foundational information is essential for understanding the platform's inherent security posture and how its controls are designed and implemented. The platform serves as the secure infrastructure upon which customer-developed or acquired systems, components, and services operate, and for which design and implementation information for their respective controls would be required.

The organization is responsible for establishing and enforcing policies and contractual obligations to require developers of systems, components, or services to provide the necessary design and implementation information for their controls. This includes defining the specific types of information (e.g., security-relevant external system interfaces, high-level design, low-level design, source code, hardware schematics) and the required level of detail, as well as implementing the processes for collecting, reviewing, and maintaining this documentation."
SA-4(3),"TDA-02.3, TDA-06","Acquisition Process | Development Methods, Techniques, and Practices
Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to demonstrate the use of a system development life cycle process that includes:
(a) [Assignment: organization-defined systems engineering methods]
(b) [Assignment: organization-defined Selection (one or more): systems security
(c) [Assignment: organization-defined software development methods
column2: testing, evaluation, assessment, verification, and validation methods
column3: and quality control processes].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides a highly automated, standardized, and secure infrastructure foundation that supports the implementation of robust system development life cycle (SDLC) processes. The platform's capabilities for infrastructure-as-code, consistent deployment, and integrated security features (such as network segmentation, encryption, and access controls) enable organizations to build and operate development, testing, and production environments with defined configurations and security postures. This standardization and automation facilitate the integration of systems engineering principles and security/privacy-by-design considerations into the development and deployment of systems and services.

column1: Adherence to this control requires the organization to define and document its specific systems engineering methods, systems security and privacy engineering methods, software development methods, and comprehensive testing, evaluation, assessment, verification, validation, and quality control processes. The organization must also demonstrate the consistent application of these defined methods and processes throughout the system development life cycle for all systems, system components, and system services developed or acquired. The platform provides the technical foundation, but the overarching SDLC governance, policy enforcement, and procedural execution remain organizational responsibilities."
SA-4(5),TDA-02.4,"Acquisition Process | System, Component, and Service Configurations
Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to:
(a) Deliver the system, component, or service with [Assignment: organization-defined security configurations] implemented

(b) Use the configurations as the default for any subsequent system, component, or service reinstallation or upgrade.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) delivers its core components, including ESXi, vCenter, NSX-T, and vSAN, with a highly opinionated and securely configured baseline. The SDDC Manager automates the deployment and initial configuration of these components, ensuring that they are implemented with robust security settings by default. This automated provisioning guarantees that the platform's foundational elements are delivered in a known, secure state. Furthermore, VCF's integrated lifecycle management capabilities ensure that these secure configurations are consistently maintained and applied during all subsequent upgrades, patches, and re-deployments of the platform's components, establishing them as the default for ongoing operations.

The organization remains responsible for defining the specific security configurations that extend beyond the VCF platform's inherent defaults, particularly for applications, custom system components, and services that are developed or acquired and subsequently deployed on VCF. The organization must establish and enforce policies and processes to ensure that the developers of these systems and services adhere to the organization-defined security configurations upon delivery and for all subsequent reinstallations or upgrades."
SA-4(6),TDA-03,"Acquisition Process | Use of Information Assurance Products

(a) Employ only government off-the-shelf or commercial off-the-shelf information assurance and information assurance-enabled information technology products that compose an NSA-approved solution to protect classified information when the networks used to transmit the information are at a lower classification level than the information being transmitted

(b) Ensure that these products have been evaluated and/or validated by NSA or in accordance with NSA-approved procedures.",Not Applicable,"End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope."
SA-4(7),TDA-02.2,"Acquisition Process | Niap-approved Protection Profiles

(a) Limit the use of commercially provided information assurance and information assurance-enabled information technology products to those products that have been successfully evaluated against a National Information Assurance partnership (NIAP)-approved Protection Profile for a specific technology type, if such a profile exists

(b) Require, if no NIAP-approved Protection Profile exists for a specific technology type but a commercially provided information technology product relies on cryptographic functionality to enforce its security policy, that the cryptographic module is FIPS-validated or NSA-approved.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) components, including ESXi, vCenter Server, NSX, and vSAN, are developed with a strong emphasis on security certifications. Many core VCF components have undergone successful evaluations against National Information Assurance Partnership (NIAP)-approved Protection Profiles for their specific technology types, such as Virtualization, Network Device, and Operating System. This ensures that the platform's foundational elements meet stringent security requirements. Furthermore, cryptographic modules within VCF components that enforce security policies are routinely FIPS 140-2 validated, ensuring that the underlying cryptographic functionality meets federal standards for secure processing.

The organization is responsible for establishing and enforcing its acquisition process to limit the use of commercially provided information assurance and information assurance-enabled information technology products to those successfully evaluated against NIAP-approved Protection Profiles, where applicable. For products without a NIAP-approved Protection Profile but relying on cryptographic functionality, the organization must ensure that the cryptographic modules are FIPS-validated or NSA-approved as part of its procurement and deployment policies. This includes the acquisition of any third-party applications, appliances, or services that are deployed within or integrated with the VCF environment."
SA-4(8),TDA-09.1,"Acquisition Process | Continuous Monitoring Plan for Controls

Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to produce a plan for continuous monitoring of control effectiveness that is consistent with the continuous monitoring program of the organization.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides the integrated management and operational tooling essential for establishing and executing continuous monitoring of control effectiveness. The platform natively integrates with vRealize Operations Manager and vRealize Log Insight, delivering comprehensive visibility into system performance, configuration compliance, and security events. These capabilities enable real-time detection and analysis, directly supporting the ongoing assessment of control effectiveness across the virtualized infrastructure.

The responsibility for developing a formal continuous monitoring plan, including defining its scope, metrics, reporting procedures, and alignment with the organization's broader continuous monitoring program, rests with the customer. This includes establishing the processes and assigning the personnel accountable for plan execution and oversight."
SA-4(9),TDA-02.1,"Acquisition Process | Functions, Ports, Protocols, and Services in Use

Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to identify the functions, ports, protocols, and services intended for organizational use.",Contributes,"The VCF platform, as a system component, inherently identifies its own functions, ports, protocols, and services. VMware, as the developer, provides comprehensive documentation detailing these aspects, including network port diagrams, service dependencies, and API specifications. This information is readily available in official product guides, security configuration guides, and hardening guides, enabling organizations to understand and manage the platform's intended operational characteristics.
The control's primary intent is to establish an organizational requirement for *any* developer to identify these elements for *any* system, component, or service intended for organizational use.

While VCF provides the infrastructure, it does not offer technical mechanisms to automate the enforcement of this identification requirement on third-party developers or to facilitate the documentation process for applications and services deployed *on* VCF. The customer is responsible for defining and implementing acquisition policies, contractual obligations, and internal development lifecycle processes to ensure that all systems, components, and services, regardless of their deployment location, have their functions, ports, protocols, and services formally identified by their respective developers."
SA-4(10),TDA-02.2,"Acquisition Process | Use of Approved PIV Products

Employ only information technology products on the FIPS 201-approved products list for Personal Identity Verification (PIV) capability implemented within organizational systems.",Not Applicable,"End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope."
SA-4(12),"AST-03, DCH-01.1, PRI-09","Acquisition Process | Data Ownership
(a) Include organizational data ownership requirements in the acquisition contract

(b) Require all data to be removed from the contractor’s system and returned to the organization within [Assignment: organization-defined time frame].",Contributes,"VCF secures organizational data through robust encryption capabilities for data at rest and in transit, ensuring confidentiality and integrity within the platform's boundaries. The platform provides a highly available and resilient storage infrastructure, which can be configured to meet stringent data retention and disposal requirements once data is within the organization's control. This includes mechanisms for secure data transfer into the VCF environment and lifecycle management for data residing on VCF-managed storage.

The establishment of organizational data ownership requirements within acquisition contracts, the enforcement of data removal clauses with external contractors, and the verification of data deletion from third-party systems are organizational policy, legal, and procedural responsibilities. VCF does not automate or directly manage contractual agreements or the operational processes for data handling on systems external to the VCF domain."
SA-5,"AST-04.1, TDA-04","System Documentation
a. Obtain or develop administrator documentation for the system, system component, or system service that describes:
1. Secure configuration, installation, and operation of the system, component, or service
2. Effective use and maintenance of security and privacy functions and mechanisms
3. Known vulnerabilities regarding configuration and use of administrative or privileged functions
b. Obtain or develop user documentation for the system, system component, or system service that describes:
1. User-accessible security and privacy functions and mechanisms and how to effectively use those functions and mechanisms
2. Methods for user interaction, which enables individuals to use the system, component, or service in a more secure manner and protect individual privacy
3. User responsibilities in maintaining the security of the system, component, or service and privacy of individuals

c. Document attempts to obtain system, system component, or system service documentation when such documentation is either unavailable or nonexistent and take [Assignment: organization-defined actions] in response

d. Distribute documentation to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles].",Contributes,"The VCF platform provides a highly standardized and automated infrastructure, ensuring consistent configurations across its components. Its inherent design and operational guides detail secure configuration, installation, and operational best practices for its core components, such as vSphere, vSAN, NSX, vCenter, and SDDC Manager. The platform offers APIs and reporting tools that facilitate the extraction of configuration data and operational parameters, which serve as foundational input for administrator documentation. Furthermore, VCF's native product documentation describes the security and privacy functions and mechanisms available within the platform for administrators and, where applicable, for users interacting directly with the VCF management plane.

The customer is responsible for developing comprehensive administrator documentation that specifically details their unique VCF deployment, including secure configuration, operational procedures, and known vulnerabilities relevant to their specific use cases and integrations. The customer must also develop user documentation tailored to their end-users, outlining user-accessible security and privacy functions, secure interaction methods, and user responsibilities within their specific applications and services hosted on VCF. Establishing and executing processes for documenting attempts to obtain unavailable documentation and defining subsequent actions is a customer policy and procedural requirement. The distribution of all system documentation to organization-defined personnel or roles is a customer operational responsibility, requiring defined policies and mechanisms outside the VCF platform's scope."
SA-8,"CFG-02, SEA-01","Security and Privacy Engineering Principles

Apply the following systems security and privacy engineering principles in the specification, design, development, implementation, and modification of the system and system components: [Assignment: organization-defined systems security and privacy engineering principles].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation is engineered with robust security and privacy principles embedded in its architecture and operational model. The platform provides a secure foundation through its hardened components, secure defaults, and automated lifecycle management, ensuring that systems and components are implemented and modified in a secure manner. It enforces secure configurations across compute, storage, and networking layers, aligning with principles such as least privilege, defense-in-depth, and data isolation. The inherent design of VMware Cloud Foundation, including its micro-segmentation capabilities via NSX and encryption options for data at rest and in transit, directly supports the implementation of secure and private systems.

The organization is responsible for formally defining its specific security and privacy engineering principles. While VMware Cloud Foundation provides the technical mechanisms to implement systems in accordance with many common principles, the overarching governance, policy, and processes for applying these defined principles during the specification, design, and development phases of applications and services deployed on the platform remain an organizational responsibility. This includes ensuring that application architects and developers adhere to these principles throughout their system's lifecycle."
SA-8(14),IAC-21,"Security and Privacy Engineering Principles | Least Privilege

Implement the security design principle of least privilege in [Assignment: organization-defined systems or system components].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation implements robust access control mechanisms that are fundamental to enforcing the principle of least privilege. Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) is natively supported across the VCF management domain and workload domains, allowing for the creation of granular roles and the assignment of specific permissions to users and groups. This ensures that administrative and operational personnel only possess the minimum necessary access to perform their assigned duties within vCenter Server, NSX-T Data Center, vSAN, and Aria Suite components. Furthermore, NSX-T Data Center provides granular network micro-segmentation capabilities, enabling the isolation of workloads and the application of security policies that restrict network communication to only what is explicitly required, thereby enforcing least privilege at the network layer. Integration with enterprise identity providers ensures consistent application of access policies across the environment.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific systems and components where least privilege must be applied, as well as establishing the detailed access policies, creating and managing specific roles, and assigning users to these roles based on their job functions. Ongoing review and auditing of access rights to ensure adherence to the principle of least privilege also fall within the customer's operational purview."
SA-8(24),SEA-07.2,"Security and Privacy Engineering Principles | Secure Failure and Recovery

Implement the security design principle of secure failure and recovery in [Assignment: organization-defined systems or system components].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) is architected with intrinsic capabilities that directly embody the security design principle of secure failure and recovery. The foundational vSphere, vSAN, and NSX-T components deliver a highly resilient and available infrastructure. vSphere High Availability (HA) and Distributed Resource Scheduler (DRS) ensure the automatic restart and optimal placement of workloads following host failures, maintaining service continuity. Concurrently, vSAN's distributed storage architecture provides robust data redundancy and integrity across multiple nodes, safeguarding against data loss during component outages. NSX-T further enhances resilience through its distributed network services, enabling secure isolation and controlled recovery pathways. These integrated platform elements are inherently designed to fail securely and facilitate robust recovery operations.

The organization retains responsibility for defining and implementing this security design principle within its specific applications and system components. This encompasses designing application architectures to effectively leverage VCF's inherent resilience features, establishing comprehensive disaster recovery plans, defining precise recovery time objectives (RTO) and recovery point objectives (RPO), and implementing secure backup and restoration procedures tailored to workloads operating on the platform. Furthermore, the organization must develop and enforce policies and processes that govern the integration of secure failure and recovery throughout the lifecycle of its defined systems."
SA-8(30),"PRM-07, SEA-07.1","Security and Privacy Engineering Principles | Procedural Rigor

Implement the security design principle of procedural rigor in [Assignment: organization-defined systems or system components].",Contributes,"The VCF platform inherently enforces procedural rigor through its validated design, standardized architecture, and automated lifecycle management. Its foundational components are deployed and maintained with a high degree of consistency and repeatability, ensuring that security configurations and operational processes adhere to a predefined, disciplined methodology.

Automated patching, upgrades, and configuration management across compute, storage, and networking layers significantly reduce the potential for human error and configuration drift, thereby embodying the principle of procedural rigor in its own operation and maintenance.

Defining the specific security design principles, establishing the overarching organizational policies and procedures for security engineering, and applying these principles to organization-defined systems and system components remain the responsibility of the customer. The platform provides the robust, consistent infrastructure, but the organization must integrate this rigor into its broader security engineering lifecycle and governance framework."
SA-8(31),"CHG-02, CHG-02.2, CHG-06","Security and Privacy Engineering Principles | Secure System Modification

Implement the security design principle of secure system modification in [Assignment: organization-defined systems or system components].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation inherently supports the principle of secure system modification through its architecture and operational model. The platform enforces a validated state for its core components, including ESXi, vCenter, NSX, and vSAN, ensuring that all updates and upgrades are applied through a controlled, automated lifecycle management process orchestrated by SDDC Manager. This design prevents unauthorized or insecure modifications to the underlying infrastructure, maintaining a consistent and secure configuration baseline. Centralized management interfaces for vSphere, NSX, and vSAN incorporate robust role-based access controls and comprehensive auditing capabilities, providing a secure framework for managing changes to the platform's configuration.

The customer is responsible for defining and implementing the overarching security design principle of secure system modification across all organization-defined systems and system components, including workloads and applications deployed on the platform. This requires establishing formal change management and configuration management processes, defining authorization requirements for all system modifications, and ensuring these processes are consistently applied and enforced throughout their operational environment."
SA-8(32),OPS-01.1,"Security and Privacy Engineering Principles | Sufficient Documentation

Implement the security design principle of sufficient documentation in [Assignment: organization-defined systems or system components].",Contributes,"The VCF platform is engineered with comprehensive, inherent documentation for its architecture, deployment, configuration, and operational procedures. This includes detailed design guides, API specifications, and a robust logging and auditing framework that captures system state and changes, all of which are critical inputs for an organization's documentation strategy. The platform's operational procedures are thoroughly documented, aligning with the need for structured information regarding system security design and implementation.

Implementing the security design principle of sufficient documentation, however, remains an organizational responsibility. The customer must define what constitutes ""sufficient documentation"" for their specific security designs, create these documents, and maintain them across their organization-defined systems and system components. This includes documenting their specific VCF configurations, security policies, operational procedures, and how the VCF environment integrates into their broader security architecture and compliance framework."
SA-8(33),"DCH-18.2, END-13.3, PES-06.5","Security and Privacy Engineering Principles | Minimization

Implement the privacy principle of minimization using [Assignment: organization-defined processes].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides a robust, secure, and auditable platform that supports the implementation of data minimization principles. Its integrated compute, storage, and networking components offer capabilities such as data encryption at rest and in transit, granular role-based access controls (RBAC), and comprehensive logging. These features enable organizations to secure data, restrict access to only authorized personnel, and monitor data handling activities, thereby creating an environment conducive to data minimization. The platform's secure storage solutions and network segmentation capabilities further assist in isolating and protecting data according to defined retention and access policies.

While VMware Cloud Foundation delivers the foundational technical controls, the organization is responsible for defining and implementing the specific ""organization-defined processes"" for data minimization. This includes establishing policies for data collection, use, retention, and disposal, as well as configuring applications and services to adhere to these policies. The customer must also ensure that data architects and application developers integrate privacy-by-design principles, leveraging VMware Cloud Foundation's capabilities to enforce their organizational data minimization strategies."
SA-9,TPM-04,"External System Services
a. Require that providers of external system services comply with organizational security and privacy requirements and employ the following controls: [Assignment: organization-defined controls]
b. Define and document organizational oversight and user roles and responsibilities with regard to external system services

c. Employ the following processes, methods, and techniques to monitor control compliance by external service providers on an ongoing basis: [Assignment: organization-defined processes, methods, and techniques].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides a highly secure and compliant infrastructure foundation, incorporating intrinsic security controls such as network segmentation, identity and access management, and comprehensive logging capabilities. These foundational controls ensure the integrity and confidentiality of data and operations within the VCF environment, establishing a secure platform upon which external services can be integrated or consumed.

VCF's robust monitoring and logging features generate detailed audit trails and operational insights for all components within its domain, which can be leveraged as part of a broader security and compliance program.

The organization is responsible for defining and enforcing contractual agreements with external system service providers, ensuring these providers adhere to specific organizational security and privacy requirements. This includes establishing the organization-defined controls that external providers must employ, documenting organizational oversight, and clearly defining user roles and responsibilities related to these external services. Furthermore, the organization must implement its own processes, methods, and techniques for continuously monitoring the compliance of external service providers against established requirements.
VCF does not manage third-party contracts, define external organizational policies, or directly monitor the security posture of external service providers in their own environments."
SA-9(1),TPM-04.1,"External System Services | Risk Assessments and Organizational Approvals
(a) Conduct an organizational assessment of risk prior to the acquisition or outsourcing of information security services

(b) Verify that the acquisition or outsourcing of dedicated information security services is approved by [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles].",Not Applicable,"End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope."
SA-9(2),TPM-04.2,"External System Services | Identification of Functions, Ports, Protocols, and Services

Require providers of the following external system services to identify the functions, ports, protocols, and other services required for the use of such services: [Assignment: organization-defined external system services].",Not Applicable,"End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope."
SA-9(3),"RSK-09, TPM-02, TPM-03, TPM-05.4, TPM-05.7","External System Services | Establish and Maintain Trust Relationship with Providers

Establish, document, and maintain trust relationships with external service providers based on the following requirements, properties, factors, or conditions: [Assignment: organization-defined security and privacy requirements, properties, factors, or conditions defining acceptable trust relationships].",Contributes,"VCF provides a robust and secure platform that supports the technical aspects of interacting with external service providers. Its inherent security features, such as network segmentation, identity and access management, and secure API gateways, enable the enforcement of defined trust parameters. The platform ensures that communications and data exchanges with external entities adhere to established security postures, providing a foundational layer for secure external system services.

The establishment, documentation, and ongoing maintenance of trust relationships, including the definition of specific security and privacy requirements, properties, factors, or conditions, remain the direct responsibility of the customer. The customer must define the acceptable trust framework and implement the necessary policies and procedures to govern these relationships.
VCF provides the technical environment to support the enforcement of these customer-defined trust parameters, but it does not define, establish, or document the trust relationships themselves."
SA-9(4),TPM-04.3,"External System Services | Consistent Interests of Consumers and Providers

Take the following actions to verify that the interests of [Assignment: organization-defined external service providers] are consistent with and reflect organizational interests: [Assignment: organization-defined actions].",Not Applicable,"End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope."
SA-9(5),"CLD-09, DCH-19, TPM-04.4","External System Services | Processing, Storage, and Service Location
Restrict the location of [Selection (one or more): information processing
column3: system services] to [Assignment: organization-defined locations] based on [Assignment: organization-defined requirements or conditions].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides a robust platform for defining and enforcing the physical and logical location of information processing, information, data, and system services. The VCF SDDC architecture ensures that all deployed workloads, including virtual machines and containers, and their associated storage via vSAN, are confined to the specific VCF instance where they are provisioned. This applies whether the VCF environment is deployed on-premises within a customer's data center or within a designated public cloud region as part of a hybrid cloud strategy, thereby restricting their location to organization-defined boundaries. Network segmentation capabilities within NSX-T further enable logical isolation and control over data flow, reinforcing location-based restrictions.

column1: Organizations must define the specific locations, requirements, and conditions for data residency, sovereignty, and service deployment. The customer is responsible for selecting the appropriate VCF deployment model and configuring the environment to align with these established organizational policies and regulatory mandates."
SA-9(6),CRY-09.7,"External System Services | Organization-controlled Cryptographic Keys

Maintain exclusive control of cryptographic keys for encrypted material stored or transmitted through an external system.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation integrates with external Key Management Systems (KMS) to manage cryptographic keys for encrypted data, such as vSAN encryption and VM encryption.

This integration leverages industry-standard protocols like KMIP, allowing the platform to request and utilize keys from a customer-managed KMS. The platform itself does not generate or store the master cryptographic keys, ensuring that key custody resides with the organization's designated KMS.
The organization is responsible for the selection, deployment, configuration, and operational management of the external Key Management System.

This includes establishing and enforcing policies and procedures to ensure exclusive control over the cryptographic keys, preventing unauthorized access or compromise, and maintaining the integrity and availability of the KMS infrastructure."
SA-9(8),"CLD-09, DCH-19","External System Services | Processing and Storage Location — U.s. Jurisdiction

Restrict the geographic location of information processing and data storage to facilities located within in the legal jurisdictional boundary of the United States.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides a comprehensive, software-defined infrastructure stack for compute, storage, and networking, which is deployed on customer-owned hardware within their designated physical data center facilities. When a VMware Cloud Foundation instance is deployed within a data center located inside the legal jurisdictional boundaries of the United States, all information processing and data storage operations performed by workloads hosted on that VCF instance will inherently occur within the US jurisdiction.

The customer is solely responsible for the physical placement of their VMware Cloud Foundation deployment, ensuring that the chosen data center facilities and any integrated external system services or data storage solutions adhere to the specified US jurisdictional requirements. The platform itself does not enforce geographical deployment restrictions."
SA-10,TDA-14,"Developer Configuration Management
Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to:
a. Perform configuration management during system, component, or service [Selection (one or more): design
b. Document, manage, and control the integrity of changes to [Assignment: organization-defined configuration items under configuration management]
c. Implement only organization-approved changes to the system, component, or service
d. Document approved changes to the system, component, or service and the potential security and privacy impacts of such changes

e. Track security flaws and flaw resolution within the system, component, or service and report findings to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel].",Contributes,"The VCF platform inherently performs configuration management across its lifecycle, encompassing design, development, implementation, and operation of the underlying infrastructure. It maintains strict version control and integrity for all changes applied to its configuration items, including hosts, networks, and storage policies. All modifications to the VCF environment are subject to robust change management processes, ensuring only approved changes are implemented through integrated workflows and role-based access controls. The platform provides comprehensive audit trails for all infrastructure changes, detailing who made the change and when. Furthermore, VCF incorporates continuous vulnerability management, tracking security flaws within its components and facilitating their resolution through automated patching and updates, with findings reported through integrated monitoring and alerting systems.

While VCF provides the secure and managed infrastructure for development and operations, the customer is responsible for defining and enforcing configuration management policies for their specific applications and services running on the platform. This includes establishing organization-defined configuration items, documenting the security and privacy impacts of application-level changes, and defining the personnel responsible for receiving security flaw reports related to their developed systems. The customer must also integrate their developer-specific change approval workflows and defect tracking systems with the underlying VCF platform to ensure end-to-end compliance for their applications."
SA-10(1),TDA-14.1,"Developer Configuration Management | Software and Firmware Integrity Verification

Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to enable integrity verification of software and firmware components.",Contributes,"The VCF platform incorporates robust mechanisms to ensure the integrity of its software and firmware components. This includes cryptographic signing of all binaries, secure boot processes for hypervisors and virtual machines, and continuous validation during lifecycle management operations. The platform verifies the authenticity and integrity of its own operating systems, hypervisors, and management components before they are executed or updated, thereby enabling integrity verification for the core VCF system.

While VCF provides a secure foundation and inherent integrity for its own components, the control extends to developers of any system component or service operating within or integrating with the VCF environment. The customer is responsible for establishing and enforcing policies that require these developers to enable integrity verification for their specific software and firmware. This includes ensuring developers leverage available platform capabilities, adhere to secure coding practices, and implement appropriate cryptographic signing and verification processes for their custom or third-party components."
SA-10(3),TDA-14.2,"Developer Configuration Management | Hardware Integrity Verification

Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to enable integrity verification of hardware components.",Contributes,"The VCF platform provides robust capabilities for hardware integrity verification.

ESXi hosts support UEFI Secure Boot, which ensures the integrity of the boot chain by verifying digital signatures of firmware and boot components. Additionally, VCF integrates with Trusted Platform Module (TPM 2.0) for cryptographic attestation, allowing for secure measurement and verification of the host's boot state. These features enable the platform to leverage and report on the integrity of underlying hardware components.
While the platform provides the technical mechanisms to enable and monitor hardware integrity, the customer retains responsibility for several critical aspects.

This includes procuring hardware that supports these integrity features, ensuring they are enabled in the server BIOS/UEFI, configuring TPM attestation, and establishing organizational policies and procedures for monitoring integrity verification results and responding to any detected anomalies."
SA-10(7),TDA-02.7,"Developer Configuration Management | Security and Privacy Representatives

Require [Assignment: organization-defined security and privacy representatives] to be included in the [Assignment: organization-defined configuration change management and control process].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides a robust, automated platform for managing the configuration of its SDDC components, including ESXi, vCenter Server, NSX, and vSAN. The platform enforces desired state configuration, detects configuration drift, and facilitates automated remediation. All configuration changes are meticulously logged and auditable, providing a comprehensive record for review. VCF integrates with external change management systems through its API, enabling the incorporation of platform-level changes into broader organizational workflows and approval processes.

However, the definition and assignment of specific security and privacy representatives, along with their explicit inclusion and roles within the organization's overarching configuration change management and control process, remain a customer responsibility. VCF provides the technical foundation and auditability for configuration changes but does not define human roles, establish organizational policies, or dictate the specific procedural steps for human involvement in change approval and review."
SA-11,TDA-09,"Developer Testing and Evaluation
Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service, at all post-design stages of the system development life cycle, to:
a. Develop and implement a plan for ongoing security and privacy assessments
b. Perform [Selection (one or more): unit
column4: regression] testing/evaluation [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] at [Assignment: organization-defined depth and coverage]

column1: c. Produce evidence of the execution of the assessment plan and the results of the testing and evaluation
d. Implement a verifiable flaw remediation process

e. Correct flaws identified during testing and evaluation.",Contributes,"The platform provides robust capabilities to integrate security and privacy assessments throughout the post-design stages of the system development lifecycle. It supports the execution of various testing methodologies, including unit, integration, system, and regression testing, by offering native integrations with CI/CD pipelines and developer toolchains. Comprehensive logging and reporting mechanisms are available to capture evidence of test execution, assessment results, and identified flaws, facilitating traceability and auditability. Furthermore, the platform integrates with common defect tracking and remediation systems, enabling a streamlined process for managing and correcting identified vulnerabilities.

While the platform furnishes the technical infrastructure for testing and evidence generation, the customer is responsible for defining the specific plan for ongoing security and privacy assessments, including the organization-defined frequency, depth, and coverage for all required testing types. The customer must also establish and implement a verifiable flaw remediation process and ensure the timely correction of all identified flaws. These policy, process, and accountability aspects remain within the customer's operational domain."
SA-11(1),TDA-09.2,"Developer Testing and Evaluation | Static Code Analysis

Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to employ static code analysis tools to identify common flaws and document the results of the analysis.",Contributes,"VCF components are developed following a rigorous Secure Development Lifecycle (SDL) that incorporates static code analysis tools. This process systematically identifies common flaws and vulnerabilities within the VCF codebase, ensuring the platform's inherent security and integrity. The results of these analyses are documented internally as part of VMware's product development and assurance processes.

The direct requirement for employing static code analysis tools for customer-developed systems, system components, or system services, and the subsequent documentation of those analysis results, remains the customer's responsibility. VCF provides the secure infrastructure for hosting these applications but does not inherently provide or enforce the use of static code analysis tools for customer-specific application codebases."
SA-11(2),"TDA-06.2, TDA-15","Developer Testing and Evaluation | Threat Modeling and Vulnerability Analyses

Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to perform threat modeling and vulnerability analyses during development and the subsequent testing and evaluation of the system, component, or service that:

SA-11(5),"IAO-02.2, IAO-04, TDA-09, TDA-09.5, VPM-07","Developer Testing and Evaluation | Penetration Testing

SA-11(6),"TDA-09, VPM-01.1","Developer Testing and Evaluation | Attack Surface Reviews

Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to perform attack surface reviews.",Contributes,"The VCF platform provides robust capabilities for application security testing and vulnerability management, which are integral to understanding and assessing an attack surface.

The customer is responsible for establishing and enforcing policies that mandate developers perform attack surface reviews. SA-11(7),"TDA-09, VPM-01.1","Developer Testing and Evaluation | Verify Scope of Testing and Evaluation

SA-15(5),"CFG-02, SEA-01","Development Process, Standards, and Tools | Attack Surface Reduction

Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to reduce attack surfaces to [Assignment: organization-defined thresholds].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides a securely hardened and configured infrastructure, inherently reducing the attack surface of the underlying platform components. SA-15(12),DCH-18.2,"Development Process, Standards, and Tools | Minimize Personally Identifiable Information

Require the developer of the system or system component to minimize the use of personally identifiable information in development and test environments.",Contributes,"VCF provides the foundational infrastructure and management capabilities essential for establishing and operating secure development and test environments. The platform enables the creation of logically isolated environments, robust role-based access controls, and automated provisioning workflows that can be configured to integrate with external data masking or synthetic data generation services. VCF's underlying storage solutions offer encryption at rest, safeguarding any data that resides within these environments.

The customer is responsible for defining and enforcing organizational policies that mandate the minimization of personally identifiable information within development and test environments. This includes implementing specific data masking, anonymization, or synthetic data generation processes at the application or data layer, ensuring developer adherence to these standards, and establishing procedures for secure data handling and sanitization.
The platform does not inherently perform data minimization or enforce developer compliance with PII usage policies."

SA-15(13),TDA-02.14,Control not found in reference sheet.,Contributes,"The platform provides robust mechanisms for securing data in transit across its various components. Management plane communications are secured using TLS 1.2 or higher, ensuring confidentiality and integrity for administrative traffic. For workload data, NSX-T Data Center offers advanced network virtualization capabilities, including micro-segmentation and encryption for east-west traffic, which can be configured to protect sensitive data flows between virtual machines. Additionally, vMotion operations, which involve the live migration of virtual machines, leverage encryption to protect the VM state and memory contents during transit. These integrated security features establish a strong technical foundation for protecting data as it moves across the infrastructure.

The specific requirements for SA-15(13) are not defined within the platform's reference documentation. Therefore, the customer is responsible for establishing and documenting the precise policies, configurations, and operational procedures necessary to meet the unstated control intent, including identifying all data in transit requiring protection, specifying acceptable cryptographic algorithms and key strengths, and ensuring their consistent application across the environment."

].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides a robust, validated, and integrated stack where the lifecycle of core components such as ESXi, vCenter, NSX, and vSAN is meticulously managed. column3: reconfiguration] on [Assignment: organization-defined systems or system components] supporting mission essential services or functions to increase the trustworthiness in those systems or components.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation establishes a highly standardized and secure infrastructure foundation, inherently contributing to the trustworthiness of systems and components deployed upon it. SA-24,SEA-01.2,Control not found in reference sheet.,Contributes,"The VMware Cloud Foundation platform provides a standardized, validated, and integrated architecture that inherently streamlines the technical aspects of system and service acquisition. SC-2,SEA-03.2,"Separation of System and User Functionality

Separate user functionality, including user interface services, from system management functionality.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation inherently separates system management functionality from user functionality through its architectural design and integrated components. The platform establishes distinct management planes for vCenter Server, NSX-T Manager, and SDDC Manager, each with dedicated administrative interfaces and robust Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) mechanisms. This allows for granular assignment of permissions, ensuring that administrators responsible for the underlying infrastructure cannot access or modify user-facing applications without explicit authorization, and vice-versa.

Furthermore, NSX-T provides advanced network segmentation capabilities, enabling the logical and physical isolation of management networks from workload networks, and facilitating the creation of secure zones for user-facing services separate from backend system components.

Customers are responsible for defining and implementing specific RBAC policies, configuring network segmentation rules within NSX-T to enforce the desired separation, and designing their application deployments to leverage these capabilities. This includes ensuring that applications deployed on the platform maintain their own separation of user and administrative functions, aligning with the overall security posture established by the VCF infrastructure."
SC-2(1),SEA-03.2,"Separation of System and User Functionality | Interfaces for Non-privileged Users

Prevent the presentation of system management functionality at interfaces to non-privileged users.",Meets,"VMware Cloud Foundation inherently prevents the presentation of system management functionality at interfaces to non-privileged users.

The platform leverages a robust, granular Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) model across all its core components, including vCenter Server, NSX-T Manager, and vRealize Suite. User interfaces and APIs are designed to display only the functions and objects authorized for a user's assigned role. For instance, within the vSphere Client, a user assigned a 'VM User' role will only see their assigned virtual machines and permitted operations, while all underlying host, network, and storage configuration options are either hidden or rendered inaccessible. This architectural design ensures that system management interfaces are not presented to unauthorized personnel.

The customer is responsible for defining and assigning appropriate roles and permissions to users and groups, ensuring that non-privileged accounts are correctly configured to prevent access to administrative functions."
SC-3,"END-16, SEA-04.1","Security Function Isolation

Isolate security functions from nonsecurity functions.",Contributes,"The VMware Cloud Foundation platform inherently isolates security functions through its foundational architecture. The dedicated Management Domain segregates all core SDDC management components, including vCenter Server, NSX-T Manager, and SDDC Manager, onto their own compute, storage, and network resources, distinct from workload domains. This architectural separation ensures that critical security and operational control planes are isolated from general application workloads.

Furthermore, NSX-T Data Center provides robust network micro-segmentation capabilities, enabling the creation of granular firewall policies to logically isolate specific virtual machines, application tiers, or security appliances from non-security functions within and across workload domains. Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) is uniformly applied across all VCF components, ensuring that administrative access to security-critical functions is strictly controlled and isolated based on defined roles and responsibilities.

While the platform provides the necessary technical mechanisms for isolation, the customer is responsible for defining the specific scope of ""security functions"" within their unique operational context and for configuring the appropriate network segmentation rules, RBAC policies, and resource allocations to enforce this isolation. The implementation of these policies and the ongoing operational procedures to maintain and monitor the defined isolation boundaries remain a customer responsibility."
SC-3(5),SEA-03,"Security Function Isolation | Layered Structures

Implement security functions as a layered structure minimizing interactions between layers of the design and avoiding any dependence by lower layers on the functionality or correctness of higher layers.",Contributes,"VCF is architected with security functions implemented as a layered structure, inherently minimizing interactions between design layers and avoiding any dependence by lower layers on the functionality or correctness of higher layers.

The ESXi hypervisor, as a bare-metal Type-1 hypervisor, forms the foundational layer, isolating virtual machines and their security contexts from the underlying hardware and from each other. This design inherently minimizes interactions between the hypervisor and guest operating systems, ensuring that lower layers do not depend on the functionality or correctness of higher layers for their security integrity.

NSX-T Data Center further extends this by providing network virtualization and micro-segmentation, enabling the creation of logically isolated network layers and granular security policy enforcement. vSAN contributes with storage virtualization and encryption, adding another distinct security layer.

The overall VCF architecture, encompassing compute, network, and storage virtualization, is fundamentally designed with distinct, well-defined layers and interfaces, ensuring robust isolation and controlled interactions.

While VCF provides this secure, layered foundation and the tools to enforce isolation, customers are responsible for designing and implementing their specific application workloads and operational processes to align with these layered security principles. This includes configuring VCF's security features, such as NSX-T micro-segmentation and vSphere security settings, to enforce their defined layered security policies and ensuring their application architectures minimize inter-layer dependencies."
SC-4,SEA-05,"Information in Shared System Resources

Prevent unauthorized and unintended information transfer via shared system resources.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive capabilities to prevent unauthorized and unintended information transfer across shared system resources.

The platform leverages NSX-T for advanced network virtualization, enabling micro-segmentation and distributed firewall policies that enforce strict isolation between workloads and tenants, even when sharing underlying physical network infrastructure. This ensures that data flows only through explicitly authorized paths, preventing unauthorized lateral movement and unintended data leakage.

Furthermore, vSphere's robust virtualization layer provides strong isolation between virtual machines, while vSAN offers secure, object-based storage with encryption at rest and granular access controls, preventing unauthorized access to stored information.

Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) across the SDDC Manager and vCenter Server management planes further restricts administrative actions, ensuring that only authorized personnel can configure or modify shared resource access.

While the platform delivers the foundational technical mechanisms for isolation and secure data handling, the customer is responsible for defining and implementing specific security policies. This includes configuring NSX-T firewall rules, establishing appropriate vSphere RBAC roles, designing workload domains for logical separation, and defining storage policies that align with their data classification and security requirements.
The customer must also establish processes for ongoing monitoring and auditing of resource access and data flows to detect and respond to any potential unauthorized or unintended transfers."
SC-5,"CAP-01, CAP-02, CAP-03, NET-02.1","Denial-of-service Protection

Employ the following controls to achieve the denial-of-service objective: [Assignment: organization-defined controls by type of denial-of-service event].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides a robust platform with inherent capabilities to mitigate and limit the effects of various denial-of-service events.

The platform's networking components, particularly NSX-T, offer advanced features such as distributed firewalling, micro-segmentation, and traffic filtering, which are instrumental in identifying and blocking malicious traffic patterns indicative of DoS attacks. These capabilities enable the implementation of granular network policies, rate limiting, and anomaly detection to protect the virtualized infrastructure and hosted workloads from common DoS vectors, ensuring the availability and resilience of critical services.

The customer is responsible for defining the specific types of denial-of-service events relevant to their operational context and for establishing the corresponding controls to be employed. This includes developing and implementing organizational policies and procedures for denial-of-service protection, configuring specific network rules and thresholds within the VCF environment, and ensuring the ongoing monitoring and response to potential DoS threats."
SC-5(1),CAP-02,"Denial-of-service Protection | Restrict Ability to Attack Other Systems

Restrict the ability of individuals to launch the following denial-of-service attacks against other systems: [Assignment: organization-defined denial-of-service attacks].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust capabilities to restrict the ability of individuals from launching denial-of-service attacks against other systems. Granular role-based access control (RBAC) within vCenter Server and NSX-T Data Center ensures that user privileges are appropriately managed, limiting the ability to deploy or execute unauthorized software that could be used for DoS activities. The integrated NSX-T Data Center further enables comprehensive network segmentation and security, including distributed firewalling at the virtual machine level and gateway firewalling. These capabilities allow for the implementation of stringent egress filtering rules, preventing specific types of outbound traffic associated with defined DoS attacks from originating within the VCF-managed environment.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific denial-of-service attacks to be restricted and for establishing the corresponding security policies and procedures. This includes configuring the precise egress filtering rules within NSX-T Data Center to block identified attack vectors and ensuring that user roles and permissions are aligned with these restrictions."
SC-5(2),"CAP-02, CAP-03","Denial-of-service Protection | Capacity, Bandwidth, and Redundancy

Manage capacity, bandwidth, or other redundancy to limit the effects of information flooding denial-of-service attacks.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides a robust, scalable, and resilient infrastructure designed to manage compute, storage, and network resources effectively.

The platform inherently supports capacity management through its ability to scale resources dynamically, leveraging vSphere DRS for workload balancing and vSAN for distributed storage. Network redundancy is a core architectural principle, with NSX-T enabling resilient network designs, including multiple uplinks and ECMP routing, to ensure high availability and bandwidth. These capabilities are fundamental in building an environment that can withstand increased load and maintain service availability, thereby limiting the effects of information flooding denial-of-service attacks on the underlying infrastructure.

While the platform delivers the foundational capabilities for capacity, bandwidth, and redundancy, the customer is responsible for defining and implementing specific capacity planning strategies, configuring resource allocation policies, and establishing operational procedures to actively manage these resources in response to potential information flooding denial-of-service attacks. This includes monitoring network traffic patterns, implementing appropriate network security controls at the perimeter, and developing incident response plans that leverage the platform's inherent resilience to mitigate attack effects."
SC-5(3),CAP-01,"Denial-of-service Protection | Detection and Monitoring
(a) Employ the following monitoring tools to detect indicators of denial-of-service attacks against, or launched from, the system: [Assignment: organization-defined monitoring tools]

(b) Monitor the following system resources to determine if sufficient resources exist to prevent effective denial-of-service attacks: [Assignment: organization-defined system resources].",Contributes,"The platform provides the foundational infrastructure necessary for deploying and operating systems that require denial-of-service protection. This includes the underlying compute, storage, and networking resources that form the basis for system operations.

The platform's architecture supports the integration of various monitoring solutions designed to detect indicators of denial-of-service attacks, whether originating from or targeting the deployed systems.

The organization is responsible for defining and implementing the specific monitoring tools to be employed for DoS detection. Furthermore, the organization must identify the critical system resources to be monitored and establish the thresholds and processes necessary to ensure sufficient capacity exists to prevent effective denial-of-service attacks. This includes the configuration of monitoring solutions and the establishment of operational procedures for incident response."
SC-6,CAP-02,"Resource Availability
Protect the availability of resources by allocating [Assignment: organization-defined resources] by [Selection (one or more): priority
[Assignment: organization-defined controls]

].",Contributes,"The VCF platform provides robust capabilities for managing and allocating compute, memory, storage, and network resources to protect availability. vSphere Resource Pools enable the definition of shares, reservations, and limits for CPU and memory, directly implementing priority and quota-based resource allocation. Distributed Resource Scheduler (DRS) continuously monitors resource utilization and automatically balances workloads across the cluster to prevent contention and ensure resource availability. Storage QoS policies, configurable through vSAN and vVols, allow for the enforcement of performance and capacity quotas, while NSX-T provides network QoS mechanisms to prioritize critical traffic flows. Admission control features within vSphere HA and DRS prevent resource overcommitment, ensuring that sufficient resources are always available for critical workloads.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific resources that require protection, establishing the precise allocation policies (e.g., specific priority levels, quotas, reservations, or limits), and configuring these mechanisms within the VCF environment to align with their operational requirements and availability objectives. This includes defining the content of resource pools, setting DRS automation levels, and specifying storage and network QoS policies."
SC-7,NET-03,"Boundary Protection
a. Monitor and control communications at the external managed interfaces to the system and at key internal managed interfaces within the system
b. Implement subnetworks for publicly accessible system components that are [Selection: physically
column2: logically] separated from internal organizational networks

column1: c. Connect to external networks or systems only through managed interfaces consisting of boundary protection devices arranged in accordance with an organizational security and privacy architecture.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive capabilities for boundary protection through its integrated NSX-T Data Center networking and security services. The platform enables the monitoring and control of communications at both external and key internal managed interfaces. NSX-T's distributed firewall capabilities enforce granular traffic policies at the virtual machine level, serving as internal managed interfaces, while NSX Edge provides robust firewalling and routing services at the logical perimeter, acting as the external managed interface for the virtualized environment. VCF facilitates the implementation of logically separated subnetworks for publicly accessible system components, ensuring isolation from internal organizational networks through logical segments, VRFs, and dedicated edge services. Connections to external networks are exclusively routed through these managed NSX Edge interfaces, which incorporate essential boundary protection devices.

While VCF delivers the foundational technical mechanisms for boundary protection, the customer is responsible for defining the specific organizational security and privacy architecture that dictates traffic flow and access policies. This includes establishing the precise rules for monitoring and controlling communications, configuring the logical separation parameters, and ensuring the overall design aligns with their security posture. The customer must also implement the operational processes for continuous monitoring, alert generation, and incident response based on the telemetry provided by the platform."
SC-7(3),NET-03.1,"Boundary Protection | Access Points

Limit the number of external network connections to the system.",Contributes,"VCF, through its integrated NSX-T capabilities, provides robust network virtualization and security features that are fundamental to managing external network connections. The platform enables the creation of logical network segments and the enforcement of granular security policies via distributed and gateway firewalls. Administrators can define explicit ingress and egress rules, effectively controlling and limiting the number of external connections permitted to systems hosted within the VCF environment. This includes the ability to configure Tier-0 and Tier-1 gateways to act as controlled access points, allowing precise management of traffic flow at the boundary.

The customer is responsible for defining the specific security policies that dictate the acceptable number and nature of external connections. This includes designing the network architecture, configuring the appropriate firewall rules within NSX-T, and establishing the operational processes for managing and auditing these connections to ensure compliance with organizational requirements."
SC-7(4),NET-03.2,"Boundary Protection | External Telecommunications Services
(a) Implement a managed interface for each external telecommunication service
(b) Establish a traffic flow policy for each managed interface
(c) Protect the confidentiality and integrity of the information being transmitted across each interface
(d) Document each exception to the traffic flow policy with a supporting mission or business need and duration of that need
(e) Review exceptions to the traffic flow policy [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and remove exceptions that are no longer supported by an explicit mission or business need
(f) Prevent unauthorized exchange of control plane traffic with external networks
(g) Publish information to enable remote networks to detect unauthorized control plane traffic from internal networks

(h) Filter unauthorized control plane traffic from external networks.",Contributes,"The VCF platform, leveraging NSX-T, provides comprehensive capabilities for establishing and managing interfaces for external telecommunication services. It enables the definition and enforcement of granular traffic flow policies through distributed and gateway firewalls, ensuring precise control over network ingress and egress. Confidentiality and integrity of information transmitted across these interfaces can be protected through integrated security services, including IPsec VPNs for data in transit. Furthermore, the platform offers robust mechanisms to prevent the unauthorized exchange of control plane traffic with external networks and to filter such traffic originating from external sources. Logging and monitoring features within NSX-T facilitate the detection of unauthorized control plane activity, supporting the publication of relevant information to remote networks.

While the VCF platform provides the foundational technical controls, the organization is responsible for defining the specific traffic flow policies, establishing the frequency for reviewing exceptions, and documenting all exceptions with supporting mission or business needs and their durations. The customer must also implement the processes for regularly reviewing and removing exceptions that are no longer justified, and for integrating VCF's logging capabilities with broader security information and event management (SIEM) systems to effectively publish and consume detection information."
SC-7(5),NET-04.1,"Boundary Protection | Deny by Default — Allow by Exception
Deny network communications traffic by default and allow network communications traffic by exception [Selection (one or more): at managed interfaces

].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation, leveraging NSX-T Data Center, provides robust capabilities for implementing network boundary protection with a ""deny by default, allow by exception"" posture. The Distributed Firewall (DFW) and Gateway Firewall (GFW) components enable granular control over network communications traffic at both the virtual machine vNIC level and across logical gateways. This allows for the precise definition and enforcement of security policies, ensuring that only explicitly permitted traffic flows are allowed at all managed interfaces within the VCF environment.

The customer is responsible for defining the specific organization-defined systems that require this boundary protection and for establishing the precise exceptions to the ""deny by default"" rule. This includes configuring the detailed firewall rules and security groups within NSX-T Data Center to reflect the organization's approved communication requirements and maintaining the associated policies and procedures for managing these exceptions."
SC-7(7),CFG-03.4,"Boundary Protection | Split Tunneling for Remote Devices

Prevent split tunneling for remote devices connecting to organizational systems unless the split tunnel is securely provisioned using [Assignment: organization-defined safeguards].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation, through its integrated NSX-T network virtualization and security platform, establishes a robust and secure network boundary for organizational systems. NSX-T provides advanced distributed firewalling, micro-segmentation, and network policy enforcement capabilities, which are critical for securing all traffic flows, including those originating from remote devices. This infrastructure enables the definition and enforcement of granular access controls and network paths, ensuring that any traffic entering the VCF-managed environment adheres to established security policies. The platform provides the necessary technical controls to implement and enforce safeguards for securely provisioned split tunnels, allowing for precise control over which network resources remote devices can access.

The prevention of split tunneling on remote devices, the configuration of external VPN concentrators, and the definition of the ""organization-defined safeguards"" for securely provisioned split tunnels are responsibilities that reside with the customer. This includes establishing the overarching policy, configuring client-side VPN software, and defining the specific network access rules and monitoring requirements that govern remote device connectivity and split tunneling behavior before traffic reaches the VCF boundary."
SC-7(8),"NET-18, NET-18.1","Boundary Protection | Route Traffic to Authenticated Proxy Servers

Route [Assignment: organization-defined internal communications traffic] to [Assignment: organization-defined external networks] through authenticated proxy servers at managed interfaces.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation, leveraging NSX-T, establishes robust network segmentation and boundary protection capabilities. It provides the necessary infrastructure to define internal communication traffic flows and external network destinations. Through advanced routing and policy enforcement mechanisms, VCF enables the redirection of specified internal traffic to external networks via designated security services. The platform manages the underlying network interfaces where these traffic routing and enforcement policies are applied, ensuring controlled egress.

The customer is responsible for defining the specific internal communications traffic and external networks subject to this control. Furthermore, the deployment, configuration, and ongoing management of the authenticated proxy servers, including their authentication mechanisms, fall outside the scope of the VCF platform. The customer must also implement the precise network policies within NSX-T to ensure all defined traffic is consistently routed through these customer-provided proxy servers."
SC-7(9),"NET-03, NET-03.2","Boundary Protection | Restrict Threatening Outgoing Communications Traffic
(a) Detect and deny outgoing communications traffic posing a threat to external systems

(b) Audit the identity of internal users associated with denied communications.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust boundary protection through NSX-T/NSX+, enabling the detection and denial of outgoing communications traffic. The NSX-T/NSX+ distributed and gateway firewalls are configured to inspect outbound traffic flows, applying security policies to restrict unauthorized or malicious communications. When integrated with NSX Advanced Threat Prevention (ATP) or Network Detection and Response (NDR) capabilities, the platform can identify and block sophisticated threats such as malware command-and-control, data exfiltration attempts, and other indicators of compromise that pose a risk to external systems. All denied communications are logged, capturing essential metadata for auditing purposes.

While VCF provides the foundational network security and threat detection mechanisms, the customer is responsible for defining the specific criteria for ""threatening"" outgoing communications and configuring the corresponding NSX-T/NSX+ firewall rules and ATP/NDR policies. Establishing the integration with identity sources, such as Active Directory, is necessary to associate denied communications with specific internal users. Furthermore, the customer must implement a comprehensive log management solution, such as VMware Aria Operations for Logs or an external SIEM, to centralize, retain, and analyze these audit logs, ensuring that the identity of internal users associated with denied communications can be effectively audited and investigated."
SC-7(10),"NET-03.5, NET-17","Boundary Protection | Prevent Exfiltration
(a) Prevent the exfiltration of information

(b) Conduct exfiltration tests [Assignment: organization-defined frequency].",Contributes,"The VMware Cloud Foundation platform, through its integrated NSX network virtualization and security capabilities, provides robust mechanisms to prevent the exfiltration of information.

Distributed firewalls and micro-segmentation enforce granular network policies, isolating workloads and restricting unauthorized outbound communication paths. Egress filtering capabilities allow for precise control over traffic leaving the environment, blocking known malicious destinations and preventing the unauthorized transfer of data. The platform's inherent network security architecture establishes a strong technical foundation for boundary protection against data exfiltration.

The organization remains responsible for defining comprehensive data exfiltration prevention policies, integrating and managing any necessary data loss prevention (DLP) solutions or advanced threat detection systems within the VCF environment, and establishing the specific frequency and methodology for conducting exfiltration tests. The operational execution and reporting of these tests, along with the subsequent remediation of any identified vulnerabilities, are also direct organizational responsibilities."
SC-7(11),"NET-03, NET-04.1","Boundary Protection | Restrict Incoming Communications Traffic

Only allow incoming communications from [Assignment: organization-defined authorized sources] to be routed to [Assignment: organization-defined authorized destinations].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation, leveraging NSX-T, provides robust network segmentation and distributed firewall capabilities essential for restricting incoming communications traffic. The platform enables the creation of granular security policies and firewall rules that precisely control traffic flow, allowing for the explicit definition of authorized sources and destinations. These capabilities ensure that only incoming communications originating from specified sources are routed to designated internal endpoints, with all other traffic implicitly denied.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific authorized sources and destinations for incoming communications based on its security policies and operational requirements. This includes designing, implementing, and maintaining the appropriate firewall rules and security groups within NSX-T to enforce these definitions. Ongoing management and auditing of these network security configurations are also required to ensure continued compliance."
SC-7(12),END-16.1,"Boundary Protection | Host-based Protection

Implement [Assignment: organization-defined host-based boundary protection mechanisms] at [Assignment: organization-defined system components].",Contributes,"The VCF platform provides robust capabilities for implementing host-based boundary protection mechanisms through the NSX Distributed Firewall.

This functionality operates at the virtual network interface card (vNIC) level of each virtual machine, effectively placing a granular firewall directly at the boundary of individual system components. This enables the enforcement of micro-segmentation policies, allowing for precise control over network traffic flows to and from workloads and preventing unauthorized lateral movement within the environment.
