While the platform delivers the technical mechanisms for host-based boundary protection, the organization is responsible for defining the specific security policies, firewall rules, and security groups that constitute these protection mechanisms. Furthermore, the organization must identify and designate the specific system components to which these host-based protections will be applied, ensuring alignment with their overall security architecture and compliance requirements."
SC-7(13),NET-06.1,"Boundary Protection | Isolation of Security Tools, Mechanisms, and Support Components

Isolate [Assignment: organization-defined information security tools, mechanisms, and support components] from other internal system components by implementing physically separate subnetworks with managed interfaces to other components of the system.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation, through its integrated NSX-T capabilities, provides robust network virtualization and segmentation features essential for isolating security tools, mechanisms, and support components. The platform's architecture establishes a dedicated Management Domain, inherently isolating core infrastructure management components.

NSX-T enables the creation of multiple logical segments (subnetworks) that can be mapped to distinct physical network segments, facilitating the implementation of physically separate subnetworks. Distributed and gateway firewalls within NSX-T enforce granular access controls and establish managed interfaces between these isolated segments and other system components, ensuring secure communication and boundary protection.

The customer is responsible for identifying the specific information security tools, mechanisms, and support components that require isolation. This includes designing and implementing the underlying physical network infrastructure to support the required physically separate subnetworks and ensuring the correct mapping of VCF's logical segments to these physical constructs.
Furthermore, the customer must define and configure the specific firewall rules and access policies within NSX-T to establish the managed interfaces and enforce the desired isolation boundaries."
SC-7(14),"PES-03.2, PES-12, PES-12.1","Boundary Protection | Protect Against Unauthorized Physical Connections
Protect against unauthorized physical connections at [Assignment: organization-defined managed interfaces].",Not Applicable,"End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope."
SC-7(15),NET-18.3,"Boundary Protection | Networked Privileged Accesses

Route networked, privileged accesses through a dedicated, managed interface for purposes of access control and auditing.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation establishes a secure foundation for privileged access by segmenting management networks and providing robust authentication and authorization mechanisms for its own components. NSX-T Data Center enables the creation of dedicated network segments and micro-segmentation policies to isolate privileged access pathways, ensuring that only authorized traffic reaches critical management interfaces.
The platform's comprehensive logging and auditing capabilities capture all administrative actions, providing the necessary data for compliance verification.

The deployment and ongoing management of the specific dedicated interface (e.g., a bastion host, jump server, or privileged access management solution) through which all networked privileged accesses are routed remains a customer responsibility. This includes defining the access control policies for that interface and integrating it with the broader security architecture."
SC-7(16),NET-03.3,"Boundary Protection | Prevent Discovery of System Components

Prevent the discovery of specific system components that represent a managed interface.",Contributes,"VCF provides robust capabilities to prevent the discovery of specific system components and their managed interfaces through advanced network segmentation and distributed firewalling. The NSX platform, integral to VCF, enables granular micro-segmentation, isolating workloads and infrastructure components into distinct security zones. This isolation ensures that only authorized network segments can communicate with or even detect the presence of specific interfaces. The distributed firewall enforces strict ingress and egress rules at the virtual machine and container level, effectively blocking unauthorized network probes, port scans, and connection attempts that could lead to component discovery. Furthermore, VCF's management plane components, such as vCenter Server and NSX Manager, incorporate robust API access controls, limiting programmatic discovery of underlying infrastructure details to authenticated and authorized entities.

While VCF furnishes the technical controls, the customer is responsible for defining the specific system components and managed interfaces that require protection from discovery. This includes establishing comprehensive security policies that dictate which network flows are permitted and which are explicitly denied, thereby preventing unauthorized reconnaissance. The customer must also configure the NSX distributed firewall rules and micro-segmentation policies to align with these defined security requirements, ensuring that the technical implementation effectively prevents the intended discovery."
SC-7(17),WEB-03,"Boundary Protection | Automated Enforcement of Protocol Formats

Enforce adherence to protocol formats.",Contributes,"VCF establishes a robust, software-defined infrastructure that supports the deployment of advanced security services. The NSX-T Distributed Firewall provides essential network segmentation and L3/L4 policy enforcement, creating a secure operational environment for applications. This foundational networking capability ensures that all network traffic can be securely routed and subjected to further inspection by specialized security solutions.

Direct automated enforcement of protocol formats, including validation of specific header structures or API payload schemas, is not an intrinsic function of the core VCF platform. Achieving this level of granular protocol adherence requires the integration and configuration of dedicated security appliances, such as Web Application Firewalls (WAFs) or API gateways, within the VCF environment. The customer is responsible for the selection, deployment, configuration, and ongoing management of these third-party security services to meet specific protocol format enforcement requirements."
SC-7(18),SEA-01,"Boundary Protection | Fail Secure

Prevent systems from entering unsecure states in the event of an operational failure of a boundary protection device.",Contributes,"VCF implements fail-secure mechanisms for boundary protection through its integrated network virtualization and security platform, NSX-T. The NSX-T Distributed Firewall (DFW) operates at the kernel level of each ESXi host, ensuring that configured network segmentation and access control policies persist and continue to be enforced even in the event of an operational failure of NSX management components. This inherent design prevents systems from transitioning into an unsecure, open state by maintaining the established security posture. Furthermore, critical boundary protection services, such as those provided by NSX-T Edge nodes, are deployed with high availability configurations, ensuring continuous operation and failover to a standby instance should an active device experience an operational failure.

While the platform provides these robust fail-secure capabilities, the customer is responsible for defining the specific fail-secure policies that align with their organizational security requirements. This includes the precise configuration of DFW rules, the establishment of appropriate high availability settings for virtualized security appliances, and the implementation of comprehensive monitoring and testing procedures to validate the effectiveness of the fail-secure posture."
SC-7(19),AST-02.5,"Boundary Protection | Block Communication from Non-organizationally Configured Hosts

Block inbound and outbound communications traffic between [Assignment: organization-defined communication clients] that are independently configured by end users and external service providers.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation, through its integrated NSX-T Data Center component, provides robust capabilities to enforce network boundary protection. NSX-T Distributed Firewall (DFW) can precisely block inbound and outbound communication traffic at the virtual machine vNIC level or at the edge gateway. Security policies can be defined to create security groups based on various attributes, including VM tags, IP addresses, or other contextual information. This allows for the granular enforcement of rules to prevent communication with specific clients or hosts that are identified as non-compliant or independently configured.

The organization is responsible for defining what constitutes an ""organization-defined communication client"" and establishing the criteria for identifying hosts that are independently configured by end users or external service providers. This includes developing policies, implementing asset management processes, and potentially integrating with external systems (e.g., CMDB, network access control solutions) to classify and tag hosts. Once these classifications are established, NSX-T can then apply the necessary distributed firewall rules to block traffic as required by the organizational policy."
SC-7(20),NET-03.6,"Boundary Protection | Dynamic Isolation and Segregation

Provide the capability to dynamically isolate [Assignment: organization-defined system components] from other system components.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation, through its integrated NSX networking and security services, provides robust capabilities for the dynamic isolation and segregation of system components.

The NSX Distributed Firewall enables granular micro-segmentation, allowing for precise control over network traffic between virtual machines and workloads, even when residing on the same host or within the same logical network. Security groups, combined with dynamic membership criteria and tagging, facilitate the automated application and enforcement of isolation policies, ensuring that workloads are dynamically segmented based on their attributes, operational role, or security posture.
This capability allows for real-time adaptation of network boundaries and security controls without manual network reconfigurations.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific system components that require dynamic isolation and segregation. This includes establishing the security policies, configuring the appropriate NSX Distributed Firewall rules, and implementing the tagging and security group assignments to enforce the desired isolation boundaries."
SC-7(21),NET-03.7,"Boundary Protection | Isolation of System Components

Employ boundary protection mechanisms to isolate [Assignment: organization-defined system components] supporting [Assignment: organization-defined missions and/or business functions].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation, leveraging its integrated NSX-T capabilities, provides robust network virtualization and security features essential for implementing boundary protection and isolating system components.

The platform enables the creation of logical segments, distributed firewalls, and gateway firewalls, facilitating granular micro-segmentation down to the individual workload level. Security policies can be applied to groups of virtual machines, logical networks, or specific applications, effectively establishing defined boundaries that restrict communication flows and isolate critical assets within the software-defined data center.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific system components that require isolation and identifying the missions or business functions they support. This includes establishing the security policies and rulesets within NSX-T to enforce the defined boundaries and ensure proper isolation according to organizational requirements."
SC-7(22),NET-03.8,"Boundary Protection | Separate Subnets for Connecting to Different Security Domains

Implement separate network addresses to connect to systems in different security domains.",Meets,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF), leveraging NSX-T Data Center, provides comprehensive network virtualization capabilities that directly support the implementation of separate network addresses. The platform enables the creation and management of distinct logical segments (subnets) that can be isolated and routed independently. This inherent capability allows for the precise establishment of separate network infrastructure to connect to different security domains, ensuring logical separation and boundary protection within the software-defined data center.

The customer is responsible for defining the specific security domains and their associated requirements. This includes designing the network topology, configuring the NSX-T logical segments, gateways, and firewall policies to enforce the desired separation, and ensuring that systems are appropriately assigned to their respective security domain subnets."
SC-7(24),NET-03.4,"Boundary Protection | Personally Identifiable Information
For systems that process personally identifiable information:
(a) Apply the following processing rules to data elements of personally identifiable information: [Assignment: organization-defined processing rules]
(b) Monitor for permitted processing at the external interfaces to the system and at key internal boundaries within the system
(c) Document each processing exception

(d) Review and remove exceptions that are no longer supported.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation, through NSX-T, provides robust capabilities for enforcing and monitoring processing rules for personally identifiable information (PII) at both external interfaces and key internal boundaries. The NSX Distributed Firewall (DFW) enables granular micro-segmentation, allowing for the precise definition and enforcement of data flow rules at the virtual machine network interface level, thereby securing internal system boundaries. The NSX Gateway Firewall protects external interfaces, controlling north-south traffic. All network traffic and security events are logged and can be exported to external Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) systems, facilitating continuous monitoring for permitted processing and identifying any deviations from established rules.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific processing rules for PII data elements, which are then implemented and enforced through NSX-T policies. Additionally, the organization must establish and maintain formal processes for documenting any processing exceptions and for regularly reviewing and removing exceptions that are no longer supported or valid."
SC-7(25),NET-05,"Boundary Protection | Unclassified National Security System Connections

Prohibit the direct connection of [Assignment: organization-defined unclassified national security system] to an external network without the use of [Assignment: organization-defined boundary protection device].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation, leveraging its integrated NSX-T software-defined networking capabilities, provides robust mechanisms for establishing and enforcing network boundary protection.

The platform enables the creation of logical network segments, security groups, and the deployment of NSX Edge appliances, which serve as highly capable boundary protection devices. Distributed and gateway firewalls within NSX-T allow for granular control over network traffic, ensuring that specific systems or network enclaves are isolated and their connections to external networks are strictly mediated. This architecture facilitates the enforcement of policies that prohibit direct connections to external networks without traversing defined security controls.

The organization is responsible for defining what constitutes an unclassified national security system and specifying the required boundary protection devices and configurations within the NSX-T environment. This includes designing the network topology, configuring firewall rules, routing policies, and deploying NSX Edge services to meet specific security requirements.

Furthermore, the organization must establish and maintain policies, procedures, and operational processes to govern the implementation, monitoring, and auditing of these boundary protections to ensure ongoing compliance."
SC-7(26),NET-05,"Boundary Protection | Classified National Security System Connections

Prohibit the direct connection of a classified national security system to an external network without the use of [Assignment: organization-defined boundary protection device].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides the foundational infrastructure and advanced networking capabilities necessary to implement robust boundary protection. Through NSX-T Data Center, VCF delivers a comprehensive software-defined networking and security stack. This includes distributed firewall (DFW) and gateway firewall (GFW) functionalities, which are critical for enforcing network segmentation and controlling traffic flow between different security domains, including those connecting to external networks. The platform's architecture supports the deployment and integration of virtual network appliances, enabling organizations to host and manage their defined boundary protection devices directly within the VCF environment. These capabilities allow for the technical enforcement of policies that prohibit direct connections and mandate the use of specific security controls at network perimeters.

While VCF provides the essential technical mechanisms, the organization retains responsibility for defining what constitutes an acceptable boundary protection device for classified national security systems. This includes selecting, configuring, and deploying the specific firewall rules, security policies, or third-party virtual appliances within the VCF environment. The customer must also identify and classify the national security systems, design the network architecture to ensure their isolation, and establish the overarching policies and procedures that prohibit direct connections to external networks without the specified boundary protection. Ongoing monitoring and auditing of these configurations and policies are also customer responsibilities."
SC-7(27),NET-05.1,"Boundary Protection | Unclassified Non-national Security System Connections

Prohibit the direct connection of [Assignment: organization-defined unclassified non-national security system] to an external network without the use of [Assignment: organization-defined boundary protection device].",Meets,"VCF, leveraging NSX-T, provides comprehensive software-defined networking and security capabilities essential for enforcing network boundaries. The platform facilitates the deployment and configuration of gateway firewalls and distributed firewalls, enabling granular control over network traffic between internal systems and external networks.

NSX Edge Gateways can be configured to act as robust boundary protection devices, preventing direct connections by routing all external traffic through defined security enforcement points. This architecture inherently supports the isolation and protection of specified unclassified non-national security systems.

The organization is responsible for defining which specific unclassified non-national security systems require this protection and for identifying the specific boundary protection devices (e.g., NSX Edge Gateways configured with specific firewall rules) that will enforce these connections."
SC-7(28),NET-06.5,"Boundary Protection | Connections to Public Networks

Prohibit the direct connection of [Assignment: organization-defined system] to a public network.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust capabilities through NSX-T to prevent direct connections of designated systems to public networks. The platform's distributed firewall (DFW) enables granular micro-segmentation, isolating workloads at the virtual machine level and preventing unauthorized egress. Furthermore, the NSX Edge Gateway Firewall enforces perimeter security, allowing for strict control over all traffic entering and exiting the VCF environment. Logical segments can be configured to ensure complete network isolation for specific workloads, routing all external communication through controlled and inspected gateways. Network Address Translation (NAT) can be employed on Edge nodes to obscure internal network topology from public exposure, ensuring that internal systems do not present direct interfaces to external networks. These capabilities are entirely policy-driven, allowing for precise enforcement of connection prohibitions.

The customer is responsible for defining the specific ""organization-defined systems"" that are prohibited from direct public network access, which is a critical policy decision. This includes designing and implementing the appropriate NSX-T firewall rules, routing configurations, and logical network segmentation policies to enforce these prohibitions effectively. Ongoing monitoring, auditing, and change management processes must be established by the customer to ensure the continued efficacy of these controls and to prevent the inadvertent creation of direct connections."
SC-7(29),"CLD-03, NET-03.8, NET-06.1","Boundary Protection | Separate Subnets to Isolate Functions
Implement [Selection: physically
column2: logically] separate subnetworks to isolate the following critical system components and functions: [Assignment: organization-defined critical system components and functions].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation, leveraging NSX-T, provides robust capabilities for implementing logically separate subnetworks to isolate critical system components and functions. NSX-T enables the creation of multiple logical segments, T1 gateways, and VRFs, allowing for granular network isolation within the software-defined data center. This architecture facilitates the separation of management, workload, and edge components into distinct logical network boundaries, and further supports micro-segmentation to isolate individual virtual machines or application tiers.

column1: The organization is responsible for defining the specific critical system components and functions that require isolation. This includes designing the network segmentation strategy, configuring the appropriate NSX-T logical segments, routing, and distributed firewall policies to enforce the defined isolation requirements. The selection and implementation of either physically or logically separate subnetworks, while VCF provides the logical mechanisms, remains an architectural decision and configuration responsibility of the customer."
SC-8,"CRY-03, CRY-04","Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity
Protect the [Selection (one or more): confidentiality
column2: integrity] of transmitted information.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation inherently protects the confidentiality and integrity of transmitted information across its management plane and workload domains. All management components, including SDDC Manager, vCenter Server, NSX Manager, and Workspace ONE Access, communicate using TLS/SSL to ensure secure transmission of control plane data. vSAN provides data-in-transit encryption for inter-node communication within storage clusters, safeguarding data as it moves between hosts. Furthermore, vSphere vMotion supports encryption, protecting virtual machine state and memory during live migrations. NSX-T Data Center provides advanced networking and security services, enabling the creation of encrypted tunnels and micro-segmentation policies to enforce secure communication paths for workload traffic.

column1: The customer is responsible for defining specific organizational policies regarding the types of information requiring protection, the acceptable cryptographic standards, and the scope of application. Activating and configuring specific encryption features, such as vSAN encryption, vMotion encryption, or NSX-T VPNs, falls under customer operational responsibility. This also includes the integration and management of Key Management Systems (KMS) for cryptographic keys and ensuring that applications deployed within the virtual machines implement appropriate encryption for their specific data in transit requirements."
SC-8(1),"CRY-01, CRY-01.1, CRY-03","Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity | Cryptographic Protection
Implement cryptographic mechanisms to [Selection (one or more): prevent unauthorized disclosure of information
column2: detect changes to information] during transmission.",Contributes,"VCF implements robust cryptographic mechanisms to ensure the confidentiality and integrity of information during transmission across its entire software-defined data center stack. NSX-T Data Center secures network traffic by providing encryption capabilities for data in transit, preventing unauthorized disclosure and detecting any changes to information exchanged between virtual machines, workloads, and external networks. This includes securing management plane communications and data plane traffic through various protocols and services. The platform leverages secure communication channels, such as TLS/SSL, for all management interfaces and inter-component communications within vSphere, vSAN, and SDDC Manager, ensuring that administrative data and operational traffic remain protected. Furthermore, VCF integrates with external Key Management Systems (KMS) to manage the lifecycle of cryptographic keys, including their generation, storage, and destruction, which underpins the cryptographic protection applied to data in transit.

column1: While VCF provides the foundational cryptographic capabilities, the customer is responsible for defining specific security policies that dictate the scope and strength of cryptographic protection required for various data classifications and transmission paths. This includes configuring the cryptographic mechanisms within NSX-T Data Center and other VCF components to align with organizational security requirements, as well as establishing and enforcing policies for key management, rotation, and access control. The customer must also implement processes for monitoring the effectiveness of these cryptographic controls and ensuring their continuous operation and compliance."
SC-8(2),"CRY-01, CRY-01.3, DCH-10","Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity | Pre- and Post-transmission Handling
Maintain the [Selection (one or more): confidentiality
column2: integrity] of information during preparation for transmission and during reception.",Contributes,"The VCF platform inherently supports the confidentiality and integrity of information during preparation for transmission and during reception through its robust data-at-rest encryption capabilities. vSAN encryption and vSphere VM encryption ensure that data residing on the underlying storage infrastructure, whether being staged for outbound transmission or stored post-reception, is protected against unauthorized access and modification. The secure design of the VCF infrastructure, including its integrated networking and compute layers, provides a trusted environment for processing and storing sensitive information at these critical junctures.

column1: While VCF provides the foundational technical controls for data protection, the customer is responsible for establishing and enforcing organizational policies and procedures that govern the specific handling of information during these pre- and post-transmission phases. This includes defining application-level security measures, data sanitization processes, and user training to ensure that information confidentiality and integrity are maintained through all stages of its lifecycle, independent of the underlying platform's capabilities."
SC-8(3),NET-13,"Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity | Cryptographic Protection for Message Externals

Implement cryptographic mechanisms to protect message externals unless otherwise protected by [Assignment: organization-defined alternative physical controls].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) establishes a robust and secure network infrastructure that inherently supports cryptographic mechanisms for protecting data in transit, including the communication of its own management plane components. The platform provides the foundational capabilities for secure network segmentation, encrypted communication channels, and the underlying hardware and software necessary to implement strong cryptographic controls across the virtualized environment. This includes support for protocols like TLS for management interfaces and the ability to integrate with network security appliances that enforce encryption for various traffic types.

The customer is responsible for defining what constitutes ""message externals"" within their specific application architectures and data flows, beyond the VCF management plane. This includes selecting and configuring appropriate cryptographic mechanisms for these defined externals, ensuring their proper implementation, and managing the associated cryptographic keys.

Furthermore, the organization must define and implement any alternative physical controls that would negate the need for cryptographic protection for specific message externals, as well as provide the necessary documentation and evidence for such controls."
SC-8(4),CRY-01.4,"Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity | Conceal or Randomize Communications

Implement cryptographic mechanisms to conceal or randomize communication patterns unless otherwise protected by [Assignment: organization-defined alternative physical controls].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides a robust platform for secure network communications. NSX-T, an integral component of VCF, enables network virtualization and micro-segmentation, offering capabilities for encrypting data in transit through various mechanisms, including IPsec VPNs and TLS for management plane traffic. This ensures the confidentiality of communication content across the VCF environment.

The platform does not inherently implement mechanisms to conceal or randomize communication patterns across all hosted workloads or its own management plane beyond standard protocol encryption. The definition and deployment of specific cryptographic mechanisms for pattern obfuscation, as well as the identification of organization-defined alternative physical controls, remain the responsibility of the customer."
SC-10,NET-07,"Network Disconnect

Terminate the network connection associated with a communications session at the end of the session or after [Assignment: organization-defined time period] of inactivity.",Contributes,"VCF management interfaces, including vCenter Server, SDDC Manager, and ESXi hosts, are configured with session timeouts to automatically terminate inactive connections. NSX-T provides stateful firewall services that can be configured to terminate network connections after a specified period of inactivity, applying these policies to network segments and workloads.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific inactivity time periods and configuring these settings across all relevant VCF components, NSX-T firewall rules, and within guest operating systems and applications to ensure comprehensive coverage for all communication sessions."
SC-11,END-09,"Trusted Path
a. Provide a [Selection: physically
column2: logically] isolated trusted communications path for communications between the user and the trusted components of the system

column1: b. Permit users to invoke the trusted communications path for communications between the user and the following security functions of the system, including at a minimum, authentication and re-authentication: [Assignment: organization-defined security functions].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) establishes logically isolated trusted communication paths through its integrated networking and security components. NSX-T Data Center provides robust network segmentation and micro-segmentation capabilities, enabling the creation of dedicated, isolated network segments for trusted system components and management interfaces. All management plane communications within VCF, including user access to SDDC Manager, vCenter Server, and NSX-T Manager, leverage secure protocols such as TLS/SSL, ensuring data confidentiality and integrity over these logically isolated paths. The platform integrates with enterprise identity providers, such as Active Directory or Workspace ONE Access, to facilitate secure user authentication and re-authentication to VCF management interfaces. This ensures that the critical security functions of user authentication occur over the established trusted communication path, which users implicitly invoke when accessing VCF management UIs or APIs.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific trusted components within their VCF deployment and identifying any additional security functions, beyond authentication and re-authentication, that require the trusted path. Furthermore, the customer must design and implement the specific NSX-T network segmentation, firewall rules, and access policies to enforce the logical isolation and ensure that user endpoints are appropriately configured to utilize these trusted paths in accordance with organizational security requirements."
SC-12,CRY-08,"Cryptographic Key Establishment and Management

Establish and manage cryptographic keys when cryptography is employed within the system in accordance with the following key management requirements: [Assignment: organization-defined requirements for key generation, distribution, storage, access, and destruction].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) establishes and manages cryptographic keys through its native integration with external Key Management Systems (KMS) that adhere to the KMIP standard. This architecture ensures that cryptographic keys employed for data-at-rest encryption, such as those utilized by vSAN Data-at-Rest Encryption and vSphere VM Encryption, are generated, distributed, stored, accessed, and destroyed in a secure and controlled manner. VCF components securely request keys from the configured KMS, which then handles the generation of robust cryptographic keys. Key distribution occurs over secure channels from the KMS to the ESXi hosts, where they are used for encryption operations. Keys are primarily stored within the hardened KMS, with VCF components holding only encrypted keys or key identifiers as appropriate. Access to these keys is strictly governed by the KMS, based on authentication and authorization policies established by the organization. Key destruction is also orchestrated through the KMS, ensuring keys are securely retired from service.

While VCF provides the foundational technical capabilities and integrations for cryptographic key management, the organization is responsible for defining the specific requirements for key generation, distribution, storage, access, and destruction. This includes establishing policies for key lengths, algorithms, rotation schedules, access controls within the KMS, and the procedures for key destruction. The selection, deployment, and ongoing operational management of the external Key Management System itself remain an organizational responsibility."
SC-12(1),CRY-09.3,"Cryptographic Key Establishment and Management | Availability

Maintain availability of information in the event of the loss of cryptographic keys by users.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides a highly available and resilient infrastructure platform, forming the secure foundation for applications and services that manage cryptographic keys. The platform integrates with external Key Management Systems (KMS) to secure infrastructure-level encryption for components such as vSAN and vSphere VM encryption, ensuring the continuous availability of encrypted data at rest within the VCF environment. This robust infrastructure ensures that any deployed key management solutions or applications designed for key recovery operate within a resilient and available framework.

The direct mechanisms for maintaining information availability following the loss of cryptographic keys by individual users are implemented at the application or identity management layer. Customers are responsible for deploying and configuring user-centric key recovery, escrow, or alternative access solutions within their applications, leveraging the VCF infrastructure. This includes establishing comprehensive policies and procedures for user key lifecycle management, secure key storage, and recovery processes that address user-specific cryptographic key loss."
SC-12(2),CRY-09.1,"Cryptographic Key Establishment and Management | Symmetric Keys
Produce, control, and distribute symmetric cryptographic keys using [Selection: NIST FIPS-validated
column2: NSA-approved] key management technology and processes.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides the foundational infrastructure and integration points necessary for leveraging external key management systems (KMS) to secure symmetric cryptographic keys. The platform supports the consumption of keys for data-at-rest encryption, such such as vSAN encryption and VM encryption, by integrating with industry-standard, KMIP-compliant KMS solutions or vSphere Native Key Provider. This architecture ensures that the underlying compute and storage resources can utilize symmetric keys for data protection.

column1: The responsibility for producing, controlling, and distributing symmetric cryptographic keys, including the selection of NIST FIPS-validated or NSA-approved key management technology and the establishment of associated processes, rests with the customer. The customer must implement and operate a compliant KMS and define the operational procedures for key lifecycle management, ensuring adherence to the specified validation requirements."
SC-12(3),CRY-09.2,"Cryptographic Key Establishment and Management | Asymmetric Keys
Produce, control, and distribute asymmetric cryptographic keys using [Selection: NSA-approved key management technology and processes
column3: DoD-approved or DoD-issued Medium Assurance PKI certificates
column4: DoD-approved or DoD-issued Medium Hardware Assurance PKI certificates and hardware security tokens that protect the user’s private key
column5: certificates issued in accordance with organization-defined requirements].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) components inherently utilize asymmetric cryptographic keys, primarily in the form of X.509 certificates, to establish secure communication channels and verify identities across the platform's management plane. VCF integrates with enterprise Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) solutions to provision and manage these certificates, ensuring the integrity and confidentiality of inter-component communications. The platform supports the use of certificates issued in accordance with organizational requirements and can be configured to leverage external Key Management Systems (KMS) and Hardware Security Modules (HSMs) for enhanced protection of cryptographic material, including private keys associated with VCF's operational certificates.

column1: The selection, implementation, and ongoing management of NSA-approved key management technology and processes, DoD-approved or DoD-issued PKI certificates (including those requiring Medium Hardware Assurance and hardware security tokens), or the definition of organization-specific requirements for asymmetric key production, control, and distribution, remain the responsibility of the customer. VCF provides the secure infrastructure foundation upon which these broader organizational key management strategies can be deployed and integrated, but it does not inherently provide the full end-to-end asymmetric key lifecycle management for all organizational use cases or enforce specific governmental approval processes."
SC-13,"CRY-01, CRY-01.2, CRY-05","Cryptographic Protection
a. Determine the [Assignment: organization-defined cryptographic uses]

b. Implement the following types of cryptography required for each specified cryptographic use: [Assignment: organization-defined types of cryptography for each specified cryptographic use].",Contributes,"The VCF platform provides comprehensive cryptographic capabilities to protect data at rest and in transit across its components. This includes robust encryption for vSAN datastores, virtual machines, and network traffic within NSX-T, leveraging industry-standard algorithms and protocols. The platform integrates with external Key Management Systems (KMS) for secure key lifecycle management and utilizes FIPS 140-2 validated cryptographic modules where applicable, ensuring a strong foundation for cryptographic protection.

The organization is responsible for defining its specific cryptographic uses and determining the appropriate types of cryptography required for each use case, based on its risk posture and compliance requirements. This includes establishing policies for cryptographic key management, algorithm selection, and the overall application of cryptographic controls within and beyond the VCF environment. The platform provides the technical mechanisms to implement these organizational definitions and configurations."
SC-15,END-14,"Collaborative Computing Devices and Applications
a. Prohibit remote activation of collaborative computing devices and applications with the following exceptions: [Assignment: organization-defined exceptions where remote activation is to be allowed]
b. Provide an explicit indication of use to users physically present at the devices.",Not Applicable,"End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope."
SC-15(1),END-14,"Collaborative Computing Devices and Applications | Physical or Logical Disconnect
Provide [Selection (one or more): physical
column2: logical] disconnect of collaborative computing devices in a manner that supports ease of use.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides the foundational infrastructure for implementing logical disconnects of collaborative computing environments. Through NSX-T, granular network segmentation and distributed firewall capabilities enable the isolation or complete logical disconnection of virtual machines hosting collaborative applications or virtual desktop infrastructure (VDI) endpoints. This allows for the enforcement of policies that can sever network communication to specific applications or user sessions. Furthermore, when integrated with VDI solutions like VMware Horizon, VCF supports the management of virtual desktop lifecycles, including the ability to disconnect or log off user sessions, thereby logically disconnecting the user from their collaborative applications running within the virtual desktop.

column1: The customer is responsible for defining and implementing the specific network segmentation policies within NSX-T to target collaborative applications or devices. This includes configuring firewall rules, security groups, and service definitions to achieve the desired logical disconnect. Additionally, the customer must configure the collaborative applications themselves, as well as any VDI session management policies, to ensure that the disconnect mechanism is user-friendly and aligns with operational requirements for ease of use."
SC-15(3),END-14.1,"Collaborative Computing Devices and Applications | Disabling and Removal in Secure Work Areas

Disable or remove collaborative computing devices and applications from [Assignment: organization-defined systems or system components] in [Assignment: organization-defined secure work areas].",Not Applicable,"End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope."
SC-15(4),END-14.2,"Collaborative Computing Devices and Applications | Explicitly Indicate Current Participants

Provide an explicit indication of current participants in [Assignment: organization-defined online meetings and teleconferences].",Contributes,"The VMware Cloud Foundation platform provides the robust, scalable infrastructure necessary to host a wide array of collaborative computing devices and applications, including those used for online meetings and teleconferences. This foundational environment supports the deployment of third-party collaboration solutions that inherently offer features to explicitly indicate current participants within a session. The underlying compute, storage, and networking capabilities ensure these applications can operate effectively and reliably, enabling the display of participant lists as a core function of the chosen software.

The organization is responsible for selecting, deploying, and configuring specific collaboration applications that possess the required functionality to explicitly display current participants. This includes defining what constitutes ""organization-defined online meetings and teleconferences"" and ensuring the chosen applications are consistently utilized and configured to meet this requirement. Policy enforcement, user training, and ongoing monitoring of these third-party applications remain the customer's responsibility."
SC-16,CRY-10,"Transmission of Security and Privacy Attributes

Associate [Assignment: organization-defined security and privacy attributes] with information exchanged between systems and between system components.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust capabilities to associate security and privacy attributes with information exchanged between systems and components. Through vSphere tags, NSX-T security groups, and vSAN storage policies, administrators can apply metadata representing security classifications (e.g., 'Confidential,' 'PCI-DSS') and privacy categories (e.g., 'PHI,' 'GDPR-Sensitive') to virtual machines, network segments, and data stores. NSX-T then enforces granular network segmentation and security policies based on these assigned attributes, ensuring that information transmission adheres to defined security postures. Furthermore, VCF leverages encryption for data in transit via NSX-T network encryption, vSAN data-in-transit encryption, and encrypted vMotion, safeguarding the confidentiality and integrity of information during exchange, directly supporting the CRY-10 mapping.

The organization is solely responsible for defining the specific security and privacy attributes pertinent to their data and operational context. This includes establishing information classification schemes and processes to accurately assign these attributes to information and the systems that process or transmit it. VMware Cloud Foundation provides the technical framework for attribute application and policy enforcement, but the initial definition and ongoing classification remain an organizational responsibility."
SC-16(1),"CRY-04, CRY-10","Transmission of Security and Privacy Attributes | Integrity Verification

Verify the integrity of transmitted security and privacy attributes.",Meets,"The VCF platform inherently secures the transmission of its own operational and management data, including security and privacy attributes, through the pervasive use of cryptographic integrity verification.

All inter-component communications leverage secure protocols, such as TLS, which provide robust mechanisms to detect and prevent unauthorized alteration of data during transit. This includes the integrity verification of authentication tokens, authorization policies, configuration parameters, and other sensitive metadata that constitute security and privacy attributes.

Furthermore, the platform integrates with enterprise key management systems, ensuring that the cryptographic keys underpinning these integrity mechanisms are securely managed and rotated, directly supporting the requirements for strong cryptographic protection and key lifecycle management."
SC-17,CRY-08,"Public Key Infrastructure Certificates
a. Issue public key certificates under an [Assignment: organization-defined certificate policy] or obtain public key certificates from an approved service provider

b. Include only approved trust anchors in trust stores or certificate stores managed by the organization.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive capabilities for managing public key infrastructure certificates across its SDDC components. The platform natively utilizes VMware Certificate Authority (VMCA) for internal certificate issuance, ensuring secure communication between management plane elements.

For external integration and adherence to organizational policies, VCF fully supports replacing VMCA-issued certificates with those obtained from an organization-approved external Certificate Authority (CA). This includes the ability to import and manage the full certificate chain, including root and intermediate trust anchors, into the respective component trust stores, such as vCenter's VMware Endpoint Certificate Store (VECS) and NSX-T's trust store. SDDC Manager streamlines the certificate replacement process, ensuring consistent application of approved certificates and trust anchors throughout the environment.

While VCF offers the technical mechanisms to implement certificate policies and manage trust stores, the organization remains responsible for defining its specific certificate policy, identifying and approving external certificate service providers, and establishing the list of approved trust anchors. The customer must also implement the necessary governance and procedural controls to ensure ongoing compliance with their defined policies for certificate issuance, renewal, and trust anchor management."
SC-18,END-10,"Mobile Code
a. Define acceptable and unacceptable mobile code and mobile code technologies

b. Authorize, monitor, and control the use of mobile code within the system.",Contributes,"The VCF platform provides the foundational infrastructure and integrated security capabilities necessary to monitor and control the use of mobile code within the system. Through its native integration with NSX-T Data Center, the platform enables granular network segmentation and distributed firewalling, allowing for precise control over communication pathways for any system utilizing mobile code. Furthermore, VCF supports the integration of third-party endpoint protection platforms (EPP) and endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions, which are critical for real-time monitoring of code execution, identification of mobile code, and enforcement of execution policies on virtual machines. These capabilities facilitate the technical enforcement of controls over mobile code behavior and network interactions.
The customer is responsible for defining acceptable and unacceptable mobile code and mobile code technologies, establishing the policies for their use, and authorizing their deployment.

This includes configuring the integrated security solutions, such as endpoint protection agents and NSX-T firewall rules, to align with the organization's defined mobile code policies. The customer must also establish and manage the processes for continuous monitoring, logging, and responding to alerts related to mobile code activity within the VCF environment."
SC-18(1),"END-10, VPM-02, VPM-04","Mobile Code | Identify Unacceptable Code and Take Corrective Actions

Identify [Assignment: organization-defined unacceptable mobile code] and take [Assignment: organization-defined corrective actions].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation, leveraging integrated security services such as NSX Advanced Threat Prevention and Carbon Black Cloud Workload Protection, provides comprehensive capabilities for identifying and managing mobile code. This includes robust application control features for whitelisting approved applications and blacklisting known unacceptable executables, scripts, and other forms of dynamic content.

Behavioral analysis, signature-based detection, and network segmentation within the platform enable the detection of suspicious mobile code activity and the isolation of affected workloads, preventing unauthorized execution and limiting potential impact.

While the platform provides the technical mechanisms for enforcement, the organization is solely responsible for defining what constitutes ""unacceptable mobile code"" within its environment. This includes establishing specific policies, criteria, and baselines for mobile code acceptance.

Furthermore, the organization must define the specific ""corrective actions"" to be taken upon identification, such as quarantine, blocking, alerting, or automated remediation, and configure the platform's security controls accordingly."
SC-18(2),"AST-02.7, END-10","Mobile Code | Acquisition, Development, and Use

Verify that the acquisition, development, and use of mobile code to be deployed in the system meets [Assignment: organization-defined mobile code requirements].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides a hardened and validated infrastructure, establishing a secure foundation for deploying workloads that may incorporate mobile code. The platform's secure boot, trusted platform modules, and network segmentation capabilities, powered by NSX-T distributed firewalls and micro-segmentation, restrict communication paths and isolate workloads. SDDC Manager ensures consistent patching and updates of the underlying infrastructure, reducing common vulnerabilities that mobile code might exploit.

The organization is responsible for defining its specific mobile code requirements for acquisition, development, and use. This includes establishing secure coding guidelines, approved sources, testing methodologies, and deployment processes. The customer must implement and enforce these policies within their applications and perform the necessary verification to ensure all deployed mobile code adheres to these defined organizational standards."
SC-18(3),"END-10, NET-18","Mobile Code | Prevent Downloading and Execution

Prevent the download and execution of [Assignment: organization-defined unacceptable mobile code].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation integrates advanced threat prevention capabilities across the network and endpoint to prevent the download and execution of mobile code. Network security components inspect ingress traffic to detect and block the transfer of known malicious or policy-violating mobile code.

Endpoint security solutions provide robust controls to prevent the execution of unauthorized or malicious mobile code, including scripts, executables, and other dynamic content, through application control, behavioral analysis, and anti-malware functions. These capabilities are designed to enforce granular policies against specific file types, sources, and behaviors.

The organization is responsible for defining what constitutes unacceptable mobile code within its environment. This definition drives the specific configuration of the integrated security controls, including establishing policies for mobile code handling, maintaining threat intelligence, and regularly updating security configurations to align with the defined unacceptable mobile code criteria."
SC-18(4),"CFG-03.3, END-10","Mobile Code | Prevent Automatic Execution

Prevent the automatic execution of mobile code in [Assignment: organization-defined software applications] and enforce [Assignment: organization-defined actions] prior to executing the code.",Contributes,"The VCF platform, through its integrated security services, provides robust capabilities for application control and execution prevention. This includes the technical mechanisms to define and enforce policies that prevent the automatic execution of mobile code within the virtualized infrastructure. The platform can implement application whitelisting, blacklisting, and granular execution controls, ensuring that only approved code is permitted to run and that specific actions are enforced prior to execution.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific software applications to which these controls apply and for establishing the precise actions that must be enforced prior to any mobile code execution. This includes developing and maintaining the policies that dictate which applications are considered mobile code, which applications are subject to automatic execution prevention, and the detailed pre-execution procedures."
SC-20,NET-10,"Secure Name/address Resolution Service (authoritative Source)

a. Provide additional data origin authentication and integrity verification artifacts along with the authoritative name resolution data the system returns in response to external name/address resolution queries

b. Provide the means to indicate the security status of child zones and (if the child supports secure resolution services) to enable verification of a chain of trust among parent and child domains, when operating as part of a distributed, hierarchical namespace.",Not Applicable,"End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope."
SC-20(2),NET-10,"Secure Name/address Resolution Service (authoritative Source) | Data Origin and Integrity

Provide data origin and integrity protection artifacts for internal name/address resolution queries.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation components inherently rely on internal name resolution services for operational functionality and inter-component communication. The platform's network virtualization capabilities, powered by NSX-T, enable robust network segmentation and firewalling, isolating the communication paths to internal name resolution services. This ensures that DNS queries originating from VCF components traverse a protected network fabric. Furthermore, VCF management components are configured to utilize customer-provided DNS infrastructure, and the platform supports the configuration of secure DNS server addresses.

While VCF provides the secure network environment and configuration points for DNS, the responsibility for implementing data origin and integrity protection artifacts, such as DNSSEC signing for the authoritative internal name resolution service, rests with the customer. The customer must ensure their authoritative DNS infrastructure provides these protections and that VCF components are configured to leverage and validate them, if applicable. This includes defining and enforcing policies for DNS server hardening, secure zone transfers, and DNSSEC implementation."
SC-21,NET-10.2,"Secure Name/address Resolution Service (recursive or Caching Resolver)

Request and perform data origin authentication and data integrity verification on the name/address resolution responses the system receives from authoritative sources.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides the foundational infrastructure necessary to host secure name/address resolution services. The platform facilitates the deployment and operation of virtual machines and network configurations that can support DNS servers, including those capable of implementing DNS Security Extensions (DNSSEC). VCF management and workload domains are configured to consume customer-provided DNS infrastructure, ensuring all platform components, such as ESXi hosts, vCenter Server, and NSX, resolve names through designated DNS servers.

The direct implementation and operational management of data origin authentication and data integrity verification on the recursive or caching DNS resolvers, specifically through DNSSEC, is a customer responsibility. This encompasses the configuration of DNSSEC on the authoritative and recursive DNS servers, the establishment of trust anchors, and the ongoing validation of DNS responses.

While VCF components rely on these external DNS services for name resolution, the customer is accountable for ensuring the chosen DNS infrastructure fully meets the requirements for secure name resolution, including the necessary authentication and integrity verification mechanisms."
SC-22,NET-10.1,"Architecture and Provisioning for Name/address Resolution Service

Ensure the systems that collectively provide name/address resolution service for an organization are fault-tolerant and implement internal and external role separation.",Contributes,"The VMware Cloud Foundation platform inherently provides a highly available and fault-tolerant infrastructure foundation for deploying name/address resolution services. Core VCF management components are deployed with high availability, and customer workloads, including virtual machines hosting DNS or DHCP services, leverage vSphere HA, DRS, and vSAN capabilities to ensure continuous operation and resilience against underlying hardware failures. This architecture directly supports the fault-tolerance requirement for critical infrastructure services.

VCF implements robust role-based access control (RBAC) across its management plane, including SDDC Manager, vCenter Server, and NSX Manager. This enables granular internal role separation for administrators managing the VCF infrastructure itself.

Furthermore, NSX-T Data Center provides advanced micro-segmentation capabilities, allowing for the logical isolation of network zones and the enforcement of security policies between internal and external name/address resolution services. This network-level separation is crucial for implementing external role separation.

The customer is responsible for defining the specific organizational policies, configuring the name/address resolution applications (e.g., DNS server roles, DHCP scopes), and establishing the human accountability and processes required to fully implement and maintain internal and external role separation within their deployed services."
SC-23,NET-09,"Session Authenticity

Protect the authenticity of communications sessions.",Contributes,"The VCF platform inherently protects the authenticity of communication sessions through its foundational architecture. All inter-component communication, management interfaces, and API interactions leverage secure protocols such as TLS, ensuring that sessions are established with verified identities and that data exchanged remains confidential and authentic. VCF integrates robust authentication mechanisms, including vCenter Single Sign-On and external identity sources like Active Directory, to validate the legitimacy of users and services initiating sessions. NSX-T micro-segmentation capabilities further enhance session authenticity by isolating workloads and management planes, thereby restricting unauthorized access and potential vectors for session compromise.

While VCF provides the secure technical framework, the customer is responsible for configuring and maintaining the specific security parameters to fully meet the control's intent. This includes managing TLS certificates, defining and enforcing strong authentication policies, configuring appropriate session timeouts for management interfaces, and implementing network segmentation rules to protect critical session endpoints. The customer must also establish operational procedures for monitoring session activity and responding to any detected authenticity anomalies."
SC-23(1),NET-09.1,"Session Authenticity | Invalidate Session Identifiers at Logout

Invalidate session identifiers upon user logout or other session termination.",Meets,"The VCF platform components, including vCenter Server, NSX Manager, SDDC Manager, and Workspace ONE Access, are engineered to invalidate session identifiers immediately upon user logout. This ensures that once a user explicitly terminates their session, the associated identifier can no longer be used to access the system.

Furthermore, these components are configured to invalidate session identifiers upon other forms of session termination, such as inactivity timeouts or administrative actions, thereby preventing unauthorized session reuse."
SC-23(3),NET-09.2,"Session Authenticity | Unique System-generated Session Identifiers

Generate a unique session identifier for each session with [Assignment: organization-defined randomness requirements] and recognize only session identifiers that are system-generated.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation components inherently generate unique, cryptographically strong session identifiers for all active sessions across the SDDC. The platform is designed to recognize and validate only those session identifiers that are internally generated, preventing the use of externally crafted or manipulated session IDs. This ensures the authenticity and integrity of user and system sessions.

The organization is responsible for defining and documenting its specific randomness requirements for session identifiers. While the platform provides robust, system-generated identifiers, the explicit policy for these requirements remains an organizational responsibility."
SC-23(5),CRY-11,"Session Authenticity | Allowed Certificate Authorities

Only allow the use of [Assignment: organization-defined certificate authorities] for verification of the establishment of protected sessions.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation components, including vCenter Server, ESXi hosts, NSX-T, and SDDC Manager, establish secure, protected sessions utilizing certificates. The platform provides robust capabilities to replace default self-signed certificates with those issued by an enterprise Certificate Authority (CA). This includes the ability to import and manage trusted root and intermediate CA certificates within the trust stores of all VCF management components, ensuring that only certificates from organization-approved CAs are trusted for VCF's internal and external management plane communications.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific certificate authorities permitted for verification of protected sessions. This definition must be established through organizational policy and subsequently implemented across all relevant systems and applications, including the configuration of trust stores for workloads and non-VCF components to exclusively accept certificates from the designated CAs."
SC-24,SEA-07.2,"Fail in Known State

Fail to a [Assignment: organization-defined known system state] for the following failures on the indicated components while preserving [Assignment: organization-defined system state information] in failure: [Assignment: list of organization-defined types of system failures on organization-defined system components].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides a highly resilient infrastructure designed to manage system failures and ensure operational continuity.

vSphere High Availability (HA) automatically restarts virtual machines on healthy hosts following an ESXi host failure, ensuring workloads return to a known operational state. vSAN provides distributed storage with data redundancy, preserving data integrity and availability across component failures. NSX-T Data Center components are deployed with built-in resilience, maintaining network services even during individual component outages.
These capabilities collectively ensure that the underlying infrastructure and hosted workloads recover to a consistent and known operational state after various types of failures.

The explicit definition of an organization-defined known system state, the specific organization-defined system state information to be preserved during failure, and the comprehensive list of organization-defined types of system failures on organization-defined system components are responsibilities that reside with the customer. While VCF provides the robust technical foundation for resilient operations and recovery, the customer must establish and document these specific organizational requirements and ensure their applications and configurations align with these definitions."
SC-25,END-11,"Thin Nodes

Employ minimal functionality and information storage on the following system components: [Assignment: organization-defined system components].",Contributes,"The VCF platform provides a highly virtualized and abstracted infrastructure that inherently supports the deployment of purpose-built, minimal-footprint workloads. Its architecture, leveraging immutable ESXi hosts and appliance-based management components, is designed to limit unnecessary functionality and storage on the core infrastructure elements themselves. The virtualization capabilities within VCF enable organizations to provision virtual machines and containers with precisely defined resources and minimal operating system installations, thereby facilitating the implementation of thin nodes for application and service components.

The organization is responsible for identifying the specific system components that require minimal functionality and information storage, as well as defining the scope of ""thinness"" for their applications. This includes configuring guest operating systems, application stacks, and ensuring that deployed workloads adhere to these architectural principles. The platform provides the foundational capabilities, but the application of the ""thin node"" concept to customer-defined components requires explicit design and configuration decisions by the organization."
SC-26,SEA-11,"Decoys

Include components within organizational systems specifically designed to be the target of malicious attacks for detecting, deflecting, and analyzing such attacks.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides the robust, virtualized infrastructure necessary to host decoy components within an organizational system. The platform's capabilities, including vSphere for virtual machine deployment and NSX-T for advanced network segmentation and traffic monitoring, enable the creation of isolated environments where decoy systems can be deployed and observed without impacting production workloads.
NSX-T's distributed firewall and network introspection features can be leveraged to direct, isolate, and analyze traffic targeting these deceptive elements.

The design, deployment, and ongoing management of the specific decoy applications, services, or operating systems that constitute the actual targets for malicious attacks remain the responsibility of the customer. This includes configuring the decoy's behavior, integrating it with threat intelligence platforms, and establishing the processes for analyzing detected attacks."
SC-27,END-10,"Platform-independent Applications

Include within organizational systems the following platform independent applications: [Assignment: organization-defined platform-independent applications].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides a robust, virtualized infrastructure capable of hosting a wide array of applications, including those designed for platform independence. The platform supports various operating systems and application runtimes within virtual machines and containers, offering the underlying compute, storage, and networking resources necessary for their deployment and operation. This foundational capability ensures that once platform-independent applications are identified, they can be seamlessly integrated and executed within the organizational system.

The organization is solely responsible for defining which specific applications are considered platform-independent and for ensuring their inclusion within the organizational systems as required by this control. This involves establishing the criteria for platform independence, identifying the relevant applications, and implementing the necessary processes and configurations to deploy and manage them on the VCF infrastructure."
SC-28,"CRY-05, END-02","Protection of Information at Rest
Protect the [Selection (one or more): confidentiality
column2: integrity] of the following information at rest: [Assignment: organization-defined information at rest].",Contributes,"The VCF platform provides comprehensive capabilities for protecting information at rest. vSAN encryption ensures the confidentiality of all data stored on vSAN datastores, including virtual machine files, configuration data, and associated objects, with FIPS 140-2 validated cryptographic modules. This encryption is applied transparently at the cluster level. Additionally, vSphere VM encryption offers granular protection for individual virtual machines, encrypting VM files and virtual disks to safeguard confidentiality regardless of the underlying storage type.

column1: Data integrity is inherently maintained across the VCF stack through mechanisms such as vSAN's distributed RAID and checksums, which detect and correct data corruption.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific information classified as ""at rest"" that requires protection, establishing the appropriate confidentiality and/or integrity requirements for that data, and implementing the VCF-provided encryption and integrity features according to those definitions. This includes configuring vSAN encryption, applying vSphere VM encryption to designated workloads, and ensuring proper key management practices are in place."
SC-28(1),"BCD-11.4, CRY-04, CRY-05, DCH-07.2","Protection of Information at Rest | Cryptographic Protection

Implement cryptographic mechanisms to prevent unauthorized disclosure and modification of the following information at rest on [Assignment: organization-defined system components or media]: [Assignment: organization-defined information].",Contributes,"VCF provides comprehensive cryptographic mechanisms to protect information at rest. Data stored on vSAN datastores can be encrypted using vSAN Encryption, ensuring confidentiality and integrity across the storage layer. Additionally, individual virtual machines can be encrypted using vSphere VM Encryption, providing granular protection for specific workloads. These capabilities leverage industry-standard cryptographic algorithms and integrate with external Key Management Systems (KMS) for secure key lifecycle management, preventing unauthorized disclosure and modification of data residing on VCF-managed system components.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific information requiring cryptographic protection and identifying the system components or media where this information resides. This includes configuring and enabling vSAN encryption or vSphere VM Encryption based on their defined scope and deploying and managing the external Key Management System. Furthermore, the organization must establish and enforce policies for data classification, encryption requirements, and key management procedures to ensure comprehensive compliance."
SC-28(2),"BCD-11, CRY-05.2","Protection of Information at Rest | Offline Storage

Remove the following information from online storage and store offline in a secure location: [Assignment: organization-defined information].",Not Applicable,"End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope."
SC-28(3),CRY-09,"Protection of Information at Rest | Cryptographic Keys
Provide protected storage for cryptographic keys [Selection:
[Assignment: organization-defined safeguards]

column2: hardware-protected key store].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust capabilities for protecting cryptographic keys by integrating with external Key Management Systems (KMS) that support Hardware Security Modules (HSMs). The platform's core components, such as vSphere and vSAN, are designed to leverage these external KMS solutions for managing encryption keys used for data-at-rest protection. This architecture ensures that cryptographic keys are generated, stored, and managed within FIPS 140-2 validated hardware, thereby meeting the requirement for a hardware-protected key store. The platform facilitates the secure communication and key exchange between the VCF environment and the external KMS/HSM infrastructure.

SC-39,SEA-04,"Process Isolation

Maintain a separate execution domain for each executing system process.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) inherently provides a strong foundation for process isolation through the ESXi hypervisor. This fundamental isolation extends to all VCF management components, which reside in dedicated virtual machines, benefiting from the same robust separation. The underlying operating systems, such as Photon OS used for many VCF components, further enforce process isolation mechanisms at the operating system level, segmenting memory and resources for individual processes within their respective virtual machines.

The hypervisor acts as a secure boundary, preventing unauthorized access or interference between these isolated execution environments by utilizing underlying CPU virtualization extensions and memory management units to enforce strict resource partitioning and memory protection."

(a) Compare the internal system clocks [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] with [Assignment: organization-defined authoritative time source]

Furthermore, NSX-T Gateway Firewall (GFW) provides perimeter enforcement capabilities at logical router interfaces, controlling traffic flow between internal segments and external networks or other security domains. This architecture enables the creation of distinct security domains using logical constructs such as segments and security groups, with policies dictating permissible communication flows.

Incorporate flaw remediation into the organizational configuration management process.",Contributes,"The VCF platform provides robust capabilities for identifying and remediating system flaws within its managed components. SI-02(07),TDA-06.6,Control not found in reference sheet.,Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation generates comprehensive audit trails and system event logs across all management components, including vCenter Server, NSX, and vSAN.
