SI-3,"END-04, END-04.1, END-04.4, NET-12, TDA-18, VPM-01, VPM-05","Malicious Code Protection
a. Implement [Selection (one or more): signature based
column2: non-signature based] malicious code protection mechanisms at system entry and exit points to detect and eradicate malicious code

column1: b. Automatically update malicious code protection mechanisms as new releases are available in accordance with organizational configuration management policy and procedures
c. Configure malicious code protection mechanisms to:
1. Perform periodic scans of the system [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and real-time scans of files from external sources at [Selection (one or more): endpoint
column2: network entry and exit points] as the files are downloaded, opened, or executed in accordance with organizational policy

column1: 2. [Selection (one or more): block malicious code

column2: and send alert to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles] in response to malicious code detection

column1: d. Address the receipt of false positives during malicious code detection and eradication and the resulting potential impact on the availability of the system.",Contributes,"The platform implements robust malicious code protection mechanisms, leveraging both signature-based and non-signature-based detection techniques. These capabilities are deployed at system entry and exit points, encompassing both endpoint and network boundaries, to effectively detect and eradicate malicious code.

The platform ensures these protection mechanisms are automatically updated as new releases become available, maintaining an up-to-date defense posture. It is configured to perform periodic system scans and real-time scans of files originating from external sources, executing these scans at endpoints and network entry/exit points as files are downloaded, opened, or executed.

Upon detection of malicious code, the platform can automatically block or quarantine the threat and generate immediate alerts. Its integrated threat detection and analysis capabilities further support the understanding and management of detected threats, which can be leveraged in addressing false positives.

Organizations must define the specific frequency for periodic system scans and establish an organizational policy governing real-time scanning parameters. They are responsible for selecting the precise actions to be taken upon malicious code detection, such as blocking or quarantining, and for identifying the specific personnel or roles designated to receive alerts.

Furthermore, the organization must develop and implement comprehensive configuration management policies and procedures to govern the automatic updates of protection mechanisms. Crucially, the organization is accountable for establishing processes to address the receipt of false positives during malicious code detection and eradication, including assessing and mitigating any resulting potential impact on system availability."
SI-3(6),END-04.5,"Malicious Code Protection | Testing and Verification
(a) Test malicious code protection mechanisms [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] by introducing known benign code into the system

(b) Verify that the detection of the code and the associated incident reporting occur.",Contributes,"VCF establishes a robust and secure virtualized infrastructure, providing the foundational platform for deploying and operating malicious code protection mechanisms. The platform's inherent security features, including network segmentation via NSX Distributed Firewall and advanced threat prevention capabilities within NSX, contribute to a secure environment where such protections can be effectively implemented. VCF components generate comprehensive logs that can be integrated with external Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) systems, enabling the collection of evidence for detection and incident reporting verification.

The active testing of malicious code protection mechanisms, including the introduction of known benign code into systems and the definition of testing frequency, remains a customer responsibility. Customers must implement and manage endpoint protection solutions within their guest virtual machines and establish the processes for verifying the detection of malicious code and the associated incident reporting. This includes defining incident response procedures and integrating VCF logs with their chosen security operations workflows to ensure comprehensive verification."

].",Contributes,"The VCF platform provides robust capabilities for system monitoring, enabling the detection of attacks, indicators of potential attacks, and unauthorized local, network, and remote connections. SI-4(1),MON-01.1,"System Monitoring | System-wide Intrusion Detection System

Connect and configure individual intrusion detection tools into a system-wide intrusion detection system.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation integrates robust intrusion detection capabilities directly into the software-defined data center. This provides the necessary infrastructure for aggregating and correlating security intelligence across the entire VCF environment.

While the platform delivers these essential components, the customer is responsible for defining the comprehensive system-wide intrusion detection strategy. This includes configuring specific IDS/IPS rulesets within NSX-T, establishing correlation policies and alert thresholds within vRealize Log Insight, and integrating these capabilities with broader organizational security operations and incident response workflows. The customer must also define the scope and configuration of any additional, non-VCF native intrusion detection tools and ensure their integration into the overall system-wide detection framework."

SI-4(4),MON-01.3,"System Monitoring | Inbound and Outbound Communications Traffic

Alert [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles] when the following system-generated indications of compromise or potential compromise occur: [Assignment: organization-defined compromise indicators].",Contributes,"The VMware Cloud Foundation platform provides comprehensive system monitoring capabilities through its integrated management and operations suite, including Aria Operations and Aria Log Insight. SI-4(7),"MON-01.11, IRO-02.1","System Monitoring | Automated Response to Suspicious Events
(a) Notify [Assignment: organization-defined incident response personnel (identified by name and/or by role)] of detected suspicious events

(b) Take the following actions upon detection: [Assignment: organization-defined least-disruptive actions to terminate suspicious events].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust capabilities for automated system monitoring and response to suspicious events. NSX Distributed Firewall (DFW) and NSX Advanced Threat Prevention (ATP) further enhance detection by identifying malicious network activity and enforcing micro-segmentation. Upon detection of suspicious events, vROps and vRLI can generate alerts, which are then integrated with vRealize Orchestrator (vRO) and vRealize Automation (vRA) to trigger pre-defined, automated actions. These actions can include isolating affected virtual machines or network segments, blocking suspicious IP addresses, or initiating forensic data collection workflows, providing a mechanism to terminate suspicious events with minimal disruption.

The organization is responsible for defining what constitutes a suspicious event, identifying the specific incident response personnel to be notified, and establishing the precise least-disruptive actions to be taken. The customer must configure the monitoring thresholds, alert rules, and design the automated workflows within vRealize Orchestrator and vRealize Automation to align with their defined incident response policies and procedures."

Make provisions so that [Assignment: organization-defined encrypted communications traffic] is visible to [Assignment: organization-defined system monitoring tools and mechanisms].",Contributes,"The VCF platform, leveraging NSX-T, provides the foundational network virtualization and security services necessary to establish visibility into encrypted communications traffic. This architecture supports the implementation of distributed firewall rules and network introspection, which are critical for identifying and managing specific traffic types and ensuring that the underlying network infrastructure can facilitate comprehensive monitoring. The platform is designed to integrate with third-party security services, including specialized appliances capable of performing SSL/TLS decryption and deep packet inspection, thereby making encrypted communications visible to designated monitoring mechanisms.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific encrypted communications traffic that requires visibility and for selecting, deploying, and configuring the appropriate system monitoring tools and mechanisms. SI-4(13),MON-01.13,"System Monitoring | Analyze Traffic and Event Patterns
(a) Analyze communications traffic and event patterns for the system
(b) Develop profiles representing common traffic and event patterns

(c) Use the traffic and event profiles in tuning system-monitoring devices.",Contributes,"The VMware Cloud Foundation platform provides comprehensive capabilities for analyzing communications traffic and event patterns across the entire SDDC stack. NSX-T Data Center delivers granular network flow visibility and distributed firewall logging, enabling detailed analysis of traffic patterns between workloads and network segments. Aria Operations for Logs centralizes event data from all VCF components, including vSphere, vSAN, NSX-T, and SDDC Manager, facilitating the identification and analysis of event patterns through advanced querying, dashboards, and alerting. Aria Operations establishes dynamic baselines for performance metrics and resource utilization, automatically profiling common operational patterns for compute, storage, and network resources.

These integrated monitoring and analytics tools allow for the development of robust profiles representing common traffic and event patterns. Aria Operations leverages these baselines and profiles to detect anomalies and deviations from normal behavior, while Aria Operations for Logs enables the creation of specific alerts and reports based on identified event patterns. This data and profiling capability is directly utilized in tuning the system-monitoring devices, as alert thresholds, dashboard configurations, and reporting mechanisms within Aria Operations and Aria Operations for Logs can be precisely adjusted to focus on significant deviations from established profiles, thereby optimizing monitoring effectiveness and reducing alert fatigue.

While the VCF platform provides the foundational tools and data for analysis, profiling, and the mechanisms for tuning monitoring devices, the customer is responsible for defining the specific criteria for common traffic and event patterns, configuring the precise alert thresholds, and establishing the operational processes for regularly reviewing and adjusting these profiles and monitoring configurations based on evolving system behavior and security requirements."
SI-4(14),MON-01.5,"System Monitoring | Wireless Intrusion Detection

Employ a wireless intrusion detection system to identify rogue wireless devices and to detect attack attempts and potential compromises or breaches to the system.",Contributes,"The VCF platform, through its NSX-T capabilities, provides advanced network intrusion detection and prevention services for both east-west and north-south traffic within the software-defined data center. This includes the ability to identify malicious traffic patterns, detect attack attempts targeting virtualized workloads and network segments, and monitor for potential compromises or breaches within the virtual network infrastructure.
The platform offers comprehensive visibility into network flows and security events across the virtualized environment, contributing significantly to the overall system's security posture.

The VCF platform does not natively incorporate a wireless intrusion detection system. It operates exclusively within the wired network infrastructure of the data center and does not extend to monitoring the physical wireless spectrum.

Consequently, the platform cannot identify rogue wireless devices, detect wireless-specific attack attempts (e.g., deauthentication attacks, evil twin access points), or monitor for compromises originating from the wireless medium itself. The implementation and management of a dedicated wireless intrusion detection system, along with the associated policies and procedures for identifying and responding to wireless threats, remain the responsibility of the customer."

SI-4(17),MON-02.3,"System Monitoring | Integrated Situational Awareness

SI-4(22),MON-11.2,"System Monitoring | Unauthorized Network Services
(a) Detect network services that have not been authorized or approved by [Assignment: organization-defined authorization or approval processes]
(b) [Selection (one or more): Audit

] when detected.",Contributes,"VCF, leveraging NSX-T, provides robust network visibility and security enforcement. The platform performs deep packet inspection and flow monitoring across the virtualized network fabric, enabling the detection of various network services operating within the environment. NSX-T logs network connections and service usage, and these logs are forwarded to external security information and event management (SIEM) systems for analysis and correlation. The platform's micro-segmentation capabilities allow for granular control over permitted service communications, inherently providing a mechanism to identify deviations from authorized traffic patterns.

The customer defines the specific authorization and approval processes for network services, establishes the baseline of authorized services, and configures the detection logic within NSX-T or integrated monitoring tools to identify services that fall outside this defined baseline. Furthermore, the customer defines the personnel or roles responsible for receiving alerts or conducting audits when unauthorized services are detected, and integrates the platform's monitoring data into their operational security procedures."
SI-4(23),MON-01.6,"System Monitoring | Host-based Devices

Implement the following host-based monitoring mechanisms at [Assignment: organization-defined system components]: [Assignment: organization-defined host-based monitoring mechanisms].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust host-based monitoring capabilities through its integrated management components. vRealize Operations Manager delivers advanced performance, health, and capacity monitoring for ESXi hosts, virtual machines, and underlying vSAN storage, collecting metrics and generating alerts based on predefined thresholds. vRealize Log Insight centralizes log collection from all ESXi hosts, vCenter Server, and NSX-T Data Center components, enabling real-time analysis, correlation, and alerting for security and operational events. The vCenter Server itself offers foundational monitoring and alerting for ESXi host status and resource utilization. These mechanisms ensure continuous visibility into the operational state and security posture of the host infrastructure.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific system components requiring host-based monitoring and establishing the precise host-based monitoring mechanisms to be implemented. This includes configuring vRealize Operations and Log Insight to align with organizational monitoring policies, defining specific alerts, thresholds, and log retention policies, and integrating these tools into broader security and operational monitoring frameworks. The customer must also ensure the ongoing operationalization and review of these defined monitoring mechanisms."
SI-4(24),"MON-01.7, MON-11.3","System Monitoring | Indicators of Compromise

Discover, collect, and distribute to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles], indicators of compromise provided by [Assignment: organization-defined sources].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides the foundational logging, monitoring, and security services necessary to discover and collect indicators of compromise (IOCs). Aria Operations for Logs aggregates system and application logs from all VCF components, including vSphere, NSX, and vSAN, which can contain evidence of suspicious activity. NSX Advanced Threat Prevention (ATP) and the Distributed Firewall can identify network-based IOCs, such as known malicious IP addresses, domains, or command-and-control traffic patterns. When integrated, VMware Carbon Black Cloud provides advanced endpoint detection and response capabilities, actively discovering and collecting IOCs at the workload level. These platforms are capable of generating alerts and forwarding collected data to external Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) systems for correlation and further analysis.

The customer is responsible for defining the specific organization-defined sources for indicators of compromise and configuring the integration with these sources, such as external threat intelligence feeds. Establishing the organization-defined personnel or roles to whom these indicators are distributed, along with the associated distribution mechanisms and response procedures, remains a customer responsibility. This includes configuring specific alerting thresholds, notification methods, and the overall incident response workflow to ensure timely and effective action on discovered IOCs."
SI-4(25),"MON-01.1, NET-03.1","System Monitoring | Optimize Network Traffic Analysis

Provide visibility into network traffic at external and key internal system interfaces to optimize the effectiveness of monitoring devices.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation, leveraging NSX-T, provides robust mechanisms for gaining visibility into network traffic. The platform facilitates the capture and export of traffic data at both external boundaries, managed by NSX Edge nodes, and key internal system interfaces, encompassing traffic between logical segments and virtual machines. This is achieved through capabilities such as distributed firewall logging, IPFIX/NetFlow export, and port mirroring (SPAN/ERSPAN), which allow for the replication of traffic to dedicated collector virtual machines or external monitoring solutions. Furthermore, NSX Intelligence offers real-time visualization and analytics of network flows, enhancing the understanding of traffic patterns and dependencies within the virtualized environment. These integrated features directly support the objective of providing comprehensive network traffic visibility to external and internal monitoring devices.

While the platform delivers the foundational capabilities for traffic visibility, the customer is responsible for configuring the specific interfaces and traffic types to be monitored, deploying and integrating external monitoring devices (such as SIEMs, IDS/IPS, or packet analyzers) to consume the exported data, and defining the operational policies and procedures for analyzing this information to optimize the effectiveness of their monitoring strategy. The customer must also establish the processes for alert generation, review, and response based on the insights derived from the traffic analysis."

Implement security directives in accordance with established time frames, or notify the issuing organization of the degree of noncompliance.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) integrates robust monitoring, logging, and threat detection capabilities across its compute, network, and storage layers. Through components such as Aria Operations for Logs, Aria Operations for Networks, and NSX Distributed IDS/IPS, the platform continuously collects security-relevant events and network flow data. This enables the automated generation of internal security alerts and notifications based on predefined thresholds, detected anomalies, and identified threats. The platform supports the ingestion of external threat intelligence feeds to enrich its detection capabilities, allowing for the technical reception of advisories. These generated alerts can be configured for automated dissemination to integrated security information and event management (SIEM) systems or other designated endpoints for further analysis and action.

The customer is responsible for defining the specific external organizations from which security alerts, advisories, and directives are received, as well as establishing the processes for their ongoing intake and analysis.

The determination of when internal security alerts, advisories, and directives are ""deemed necessary"" and their specific content, along with the definition of personnel, roles, or organizational elements for dissemination, falls to the customer.

Furthermore, the implementation of security directives in accordance with established time frames and the notification of issuing organizations regarding noncompliance are entirely customer-defined policy and operational responsibilities."
SI-5(1),THR-03,"Security Alerts, Advisories, and Directives | Automated Alerts and Advisories

Broadcast security alert and advisory information throughout the organization using [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation components inherently generate and manage a comprehensive array of security-relevant events, alerts, and advisories. vCenter Server provides system-level alarms, while NSX-T/NSX+ delivers network security events, IDS/IPS alerts, and firewall logs. Aria Operations (formerly vRealize Operations) aggregates these metrics, logs, and events across the entire SDDC, identifying security anomalies, compliance deviations, and operational alerts. Aria Operations for Logs (formerly vRealize Log Insight) further collects and analyzes all component logs, enabling custom alert generation based on specific security event patterns.

These platforms are equipped with robust alerting frameworks capable of automated distribution via email, SNMP, webhooks, syslog, and direct integration with Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) systems, thereby providing the necessary automated mechanisms for broadcasting security information.

While VMware Cloud Foundation provides the foundational technical mechanisms for automated alert generation and distribution, the organization is responsible for defining the specific automated mechanisms to be utilized and configuring them to meet the ""throughout the organization"" requirement. This includes establishing the policies for alert content, defining the target audience for various alert types, and integrating VCF's native alerting capabilities with broader enterprise security alert and advisory distribution systems, such as a centralized SIEM, SOAR platform, or incident response communication channels. The customer must also define the processes for managing and responding to these broadcasted alerts and advisories."

] when anomalies are discovered.",Contributes,"VCF provides comprehensive capabilities for verifying the correct operation of its integrated security and privacy functions. This includes continuous monitoring of network segmentation policies enforced by NSX, access controls managed by vCenter, and data protection features such as vSAN encryption. The platform automatically performs verification checks during system transitional states, such as component restarts or configuration changes, and maintains continuous operational health monitoring. Authorized administrators can also initiate on-demand verification of specific security configurations and services. SI-6(3),CHG-06.1,"Security and Privacy Function Verification | Report Verification Results

Report the results of security and privacy function verification to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust logging and auditing capabilities across its management and workload domains, capturing events related to security configurations, access controls, and operational status. These capabilities generate the essential data and evidence required to perform security and privacy function verification, including detailed records of system changes, security alerts, and compliance posture assessments. The platform's integration with external monitoring and SIEM solutions facilitates the collection and aggregation of this operational data, serving as a critical input for any verification process.

The customer is responsible for defining the specific security and privacy functions that require verification, establishing the methodologies for such verification, and identifying the organization-defined personnel or roles designated to receive the verification results. SI-7,"END-06, NET-12, TDA-18","Software, Firmware, and Information Integrity
a. Employ integrity verification tools to detect unauthorized changes to the following software, firmware, and information: [Assignment: organization-defined software, firmware, and information]

b. Take the following actions when unauthorized changes to the software, firmware, and information are detected: [Assignment: organization-defined actions].",Contributes,"The VCF platform inherently maintains the integrity of its foundational software and firmware components. It employs robust mechanisms to detect unauthorized changes across the virtualized infrastructure, including ESXi hosts, vCenter Server, NSX, and vSAN. Firmware updates for ESXi hosts and network devices are managed through trusted channels, ensuring authenticity and integrity. The platform leverages desired state configuration management and cryptographic verification for its core software components, preventing tampering and ensuring that only authorized and signed code is executed. This extends to the integrity of the underlying storage infrastructure provided by vSAN, which contributes to the overall integrity of information residing within the platform.

While the platform provides comprehensive integrity verification for its own components, the customer is responsible for defining the specific scope of all software, firmware, and information requiring integrity monitoring beyond the core VCF infrastructure. This includes applications, guest operating systems within virtual machines, and specific data sets. Furthermore, the customer must establish and implement the organization-defined actions to be taken when unauthorized changes are detected, including incident response procedures, remediation playbooks, and assigning human accountability for these processes."
SI-7(1),END-06.1,"Software, Firmware, and Information Integrity | Integrity Checks
Perform an integrity check of [Assignment: organization-defined software, firmware, and information]
[Selection (one or more): at startup
column2: at [Assignment: organization-defined transitional states or security-relevant events]

column1: [Assignment: organization-defined frequency]

].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) establishes a robust foundation for integrity checks by leveraging secure boot for ESXi hosts and virtual machines, ensuring the integrity of boot components and the trusted boot chain. The SDDC Manager continuously performs health and compliance checks across the VCF stack, verifying the integrity of deployed software and configurations. Lifecycle management operations within VCF inherently validate the integrity of software and firmware components during patching and upgrades, and vSphere Host Profiles can enforce and monitor the configuration integrity of ESXi hosts.

The customer is responsible for defining the specific ""organization-defined software, firmware, and information"" that requires integrity checks beyond the core VCF components. This includes establishing the ""organization-defined transitional states or security-relevant events"" and the ""organization-defined frequency"" for these checks, particularly for guest operating systems, applications, and data residing within the virtualized environment. Additional tools or processes must be implemented by the customer to perform integrity checks on their specific applications and data."
SI-7(2),END-06.3,"Software, Firmware, and Information Integrity | Automated Notifications of Integrity Violations

Employ automated tools that provide notification to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles] upon discovering discrepancies during integrity verification.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust, automated tools for verifying the integrity of its core software, firmware, and configuration across the compute, storage, and networking layers. vSphere ensures the integrity of ESXi hosts through secure boot, TPM integration, and host profile compliance. vSAN continuously verifies data integrity across the distributed storage fabric, and NSX-T monitors the integrity of the network virtualization components. The platform leverages vRealize Operations Manager (vROps) to provide comprehensive, automated monitoring capabilities, detecting configuration drift, health anomalies, and security-related discrepancies across the entire VCF stack.

Upon the discovery of any integrity violation or discrepancy within the managed infrastructure, vROps is configured to generate alerts and can automatically trigger notifications. These notifications can be delivered through various channels, including email, SNMP traps, webhooks, or syslog, ensuring that relevant stakeholders are promptly informed of potential integrity issues.

The customer is responsible for defining the specific ""organization-defined personnel or roles"" who will receive these automated notifications. This includes configuring the appropriate notification targets and channels within vROps and establishing the operational procedures and response protocols for addressing detected integrity discrepancies. Furthermore, the scope of ""Software, Firmware, and Information Integrity"" extends beyond the VCF infrastructure layer, requiring the customer to implement additional integrity verification tools and processes for guest operating systems, applications, and other information assets not directly managed by the VCF platform."
SI-7(5),END-06.4,"Software, Firmware, and Information Integrity | Automated Response to Integrity Violations
Automatically [Selection (one or more): shut the system down

] when integrity violations are discovered.",Contributes,"The VCF platform does not inherently provide automated mechanisms to shut down, restart, or implement organization-defined controls in response to discovered integrity violations. While VCF components contribute to overall system integrity, the specific automated remediation actions described are not native, out-of-the-box capabilities.

Customers are responsible for implementing and configuring external automation, scripting, or third-party solutions to detect integrity violations within the VCF environment and trigger the required automated responses. This includes defining the specific integrity violations to monitor and the corresponding automated actions to be taken."
SI-7(6),CRY-01,"Software, Firmware, and Information Integrity | Cryptographic Protection

Implement cryptographic mechanisms to detect unauthorized changes to software, firmware, and information.",Contributes,"VCF implements robust cryptographic mechanisms to ensure the integrity of its core software and firmware components.

All VCF software bundles, including ESXi, vCenter Server, NSX, vSAN, and SDDC Manager, are cryptographically signed and verified during installation and update processes, preventing the deployment of unauthorized or tampered code. ESXi hosts leverage Secure Boot and Trusted Platform Modules (TPM) to cryptographically verify the integrity of the boot chain and firmware, detecting any unauthorized modifications at the platform level.
These integrated capabilities provide foundational cryptographic protection for the VCF infrastructure itself.

Customers are responsible for implementing and managing cryptographic integrity mechanisms for their specific application software, firmware, and data residing within guest virtual machines or containers deployed on the VCF platform. This includes selecting and configuring appropriate tools and processes within their workloads to detect unauthorized changes to their application-specific information."
SI-7(7),END-06.2,"Software, Firmware, and Information Integrity | Integration of Detection and Response

Incorporate the detection of the following unauthorized changes into the organizational incident response capability: [Assignment: organization-defined security-relevant changes to the system].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation, through its integrated security services and underlying platform capabilities, provides robust mechanisms for detecting unauthorized changes to software, firmware, and information integrity. VMware Carbon Black Cloud, when deployed, continuously monitors endpoint activity, including file system modifications, process execution, and network connections, to identify anomalous or malicious behavior indicative of unauthorized changes.
The platform itself leverages secure boot, Trusted Platform Modules (TPM), and host integrity checks to maintain the integrity of its foundational components and firmware.

All security-relevant events and alerts generated by VCF components and integrated security tools are systematically forwarded to a centralized Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) system, establishing the technical foundation for incident detection and response.
The organization remains responsible for defining what constitutes ""security-relevant changes"" specific to their environment and configuring the integrated detection tools accordingly.

Furthermore, the organization must establish and maintain its formal incident response capability, including defining processes, procedures, and assigning human accountability for responding to the detected unauthorized changes."
SI-7(9),END-06.5,"Software, Firmware, and Information Integrity | Verify Boot Process

Verify the integrity of the boot process of the following system components: [Assignment: organization-defined system components].",Contributes,"The VCF platform provides robust capabilities to verify the integrity of the boot process for its underlying ESXi hypervisor hosts. ESXi hosts support UEFI Secure Boot, which cryptographically verifies the integrity of the bootloader and kernel modules against trusted certificates.

Furthermore, VCF environments can leverage Trusted Platform Modules (TPM) 2.0, enabling hardware-rooted trust and attestation. This allows for the measurement of boot components and the secure storage of these measurements, which can then be remotely attested by vCenter Server to confirm the integrity of the host's boot sequence.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific system components within the VCF stack that require boot integrity verification, beyond the inherent capabilities for ESXi hosts. This includes ensuring the underlying hardware supports Secure Boot and TPM, enabling these features, configuring attestation services, and establishing policies and procedures for monitoring boot integrity status and responding to any detected integrity failures."
SI-7(10),END-06.6,"Software, Firmware, and Information Integrity | Protection of Boot Firmware

Implement the following mechanisms to protect the integrity of boot firmware in [Assignment: organization-defined system components]: [Assignment: organization-defined mechanisms].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) inherently supports and enables robust boot firmware integrity mechanisms across its underlying ESXi hosts. The platform leverages UEFI Secure Boot to ensure that only cryptographically signed firmware and bootloaders are executed, establishing a secure boot chain from the hardware root of trust. VCF also integrates with Trusted Platform Modules (TPMs) for hardware-based root of trust and host attestation, allowing for continuous verification of the ESXi host's boot integrity against a known good state. These capabilities are fundamental to the secure operation and integrity of the VCF infrastructure.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific system components within their VCF deployment that require boot firmware protection, as well as the precise mechanisms to be implemented. This includes configuring Secure Boot settings, enabling and managing TPMs, and establishing organizational policies and procedures for monitoring attestation status and responding to any integrity violations."
SI-7(15),CHG-04.2,"Software, Firmware, and Information Integrity | Code Authentication

Implement cryptographic mechanisms to authenticate the following software or firmware components prior to installation: [Assignment: organization-defined software or firmware components].",Contributes,"The VCF platform inherently implements robust cryptographic mechanisms to authenticate its core software and firmware components prior to installation and during lifecycle management.

This includes the use of digitally signed VMware software bundles, VIBs, and firmware updates, all managed through SDDC Manager to ensure their integrity and authenticity. Secure Boot is enabled for ESXi hosts, which cryptographically verifies the boot components before the hypervisor loads, establishing a secure chain of trust from the hardware up through the hypervisor. These capabilities ensure that the foundational SDDC components are validated against known good cryptographic signatures.

While VCF secures its own stack, the organization retains responsibility for defining and implementing cryptographic authentication mechanisms for any additional, organization-defined software or firmware components deployed within the VCF environment. This includes establishing policies and procedures for validating the authenticity of guest operating system applications, custom tools, or third-party solutions not directly managed by the VCF lifecycle prior to their installation."
SI-8,END-08,"Spam Protection
a. Employ spam protection mechanisms at system entry and exit points to detect and act on unsolicited messages

b. Update spam protection mechanisms when new releases are available in accordance with organizational configuration management policy and procedures.",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides a robust, secure, and continuously updated software-defined data center platform that serves as the foundational infrastructure for deploying enterprise-grade spam protection solutions. The platform's integrated lifecycle management ensures that all underlying components, including ESXi hosts, vCenter Server, NSX-T Data Center, and vSAN, are maintained with the latest security patches and software releases.

NSX-T Data Center offers advanced network segmentation and micro-segmentation capabilities, enabling the isolation and secure deployment of dedicated spam protection appliances or virtual machines at network entry and exit points. This network-level control allows for the enforcement of traffic flow policies to direct messages through designated security mechanisms.

While VCF provides the secure and managed infrastructure, the direct implementation and management of spam protection mechanisms, including the selection, deployment, configuration, and ongoing operational updates of specific anti-spam software or appliances, remain the responsibility of the customer. Organizations must define and implement their own configuration management policies and procedures for these application-level security tools, ensuring they are updated in accordance with vendor releases and organizational requirements to detect and act on unsolicited messages effectively."

SI-10,"NET-12, TDA-18","Information Input Validation

Check the validity of the following information inputs: [Assignment: organization-defined information inputs to the system].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation components inherently validate various inputs to maintain operational integrity and security.
NSX-T provides robust network input validation through distributed firewalls, edge firewalls, and security policies, ensuring only authorized and well-formed network traffic is processed.

The management plane, including vCenter Server and SDDC Manager, performs rigorous validation of API calls, configuration parameters, and user commands to prevent unauthorized or malformed inputs from impacting the SDDC. This includes schema validation for API requests and parameter checks for system configurations.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific information inputs that require validation within their applications and workloads running on the VCF infrastructure. This includes establishing policies for application-level input validation and configuring security controls to align with those definitions."
SI-11,TDA-19,"Error Handling
a. Generate error messages that provide information necessary for corrective actions without revealing information that could be exploited

b. Reveal error messages only to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation components generate comprehensive error messages and logs, which are aggregated and managed by Aria Operations for Logs. This centralized logging solution provides robust role-based access control (RBAC), allowing administrators to define and enforce who can view specific log data, including error messages. The platform's native RBAC capabilities extend to all management interfaces, ensuring that access to diagnostic information is restricted to authorized personnel.

While the platform provides the necessary technical controls for secure error handling and restricted access, the customer is responsible for defining the specific ""organization-defined personnel or roles"" that are authorized to view error messages. Furthermore, the customer must ensure that applications and workloads deployed within the VCF environment are configured to generate error messages that provide sufficient information for corrective actions without revealing exploitable details, as the platform itself does not dictate the content of application-level error messages."
SI-12,"DCH-18, PRI-05","Information Management and Retention

Manage and retain information within the system and information output from the system in accordance with applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, guidelines and operational requirements.",Contributes,"VCF provides the foundational infrastructure for managing and retaining information. The platform's integrated storage solutions, powered by vSAN, ensure the secure and resilient storage of data within the system. Operational logs generated by VCF components, such as vCenter, NSX, and vSAN, are retained for configurable periods and can be seamlessly integrated with external log management and SIEM solutions for long-term archival, supporting the retention of system-generated information and output. This robust infrastructure provides the necessary technical underpinnings for data management and retention strategies.

Defining and enforcing specific information management and retention policies, including the interpretation of applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, guidelines, and operational requirements, remains the customer's responsibility.

The customer must establish and implement these policies for all information types, including application data and user-generated content, and ensure that applications running on VCF are configured to adhere to these defined retention schedules. VCF provides the secure and available platform, but the policy layer and its application-level enforcement are managed by the customer."
SI-12(1),"DCH-18.1, PRI-05.1","Information Management and Retention | Limit Personally Identifiable Information Elements

Limit personally identifiable information being processed in the information life cycle to the following elements of PII: [Assignment: organization-defined elements of personally identifiable information].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides a robust and secure infrastructure platform, establishing a trusted execution environment for workloads that may process personally identifiable information. The platform ensures data confidentiality and integrity through advanced networking, storage, and compute virtualization capabilities, including encryption at rest and in transit, and granular access controls for infrastructure components. This foundational security is critical for protecting any PII processed within the environment.

The definition of specific personally identifiable information elements and the enforcement of limitations on their processing within the information lifecycle remain the direct responsibility of the customer. This includes establishing organizational policies, implementing data classification schemes, and configuring application-level controls to ensure only organization-defined PII elements are processed. VMware Cloud Foundation does not inherently classify or manage the content of data processed by guest workloads or applications."
SI-12(2),"DCH-18.2, PRI-05.1","Information Management and Retention | Minimize Personally Identifiable Information in Testing, Training, and Research

Use the following techniques to minimize the use of personally identifiable information for research, testing, or training: [Assignment: organization-defined techniques].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides a robust and secure infrastructure platform that supports the isolation and protection of data within testing, training, and research environments. The platform's capabilities, including logical network segmentation via NSX, secure storage with vSAN encryption, and granular role-based access controls, establish a foundational layer of security. This infrastructure ensures that environments can be provisioned with appropriate access restrictions and data protection mechanisms, thereby supporting an organization's broader strategy to minimize PII exposure.

The organization is responsible for defining and implementing the specific techniques to minimize personally identifiable information within applications and data sets used for research, testing, or training. This includes the selection and deployment of data masking, anonymization, synthetic data generation, or data redaction tools and processes at the application or database layer. Furthermore, the organization must establish and enforce policies and procedures governing the handling of PII in non-production environments, ensuring that these techniques are consistently applied and audited."
SI-12(3),"DCH-21, PRI-05","Information Management and Retention | Information Disposal

Use the following techniques to dispose of, destroy, or erase information following the retention period: [Assignment: organization-defined techniques].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides the foundational infrastructure for secure data handling, including capabilities that support the secure disposal of information. The platform leverages underlying storage technologies that offer secure erasure methods for physical and virtual disks, ensuring data is unrecoverable when storage resources are deprovisioned or repurposed. Virtual machine disks can be securely deleted, and storage arrays integrated with VCF often provide data shredding or cryptographic erasure features. These capabilities ensure that the underlying storage infrastructure can support an organization's defined data disposal techniques.

The organization is responsible for defining the specific techniques for disposing, destroying, or erasing information following its retention period. This includes establishing policies and procedures for data lifecycle management, implementing these techniques at the application and data layers, and ensuring that the chosen methods align with regulatory requirements and organizational retention policies. While VMware Cloud Foundation provides the secure infrastructure, the customer must integrate these platform capabilities into their broader information disposal strategy and operationalize the defined techniques."
SI-13,"BCD-12.2, SEA-07","Predictable Failure Prevention
a. Determine mean time to failure (MTTF) for the following system components in specific environments of operation: [Assignment: organization-defined system components]

b. Provide substitute system components and a means to exchange active and standby components in accordance with the following criteria: [Assignment: organization-defined MTTF substitution criteria].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust capabilities for predictable failure prevention through its inherent architecture and integrated components.

vSphere High Availability (HA) automatically restarts virtual machines on healthy hosts in the event of a host failure, effectively providing substitute compute resources and a seamless exchange mechanism. vSAN ensures storage redundancy and resilience by distributing data across multiple physical disks and hosts, maintaining data availability even if underlying storage components fail. NSX-T Data Center delivers network redundancy and high availability for network services, preventing single points of failure in the network fabric.

The underlying physical infrastructure supporting VCF deployments is designed with redundancy at multiple layers, including power, networking, and compute, to minimize the impact of component failures.

While VCF provides the technical mechanisms for component substitution and exchange, the organization is responsible for defining the specific system components for which mean time to failure (MTTF) is determined, as well as establishing the organization-defined MTTF substitution criteria. The customer must also implement the processes and policies for formally determining MTTF, provisioning substitute components, and managing the exchange of active and standby components in accordance with their defined criteria."
SI-14,SEA-08,"Non-persistence
Implement non-persistent [Assignment: organization-defined system components and services] that are initiated in a known state and terminated [Selection (one or more): upon end of session of use

].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation delivers the foundational infrastructure to implement non-persistent system components and services. The platform's virtualization capabilities, powered by vSphere, enable the deployment of ephemeral virtual machines, such as instant clones for virtual desktop infrastructure, which are inherently designed for non-persistence. Similarly, the integrated Tanzu Kubernetes Grid facilitates the orchestration of containerized applications, where pods are routinely initiated from defined images and terminated upon completion or failure, ensuring a known operational state. These mechanisms inherently support the initiation of components from a secure, known configuration.

The organization retains responsibility for defining the specific system components and services that will operate in a non-persistent fashion. This includes the creation and ongoing maintenance of the secure golden images, templates, or container images that establish the ""known state"" for these deployments. Furthermore, the customer must configure and implement the specific termination policies, whether tied to the end of a user session, a defined periodic refresh frequency, or through custom orchestration logic that leverages VCF's automation capabilities."
SI-14(1),SEA-08.1,"Non-persistence | Refresh from Trusted Sources

Obtain software and data employed during system component and service refreshes from the following trusted sources: [Assignment: organization-defined trusted sources].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust mechanisms for managing and deploying software and data employed during system component and service refreshes, particularly in non-persistent environments.

vSphere Content Libraries offer a secure, centralized repository for VM templates, ISOs, and scripts, allowing for controlled distribution and versioning. Access to these libraries is governed by role-based access control, ensuring that only authorized personnel can manage or retrieve assets.

For containerized workloads managed by Tanzu Kubernetes Grid, VCF integrates with private container registries, enabling organizations to enforce the use of approved, trusted image sources for application deployments and refreshes.
The platform's inherent security features, including integrity checks for its own lifecycle management, further support the secure sourcing of components.

The organization is responsible for defining what constitutes a ""trusted source"" for their specific software and data. This includes establishing policies and procedures for vetting, approving, and maintaining these sources, as well as configuring the VCF environment (e.g., Content Library subscriptions, container registry policies) to enforce the exclusive use of these defined trusted sources during all system component and service refreshes."
SI-15,SEA-09,"Information Output Filtering

Validate information output from the following software programs and/or applications to ensure that the information is consistent with the expected content: [Assignment: organization-defined software programs and/or applications].",Contributes,"VMware Cloud Foundation provides a secure and controlled environment for application deployment, leveraging NSX-T for robust network segmentation and egress filtering.
